Course: Blackstone Course, Mission Hills Resort Haikou
Blackstone Course, Mission Hills Resort Haikou

Pre Tournament Notes:
- Trends:
    - 14 in the last 17 winners had a AM/PM tee split, Rory has PM/AM last year.
    - The last 14 Sawgrass champions finished in the top 35 in their preceding tournament.
    - The last 6 winners ranked top10 in the world rankings: 2025: Mcilroy 2; 2024: Scheffler 1; 2023: Scheffler 2; 2022: Smith 10; 2021: Thomas 3; 2019: McIlroy 6.
    - 4 in the last 6 winners ranked 1st T2G
    - 5/5 ranked top 10 APP
    - 4/5 ranked outside top 35 putting, the other was Cam Smith 2022 ranking 1st
    - Experience needed, only 1 past champion in 30/40 years has won as a rookie.
    - A focus on T2G, OTT & APP
- It does seem to have a big emphasis on driving distance.
- Small chance of rain on Thursday, wind seems to be up on Thursday gusting upto 20mph. Otherwise fairly calm for the week.
- [x] Collin Morikawa catches my eye and seems to fit into the trends above. For last three months he ranks 3rd T2G, 5th APP which bodes well here. Decent 5th place at Bay Hill last week, 7th APP, 9th OTT and interestingly 13th Putt which is good for him. T10 here last year with T13 2023.
- [x] Min Woo Lee arguably playing the best golf of his career to date. T2 at Pebble, T12 at Riviera (includes a poor 3rd round), T6 at Bay Hill last week where I didn’t think it would suit his game. For last three months he ranks 6th OTT, 26th PUTT. T6 here in 2023, T20 last year.
- [x] Adam Scott is having a fantastic year, regularly featuring in some big leaderboards. 4th at Riv which included two 63s, T11 at Bay Hill this last week, 12th APP, 11th Putt. Been here plenty of times 
- [ ] Viktor
- [x] Matti Schmid
- [ ] Joel Dahmen
Post Tournament Notes:
Cameron Young bold finish to see off Matt Fitz was not what how I expected this tournament to end. From Thursday to mid Sunday it was the Ludvig show, until he dumped two balls in the water on 11 & 12 out of nowhere. This was where a charging Fitz and Young stormed into the lead, both making fantastic putts and hitting bold shots, Young with a great birdie on 17 setup a tie for the lead going down 18 and then an incredible 375 yard drive down the 18 set up a simple par finish which beat Fitzy’s bogey. A great tournament with a finish to match. Course was on point this year, playing firm and fast with some naughty run offs, rough very pineal. Young seemed to fit all the trends again, apart from 1 which is the top10 OWGR ranking, however if you look at the data golf rankings he does fit the trend for top10 winners… a note to take for next year.
Eye Catchers - Xander looking back to his best warming up nicely for the Masters. Rookie Yellamaraju looks like a great prospect fresh off the Korn Ferry tour.

Course Details:
Course Arnold Palmer's Bay Hill Club and Lodge, Orlando, Florida
Length 7,466 yards
Par 72
Course make up Four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Grass type Fairways: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye; Rough: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye 3″; Greens: 7,500 sq.ft average TifEagle Bermudagrass; Tournament Stimp: 12ft.
Course overview Bay Hill has hosted a PGA Tour event every year since 1979. Arnold Palmer made significant changes to the track before the 2010 edition, so form before then is of little relevance. Water is in play on several holes and there are lots of doglegs. The Arnold Palmer has been a Signature Event since 2023.
What will it take to win the Arnold Palmer Invitational? There is plenty of water to trouble the competitors on this tough layout, as well as greenside run-off areas, putting a premium on accuracy and the ability to grind pars.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Looking for strong ball strikers, last year this was firm and fast and you really wanted to be accurate and excel in GIR.
- Bay Hill is relitively long but main difficulty is the firmness of the course and getting the ball to stop.
- [x] Kurt Kityama form can be patchy but each year seems to have a purple patch or two where he picks up top 10s and a win. He won this back in 2023 on debut, with a 2nd at Riv in his last start on tough greens, if he can putt like that this week he could get himself right in the mix.
- [x] Sepp Straka has had a quiet start to this season, most likely trying to find some form after dealing with some difficult personal matters off the course last year. He’s always been a top quality ball striker and straight off the tee, T5 last year and his game should suit round here. At a big price for him he could go well this week.
- [x] Rory Mcilroy holds the best scoring record round here for the last ten year by some margin. Arguably should have won at Riv on his last start but the putter let him down over the weekend, which as of late is unusual as he’s become a top putter. I think the change back to bermudagrass will help him this week, as long as the T2G is still strong he could have a massive chance this week to pick up a second Arnold Palmer invitational.
Post Tournament Notes:
Bay Hill played firm and fast as expected, Daniel Berger was seemingly on his way to a wire-to-wire win until he was pipped on the last hole by Akshay Bhatia to force a playoff down 18 which Akshay won. Akshay was holing putts from all over the place especially on the back 9. An incredible eagle on the 16th put him right in contention and played the 18th impressively whereas Berger did not bogeying the hole in regulation and the playoff. Rory WD prior to the 3rd round with a tweak in his back, Sepp was right in the mix on Sunday but really disappointed on the back 9 with a string of bogies. Kurt midway back.
Eye Catchers - Min Woo Lee continues to impress on courses you wouldn’t think he we would excel on. Morikawa looks back to his best.

Course Details:
Course Grand Reserve Golf Club, Rio Grande, Puerto Rico
Length 7,506 yards
Par 72
Course make up Four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Grass type Fairways: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye; Rough: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye 3″; Greens: 7,500 sq.ft average TifEagle Bermudagrass; Tournament Stimp: 12ft.
Course overview This Tom Kite layout has hosted the Puerto Rico Open since 2008, with various name changes through the years (Trump International, Coco Beach GC). It is flat and has paspalum grass.
What will it take to win the Puerto Rico Open? With Ryan Brehm and Brice Garnett, one of the longest drivers in the world and one of the shortest, on the honours board in two of the last four years, there is clearly more than one way to succeed at Grand Reserve. Accuracy will count for more this week with a wind blowing throughout.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Looking for good ball strikers. Likely to be windy in Puerto Rico gusting upto 20mph on each day. 
- [x] Ricky Castillo looked impressive last week at PGA National finishing 5th despite a fairly slow start. T64 here last year so he knows the place and in this weak field could come out on top for a maiden PGA tour win.
Post Tournament Notes: I didn’t watch any of this as it was not broadcasted in the UK, however very happy my only pick Ricky Castillo prevailed on Sunday. The 25 yo is an upcoming talent, narrowly missing out on his tour card last year he is back more determined than ever. This win will provide exemption for the next two years and the shackles are off for him to really make his mark on the PGA tour. Former world no1 amateur lets not forget, he has the game and talent to go really far.

Course Details:
Course Houghton Golf Club, Johannesburg, South Africa
Length 7,241 yards
Par 70
Course make-up Two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Grass type Greens are undulating Bentgrass
Course Overview AM Copeland designed this classic track, which was opened in 1926, and it staged the South African Open eight times between 1951 and 1992. The South African PGA was played there from 1993 through 1999. And from 2000 through 2004, Houghton was the venue for the Alfred Dunhill Championship on the European Tour. The course was then sold, though, and completely changed by Jack Nicklaus and his team, opening in 2009. The only significant course form since the redesign is the Sunshine Tour Qualifying School in 2019 and 2020, and the Joburg Opens in 2022, 2023 and last year. This is a short track, given it is played at altitude in the Highveld. Heavy bunkering, water hazards and undulating greens are the defence. The par was reduced from 71 to 70 in 2024, with the third hole becoming a par-four, rather than a five.
What will it take to win the Joburg Open As with most Nicklaus designs, accurate iron-play is essential to greens heavily guarded by bunkers.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather in Joburg looks mixed, risk of thundery showers Thurs-Sat and then windy on Sunday gusting upto 25mph.
- Relatively short course with the altitude factored in. Nicklaus redesigned with plenty of bunkers guarding greens. Looking for strong ball strikers and T2G, want to be playing second shots from FW.
- [x] Daniel Van Tonder has a great record here, T3, CUT, 5 on his last three goes. As like many SA players, comes alive in his home country. T6 last week at Stellenbosch indicates his game is back to its best after a few poor week prior. Should go well this week.
- [x] Jacob Scov Olesen is an old favourite of mine, picking up plenty of place money for me since noticing him as an amateur. Normally I like him on links and/or where it’s going to be windy and wet. According to the forecast we will see the latter. T7 here on debut last year where I was on him and placed, he does seem to like this African stretch and willing to take another punt on him this year.
Post Tournament Notes:
A typical SA feeling track this week, Houghton GC played at altitude with many SA players rising to the top of the leaderboard. However it was Englishmen Dan Bradbury who came out on top, fending off Casey Jarvis (for his third win in a row), Hennie Du Palsies & Branden RT. Bradbury has a sneakily good record in SA and when the putter is hot, can battle against anyone on the DPWT. Daniel Van Tonder returned a shared place, Jacob Scott Olesen disappointed in the last round with a 74 when not far off contention.
Eye Catchers -

Course Details:
Course Arnold Palmer's Bay Hill Club and Lodge, Orlando, Florida
Length 7,466 yards
Par 72
Course make up Four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Grass type Fairways: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye; Rough: Celebration Bermuda overseeded with Perennial Rye 3″; Greens: 7,500 sq.ft average TifEagle Bermudagrass; Tournament Stimp: 12ft.
Course overview Bay Hill has hosted a PGA Tour event every year since 1979. Arnold Palmer made significant changes to the track before the 2010 edition, so form before then is of little relevance. Water is in play on several holes and there are lots of doglegs. The Arnold Palmer has been a Signature Event since 2023.
What will it take to win the Arnold Palmer Invitational? There is plenty of water to trouble the competitors on this tough layout, as well as greenside run-off areas, putting a premium on accuracy and the ability to grind pars.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Looking for strong ball strikers, last year this was firm and fast and you really wanted to be accurate and excel in GIR.
- Bay Hill is relitively long but main difficulty is the firmness of the course and getting the ball to stop.
- [x] Kurt Kityama form can be patchy but each year seems to have a purple patch or two where he picks up top 10s and a win. He won this back in 2023 on debut, with a 2nd at Riv in his last start on tough greens, if he can putt like that this week he could get himself right in the mix.
- [x] Sepp Straka has had a quiet start to this season, most likely trying to find some form after dealing with some difficult personal matters off the course last year. He’s always been a top quality ball striker and straight off the tee, T5 last year and his game should suit round here. At a big price for him he could go well this week.
- [x] Rory Mcilroy holds the best scoring record round here for the last ten year by some margin. Arguably should have won at Riv on his last start but the putter let him down over the weekend, which as of late is unusual as he’s become a top putter. I think the change back to bermudagrass will help him this week, as long as the T2G is still strong he could have a massive chance this week to pick up a second Arnold Palmer invitational.
Post Tournament Notes:
Bay Hill played firm and fast as expected, Daniel Berger was seemingly on his way to a wire-to-wire win until he was pipped on the last hole by Akshay Bhatia to force a playoff down 18 which Akshay won. Akshay was holing putts from all over the place especially on the back 9. An incredible eagle on the 16th put him right in contention and played the 18th impressively whereas Berger did not bogeying the hole in regulation and the playoff. Rory WD prior to the 3rd round with a tweak in his back, Sepp was right in the mix on Sunday but really disappointed on the back 9 with a string of bogies. Kurt midway back.
Eye Catchers - Min Woo Lee continues to impress on courses you wouldn’t think he we would excel on. Morikawa looks back to his best.

Course: The Champion Course, PGA National Resort
Course Details:
Course The Champion Course, PGA National Resort, Palm Beach Gardens, Florida
Length 7,223 yards
Par	71 Three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Champions, a course created by George Fazio and redesigned by Jack Nicklaus, used to be one of the toughest layouts on the PGA Tour.  The Honda Classic, renamed the Cognizant Classic in 2024, has been at this venue since 2007, with 72-hole totals of just six under par or worse enough to top the leaderboard four times.  Over recent years, though, the PGA Tour has made the layout easier, changing the tenth hole from a par four to a par five in 2024, overseeding the track with Ryegrass to soften the turf, shortening the rough and widening the fairways. 
Thicker rough has been a feature of PGA Tour venues this season, so anything double-figures under par may have a winning chance this time.
What will it take to win the Cognizant Classic? Three breezy days should make this more of a grind than has been the case recently, so accuracy from tee to green should prove the key to success.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Dry but windy throughout most of the week will make it a tougher course than it has been in recent years.
- Relatively long but overseeded rye makes it gettable even out of the rough. Water everywhere here, features on 16 holes.
- [x] Michael Thorbjornson has to win soon surely? T39 on debut last year, master T2G and if he can have a good week with the putter in a field like this he will have a great chance of shaking off the maiden tag. 
- [x] Nico Echavarria has had a slow start this season since getting married towards the end of last year. T8 at Pebble a few weeks back shows his game is creeping back to form, missed cut at the Genesis I can forgive given the difficult greens. He is a Florida resident and I’m sure he will appreciate being back on bermudagrass. T21 best finish in 2024, this two time PGA winner is undervalues in this market at 60-1.
- [x] Adam Hadwin is now on the Korn Ferry following a really disappointing year last year. I think he’s better than that, and with the few opportunities he will get to play in PGA tour events, he needs to make them count. He’s here this week as an alt player and usually like the wind. Could go well at a big price.
Post Tournament Notes:
A crazy finish saw Echavarria snatch a win after Lowry completely bottles it, hitting it in the drink off the 16th tee and again on the par3 17th. I thought the course played better this year, wind made it interesting and the greens were firm meaning you were still at some disadvantage playing out of the overseeded rye rough. That is Echavarrias third PGA tour win, an underrated player in my view and seems to thrive when under pressure.
Eye-catchers - Castillo looked good and ready to breakthrough soon.

Course Details:
Course Stellenbosch Golf Club, Stellenbosch, South Africa
Length 7,213 yards
Par	70 Two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Stellenbosch is hosting the South African Open for the first time since 1999 – when David Frost won with a five-under-par 72-hole total. Two 54-hole Sunshine Tour events have taken place at Stellenbosch since – the 2010 and 2019 Vodacom Origins event. The course has been upgraded and lengthened since. The fairways are kikuyu grass and the greens are bentgrass. Long par-fours and short par-threes are a feature of the course.
What will it take to win the South African Open? The course was set up easier for the Vodacom Origins events, with 54-hole winning scores of 17 under and 15 under, but the layout has been toughened up since and grinding greens in regulation will probably be the key to success.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Looking for decent approach play
- SA players have won this the last 8 events
- The event is played at a different course each year. 
- [x] Branden Grace has a decent record winning this in 2020 and T4 last year when in Durban (reduced to 54 holes). He’s also been playing good golf this year on LIV & the Dunhill championship back in December, not finishing outside of the top10. Dropping down in grade you could say for this week and this course should suit his game.
- [ ] Jayden Schaper
- [ ] Jacob Olesen
- [x] Kieren Vincent 
- [x] Oliver Bekker
Post Tournament Notes: Casey Jarvis does it again, I was slightly disappointed I overlooked him for this considering how good a player he is and the nature of players winning back to back this year especially in South Africa. Branden Grace was the only one out of mine that made the cut and was very underwhelming at the weekend. Stellenbosch is a decent course, down by the Cape Town region of SA. This means not as much altitude compared to when they play in other areas. Played firm with SA players featuring heavily again, they do seem much more accustom to the grasses.

Course Details:
Course Riviera Country Club, Pacific Palisades, California, USA
Lengths	7,383 yards
Par	71
Course type	Parkland
Course overview Riviera Country Club has been home for the tournament that started out in 1926 as the Los Angeles Open and in all its subsequent guises since 1973, except for in 1983, 1998 and last year, when fire damage meant the tournament was moved to Torrey Pines South.
Players will be presented with a course that needs to be managed and has unusual features, such as a par-three sixth that has a bunker in the middle of the green. And while the par-four tenth is driveable, it's a huge task to find the small green and danger lies around the edge.
Finding the kikuyu fairways is key as the rough presents all sorts of problems and the greens are poa annua.
What will it take to win? Riviera is a great test of course management and the savviest players often go well. Accurate iron play has been a key to success while scrambling ability and solid putting can often tip the balance. Course experience is a huge benefit too.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Different type of grass at Riv, it’s similar to Torrey Fairways: Kikuyugrass; Rough: Kikuyugrass 2″; 7,500 sq.ft average featuring Poa Annua; Tournament Stimp: 12.5ft.
- Looking for good putters on this surface, T2G, GIR important here as is a decent APP
- [ ] Tommy
- [ ] Fitz
- [x] Hideki Matsuyama
- [x] Ryan Fox
- [x] Ludvig Aberg
Post Tournament Notes:
Jacob Bridgemen gave himself a nice cushion of 6 from Rory after a brilliant Saturday round. He headed the field in putting & approach negating his driver length which is usually a trait that’s needed here. Really impressive after a disappointing finish at Pebble beach last week. Rory looked really good but couldn’t putt throughout the weekend. Ryan Fox the best of my bets finishing a good T7 for 125-1. Interested to see Xander come back to form, like Rory struggled with the putter at the weekend but I expect big things from him in near future.
Eye-catchers - Kurt Kitayama looks back to his best, pure class ball striker.

Course: Pebble Beach Golf Links, Spyglass Hill Golf Club
Course Details:
Course Pebble Beach Golf Links, Spyglass Hill Golf Club, Pebble Beach, California
Lengths Pebble Beach 6,989 yards; Spyglass Hill 7,071 yards
Par	Both 72
Course type	Links/parkland
Course Overview The Pebble Beach Pro-Am used to played over three different courses, with 36 holes at Pebble Beach and 18 apiece at Monterey Peninsula and Spyglass Hill, with a cut after 54 holes. However, the event was promoted to Signature status in 2024, meaning only two courses are now used and the field shrunk from 156 to just 80. 
The players have 18 holes at Pebble Beach and Spyglass Hill over the first two days, then the final 36 holes are at Pebble Beach. There is no cut.
Pebble Beach hosted the 2010 and 2019 US Opens, as well as the 1999 and 2018 US Amateur. Small greens are a feature of the iconic Californian venue and Spyglass Hill has traditionally been the most difficult venue in the Pro-Am. The greens are Poa annua.
What will it take to win Accurate approach-play and a sharp putting has been the key to success at Pebble Beach over the last decade.
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Looks like a small chance of rain Thursday morning, and on Sunday. Wind is fairly settled not gusting above 13mph.
- Looking for good putters this week on poa Anna surfaces, good APP play also important here
- [x] Scottie Scheffler looks to be in a different league compared to anyone else and has done for a while. A quite shocking opening round 73 at Scottsdale followed by 54 holes of near perfect golf left him a shot shy out of the playoff. He demolished the field in the AMEX on his first appearance of the year and with course form at Pebble of T6 & T9 the past two years he’s primed to take home the first signature event of the year. I can’t remember when/where mentioned but I believe he also has closer attention on tournaments which he has not yet won on, given this is played on a special course which he’s played well around it.
- [x] Michael Kim last appearance here in 2023 the year before this became a signature event resulted in a T11. He’s on a different level now having a great season last year. His game suits Pebble, specialising in PUTT & APP, he mentioned in his latest tweets this is his favourite track and after a T18 at Scottsdale his game is on point and could go well at a nice price this week.
- [x] Russell Henley not the longest hitter but you don’t have to be round here, sublime putter and 1st in APP over the past 3 months. He’s made a decent start to the year T8 at the AMEX, T13 at the Sony his only two appearances. Decent record here making his debut here in 2014. T5 last year 1st in PUTT as well as 7th in APP more of the same this week and he could go close.
- [x] Jason Day Similar profile to Russell Henley, doesn’t hit the ball as far as he did in his younger days. Hasn’t won for a while but I saw some promising signs at the AMEX his game looks sharp finishing 2nd behind scottie, 1st PUTT, 1st ARG, 3rd T2G. Played here tons down the years with some recent good finishes. T7 2021, T6 2024 & T13 2025."
Post Tournament Notes:
I really enjoy watching this event each year, Pebble is a classic with interesting holes with weather providing its protection. The wind played a big factor especially on the weekend. My fancies were not great this week with not even a place, things looked alright after 36 but all faded away apart from a resurgent Scottie on the Sunday, shooting a final round 63 (included 2 bogies) to finish two back. He almost put himself out of contention on the first round a Spyglass Hill shooting 72 but really clawed it back over the Friday and weekend especially Sunday.
Eye catchers: Sepp continues to impress after \

Course Details:
Course Doha Golf Club, Doha, Qatar
Length	7,508 yards
Par	72 - Four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Grass type Bermuda; Rough: Bermuda; Greens: Paspalum, 11’6″ on the stimp.
Overview Doha has been the traditional home of the Qatar Masters, staging the inaugural tournament in 1998, then every year until 2020, when Education City Golf Club took over for two years. Doha resumed hosting duties in 2022 for a windy event which produced the worst winning total in the tournament's history of seven under par. The Qatar Masters was played in either January, February or March every year, so the 2023 late October gathering was unusual. The weather is crucial at Doha, an exposed track which can be bullied by big-hitters under calm skies, but becomes much more testing in a breeze.
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy and control is required when the wind is blowing at Doha, but this is a long track where power is also a useful asset.
Key Attribute Accurary
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks good all week sunny, dry with not much wind which is slightly unusual for Qatar where he has been blustery in previous years.
- Schaper, Reed, Hillier & Ayora top the billing in the field this week.
- Tends to suit good ball strikers and accurate players, however if the wind is down can be overcome by big hitters.
- [x] Ugo Coussaud finished strongly last week to cap off his first top 10 of the season. The two times he’s played here he’s finished 2 in 2024 & T35 in 2025. He was in-between PGA and DPWT schedules last year which I don’t think helped and ended up being slightly disappointing year. He putted alright on these paspalum ok and hope for more of the same this week, T2G and APP are strong categories for Ugo and I expect a good week this week to get his season started well in an attempt to get back a PGA tour card.
- [x] Nacho Elvira has been playing lights out since the start of the Desert swing this year. I sometimes worry about his ability to close tournaments out, there is usually a wobble on the Sunday but he gets himself into those positions and like in Dubai proves he can get it done under pressure. T7 only 3 shots off the lead with 4 solid rounds, the paspalum greens this week are similar surface to those last week where he was ranked 6th +1.77 SG. He’s playing solid golf overall, comfortably in the top 50 in all true strokes gained categories and 50s for a player with a win and top 10 out of 3 tournaments is good value imo.
- [x] Joshua Berry I’ve kept an eye on since his great year on the hotel planner tour last year with a few impressive DPWT performances thrown in. He tailed off a bit towards the end of last year but still qualified for full DPWT rights this year. It’s been a slowish start missing 4 cuts between Aussie->SA events towards the end of last year. His first start this year in Bahrain last week resulted in T13 which is a great start to the year. This included T4 for GIR%, +1.21 PUTT SG. Missed the cut in this last year but can be forgiven for the 20yo. A huge price this week for the rookie but another strong putting week could put him right in contention.
Post Tournament Notes:
- One emphasis that I missed this week is trends with Links golf really shine through here. The top 20 of the leaderboard was full of links specialists. Such as Jacob Scov Olesen, Grant Forrest, Lagergren and more. Next year it’s worth referring back to tournaments such as the Scottish Open and Dunhil Links. Patrick Reed won his second DPWT event this year and is well on his way to picking up at PGA tour card at this rate. Ugo Coussaud had a very disappointing Friday to miss the cut after a decent first round, Berry never got that close. Nacho had a decent weekend to get close to the places but was two shy in the end, he is still playing great golf.
- Eye catchers - Lindell still really impressive and the good form continues, when Dan Bradbury’s putter warms up he’s a threat.

Course Details:
Course: Stadium Course, TPC Scottsdale, Scottsdale, Arizona
Length: 7,261 yards
Par: 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes Field 132 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Time difference: Arizona is seven hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type: Desert Course
Grass type: Fairways: Bermudagrass with Perennial Rye and Fine fescue; Rough: Bermudagrass overseeded with Perennial Rye and Fine Fescue 2″; Greens: 7,069 sq.ft average featuring TifEagle Bermudagrass overseeded with Poa Trivialis and Bentgrass; Tournament Stimp: 12ft.
Course overview: The course has been used for this event since 1987. A score in the high-teens under par is usually needed for victory. The galleries are a test for the competitors, with more than 200,000 fans normally crammed into the course for the Friday and Saturday. This was a Signature Event in 2023, with a stellar field Story of last year Nick Taylor defeated Charley Hoffman in a playoff, thwarting Scottie Scheffler's hat-trick bid Weather forecast Sunny and warm, with light breezes throughout
Type of player suited to the challenge: Scottsdale is traditionally conquered by players who churn out greens in regulation for four days
Key attribute: Power
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks good all week, dry & sunny with little winds. Thursday gets up to 9.5 mph
- Not a signature event but attracts a good field with loads of fans on site throughout the week. Scottie, Ben Griffin, Xander & Si Woo top the market.
- Thomas Detry trounced the field by 7 shots last year to win his maiden PGA Tour event. Quite surprising given he’s found it hard to win prior to this on both tours. He topped GIR, APP and 2nd in PUTT in this last year. Nick Taylor highlighted similar the year before T2 GIR, 9th APP but 1st in PUTT.
- APP/GIR looks to be the attributes this week with some good putting.
- [x] Nick Taylor Has a great record here having won this in 2024 and runner up in 2023. His game suits this track, not the longest hitter but APP & PUTT his fortes. He has won 3 times on Bermudagrass Made a really impressive start to the season with T13 in Hawaii with a first round of 62. Nothing spectacular at the amex T27 but he hasn’t got the best record there so can forgive him there, skipped the Farmers like he usually does giving him a week off prior to get the game sharp.
- [ ] Sepp Straka missed the shortlist, need to see a little more of him to take a punt but price did catch my eye.
- [x] Sahith Theegala had a terrible 2025 compared to 2024. Didn’t make the Playoffs at all and had a top finish of T17 at the Genesis. He is a talented player when on form and healthy who can contend at all different types of courses. So far in 2026 he’s already looking much better than last year. T31 at the Sony, with one bad first round followed by three impressive rounds. T8 at the Amex, similar story one bad first round with three quality. Farmers T7 with a bad round in R3. Putting SG looks really impressive with the APP steadily improving with each tournament. He’s getting back to his best and coming back to a course in Scottsdale he’s enjoyed in the past T3 in 2022, 5th 2024. Should go close this week at a decent price for his talents
- [x] Jake Knapp caught my eye on insta practising round here a few weeks back with friends, perhaps this is a target for him this year? I backed him in the Farmers last week and returned a T5 finish. A really solid week, finishing the Sunday with a quality 67 on a tough setup. His record here isn’t quite as good as Torrey but as long as he continues to putt well I think his game should suit better, more room off the tee, rough not a penal and perfect conditions.
- [x] Stephen Jaeger caught my eye at the Farmers last week. Not normally a course I would think he would do that well at as the rough is tough despite his advantages with length off the tee. He finished T5 with a 65,66 round the south course on the weekend, really impressive. He is a patchy type of player hence the price I’m getting this week but like Knapp could use his length with open fairways this week and if the putter works well like it did last week he should go well. Won on Bermudagress greens in the past in Houston.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Interesting week that saw Hideki loose in a playoff to Chris Gotterup who shot a brilliant final round 64 to set the clubhouse lead which Hideki couldn’t match. An interesting course which provides plenty of opportunities when in the right positions from the fairway. Gotterup was 49th in PUTT but for the rest of them putting was really at the fore this week. Knapp was the best performer from my selections with 8 place, he could have got into the playoff but played 14 & 16 really poorly two bogies that would of made up the two shot difference in the end. Still looks a really good prospect for the win this year. Sahith & Jaeger both were in good positions before Sundays round to pick up a place but both terrible Sundays. All in all it was an ok week that should have been better.
- Eye catchers - Nicolai Hojgaard is playing fantastic golf, chances went after a 2nd hole triple on Sunday but clawed back to shoot 68, Michael Thorbjornson & Si woo both elite ball strikers who can pick up a win very soon if they can hole pressure putts on a Sunday.

Course Details:
Course Doha Golf Club, Doha, Qatar
Length	7,508 yards
Par	72 - Four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Grass type Bermuda; Rough: Bermuda; Greens: Paspalum, 11’6″ on the stimp.
Overview Doha has been the traditional home of the Qatar Masters, staging the inaugural tournament in 1998, then every year until 2020, when Education City Golf Club took over for two years. Doha resumed hosting duties in 2022 for a windy event which produced the worst winning total in the tournament's history of seven under par. The Qatar Masters was played in either January, February or March every year, so the 2023 late October gathering was unusual. The weather is crucial at Doha, an exposed track which can be bullied by big-hitters under calm skies, but becomes much more testing in a breeze.
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy and control is required when the wind is blowing at Doha, but this is a long track where power is also a useful asset.
Key Attribute Accurary
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks good all week sunny, dry with not much wind which is slightly unusual for Qatar where he has been blustery in previous years.
- Schaper, Reed, Hillier & Ayora top the billing in the field this week.
- Tends to suit good ball strikers and accurate players, however if the wind is down can be overcome by big hitters.
- [x] Ugo Coussaud finished strongly last week to cap off his first top 10 of the season. The two times he’s played here he’s finished 2 in 2024 & T35 in 2025. He was in-between PGA and DPWT schedules last year which I don’t think helped and ended up being slightly disappointing year. He putted alright on these paspalum ok and hope for more of the same this week, T2G and APP are strong categories for Ugo and I expect a good week this week to get his season started well in an attempt to get back a PGA tour card.
- [x] Nacho Elvira has been playing lights out since the start of the Desert swing this year. I sometimes worry about his ability to close tournaments out, there is usually a wobble on the Sunday but he gets himself into those positions and like in Dubai proves he can get it done under pressure. T7 only 3 shots off the lead with 4 solid rounds, the paspalum greens this week are similar surface to those last week where he was ranked 6th +1.77 SG. He’s playing solid golf overall, comfortably in the top 50 in all true strokes gained categories and 50s for a player with a win and top 10 out of 3 tournaments is good value imo.
- [x] Joshua Berry I’ve kept an eye on since his great year on the hotel planner tour last year with a few impressive DPWT performances thrown in. He tailed off a bit towards the end of last year but still qualified for full DPWT rights this year. It’s been a slowish start missing 4 cuts between Aussie->SA events towards the end of last year. His first start this year in Bahrain last week resulted in T13 which is a great start to the year. This included T4 for GIR%, +1.21 PUTT SG. Missed the cut in this last year but can be forgiven for the 20yo. A huge price this week for the rookie but another strong putting week could put him right in contention.
Post Tournament Notes:
- One emphasis that I missed this week is trends with Links golf really shine through here. The top 20 of the leaderboard was full of links specialists. Such as Jacob Scov Olesen, Grant Forrest, Lagergren and more. Next year it’s worth referring back to tournaments such as the Scottish Open and Dunhil Links. Patrick Reed won his second DPWT event this year and is well on his way to picking up at PGA tour card at this rate. Ugo Coussaud had a very disappointing Friday to miss the cut after a decent first round, Berry never got that close. Nacho had a decent weekend to get close to the places but was two shy in the end, he is still playing great golf.
- Eye catchers - Lindell still really impressive and the good form continues, when Dan Bradbury’s putter warms up he’s a threat.

T31 at the Sony, with one bad first round followed by three impressive rounds. T8 at the Amex, similar story one bad first round with three quality. Farmers T7 with a bad round in R3. Putting SG looks really impressive with the APP steadily improving with each tournament. He’s getting back to his best and coming back to a course in Scottsdale he’s enjoyed in the past T3 in 2022, 5th 2024. Should go close this week at a decent price for his talents
- [x] Jake Knapp caught my eye on insta practising round here a few weeks back with friends, perhaps this is a target for him this year? I backed him in the Farmers last week and returned a T5 finish. A really solid week, finishing the Sunday with a quality 67 on a tough setup. His record here isn’t quite as good as Torrey but as long as he continues to putt well I think his game should suit better, more room off the tee, rough not a penal and perfect conditions.
- [x] Stephen Jaeger caught my eye at the Farmers last week. Not normally a course I would think he would do that well at as the rough is tough despite his advantages with length off the tee. He finished T5 with a 65,66 round the south course on the weekend, really impressive. He is a patchy type of player hence the price I’m getting this week but like Knapp could use his length with open fairways this week and if the putter works well like it did last week he should go well. Won on Bermudagress greens in the past in Houston.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Interesting week that saw Hideki loose in a playoff to Chris Gotterup who shot a brilliant final round 64 to set the clubhouse lead which Hideki couldn’t match. An interesting course which provides plenty of opportunities when in the right positions from the fairway. Gotterup was 49th in PUTT but for the rest of them putting was really at the fore this week. Knapp was the best performer from my selections with 8 place, he could have got into the playoff but played 14 & 16 really poorly two bogies that would of made up the two shot difference in the end. Still looks a really good prospect for the win this year. Sahith & Jaeger both were in good positions before Sundays round to pick up a place but both terrible Sundays. All in all it was an ok week that should have been better.
- Eye catchers - Nicolai Hojgaard is playing fantastic golf, chances went after a 2nd hole triple on Sunday but clawed back to shoot 68, Michael Thorbjornson & Si woo both elite ball strikers who can pick up a win very soon if they can hole pressure putts on a Sunday.

Course: Torrey Pines Golf Course (South Course);Torrey Pines Golf Course (North Course)
Course Details:
Course Torrey Pines South and North, La Jolla, San Diego, California
Lengths South 7,765 yards, North 7,258 yards
Par Both 72
Time difference California is eight hours behind the UK and Ireland
Last week – The American Express 1 S Straka (60-1), 2 J Thomas (12-1), T3 J Lower (150-1), J Day (70-1), T5 P Cantlay (16-1), C Hoffman (175-1), T7 C Villegas (500-1), T Moore (175-1), B Griffin (60-1), M Greyserman (35-1)
Course type Parkland
Grass type: Bentgrass (North) / Poa Annua (South)
Course overview Torrey Pines has hosted since 1968. The South, opened in 1957, was redesigned by Rees Jones in 2001 to make it tougher for the 2008 US Open, which was won by Tiger Woods. Jon Rahm won the US Open there in 2021. It is a severe test and has traditionally been at least two shots harder than the North in this event. Tom Weiskopf redesigned the North in 2015, while the South had five holes redesigned prior to the 2020 Farmers. The greens are bentgrass at the North and Poa annua at the South. The North is used for only one round and the final 36 holes are played at the South once the cut has been made. The small greens on the South are undulating and tricky
Story of last year PGA Tour rookie Matthieu Pavon fended off Nicolai Hojgaard to secure a sensational maiden title
Weather forecast Sunny, calm and pleasant over the first three days, before a cloudy, cool, breezy final round on Saturday
Type of player suited to the challenge The historic honours board at Torrey Pines indicated the venue is a big-hitters' paradise, but three of the last four winners have bucked the trend, and accurate approach-play seems most important of all since the course revisions
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seven of the last eight winners have ranked inside the top five for strokes-gained on approach
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks sunny with no rain and little wind all week.
- First two days are played over North & South with South playing the last 36 holes. South is the much harder playing at 7,700+ yards and on average two shots harder than the South.
- The North course also hosted the Genesis Invitational last year (due to LA fires) with Ludvig Aberg winning with -12.
- Interesting field here, headed by Xander Schauffele, JJ Spaun, Cameron Young, Ludvig Aberg & Patrick Cantlay. A lot of eyes will be on Brooks Koepka’s first PGA Tour event since his return from LIV.
- [ ] Sam Stevens has a good record here, finishing a shot behind in last year’s event won by Harris English. T6 last week in La Quinta ranking 8th OTT & 11th ATG. In last years event he ranked 1st T2G, 3rd APP 
- [x] Hideki Matsuyama - I’m looking for players this week who are good T2G, above average length but perhaps more importantly are topping the APP stats. Hideki fits the criteria, he has a decent start to the season in Hawaii finishing 13th with a good weekend scoring. That week he ranked 7th in APP, 3rd T2G and 6th ARG. In the last 6 months, his SG data in the field this week ranks him 12th in APP, 7th in T2G & 4th ARG. If he can get the putter going this week he could go real close, his SG data isn’t that great in that department but he was 5th in PUTT in the genesis last year (all on the south course). Course History is fairly strong here.
- [x] Jake Knapp - Had a great 2025 with some near misses and strong top10s. He started this season off well in Hawaii with a T11, perhaps not his best week T2G but 10th in PUTT & 19th in OTT is promising. He was T17 in the Genesis on the south course last year and 5th OTT, 6th T2G & 15th APP along with a poor putting performance, but in a strong field that was a good performance. He was T3 here in 2024 with almost similar SG stats but slightly better putting. If he putts like he did in Hawaii but displays his usual great T2G game he could go very close this week
- [x] Nicolai Hojgaard - Played great a course he is comfortable on last week in Dubai, he wants “bombers” courses to allow him to benefit from his length. This place also fits the bill. One concern you might have is the travel from Dubai to the west coast US in a short space of time, however he made the same move in 2024 when he finished T7 in Dubai and 2nd at Torrey just behind Matthieu Pavon. That was his sole appearance at Torrey which is quite impressive considering. He ranked 8th in putting last week which is a good sign on bentgrass greens. His SG stats in the past 6 months rank him 40th APP, 49th T2G and 3rd in distance. He could go one closer than in 2024 and break his maiden PGA tour tag.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Perfect weather offered little defence for Torrey Pines which was quite inspiring other than the south course is very long and the rough was thick. If you miss it off the tee here most holes are very hard to hit. Poa-anna grass greens did make a difference with some players struggling to get any momentum. Justin Rose won wire-to-wire in dominant fashion, he was 7 shots clear before the Sunday round and never looked like faltering winning by 7 clear in the end. Jake Knapp was the only player to return some money for me finishing T5, 15 under, he looks class at the moment and would have been in contention had it not been for Rose pulling clear. Promising to see the putter work well. Matsuyama was disappointing finishing T11, looked good T2G but missed a lot of short putts, he did similar in the Sony Open so something to look out for. Nicolai Hogjaard looked like he could potentially place but had an average back nine on Sunday to ultimately finish T22 but his game looks good and I’m expecting a good go at the PGA tour this year.
- Eye-catchers I continue to be bullish on Jake Knapp he could pick up a big win if he continues to play well, Pierceson Coody finished T2 at a course that suits his game. Sahith Theegala looks back to his best with a T7 finish.S9

Course Details:
Course Royal Golf Club, Kingdom of Bahrain, Bahrain
Length 7,302 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Bahrain is three hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Grass type Paspalum
Course overview The 2011 Volvo Golf Champions – a DP World Tour event won by Paul Casey – was the only previous DPWT course form for punters to study prior to last year's inaugural Bahrain Championship. Royal GC yielded a 20-under-par winning total in 2011, but only 13 under par topped the leaderboard 12 months ago. The Bahrain Open was played on the MENA Tour at this venue in 2019 and 2020. The Colin Montgomerie-designed layout, opened in 2009 and made entirely from paspalum grass, is lined by sandy waste bunkers. Man-made lakes punish the errant on the final four holes. The back nine has floodlights, affording after-dark golf. The fairways and greens have lots of undulations
Weather forecast Sunny and windy, particularly over the first two days
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy into heavily contoured greens appears the key to success. Hitting the right sections of the dancefloors should yield low scores
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
- As of Monday morning the weather dry, perhaps a little on the colder side with it being in the Middle East. Thursday & Friday looks relatively windy with gusts uptown 18mph on Thursday & 16mpg on Friday.
- Fairly long course but plays firm, emphasis on good accuracy and APP & T2G. Laurie Canter around top 10 in both categories. 
- DPWT have played here three times, first in 2011 then in 2024 (Dylan Fritelli 2 shots clear -13) & 2025 (Laurie Canter in a Playoff -14). Both the previous two years have been blustery.
- Ok field with plenty of DPWT regulars, based on DG rankings: Jayden Schaper (29), Angel Ayora (61), Daniel Hillier (65), Thomas Detry (90) & fresh off a win in Dubai Patrick Reed (97). 
- [ ] Jayden Schaper has really impressed since his two victories in SA & Mauritius at the back end of last year. As those were effectively home events for him there were question marks on how he would perform in the bigger events which he has put to bed. T4 in the Dubai Desert Classic & a respectable T20 in the Dubai Invitational. He’s almost already guaranteed a tour card come the end of the season, given how early it is in the season that is some feat. He is playing fantastic so far this year topping the Strokes gained as well as third in putting. Course history round here isn’t great missing the cut on both appearances however his game has levelled up.
- [ ] Julien Guerrier had a strong finish to his tournament in Dubai last week shooting a low round of the day 65 on Saturday and finishing up with a 69 on Sunday to finish T3. He finished the Dubai Invitational the week before T3 so he’s bang in form at the moment. Course history is also not bad , T8 in 2024 & T21 in 2025. 
- [x] Rikuya Hoshino has been a player I’ve followed for a little while. He gained a PGA tour card in 2024 after a win in the Qatar Masters with a few other good results on the DPWT. Struggled on the PGA tour and I think he had some injury troubles which didn’t help last year resulting in him loosing his card. Looks like he’s back playing a full DPWT schedule this year, after a slowish start this year, he finished T14. That with 11th APP last week shows a positive trend back to form which should give him some confidence heading into an event he finished T12 back in 2024.
- [x] Oliver Lindell is starting to show glimpses of the form he displayed last year with some very good top10/20 results. He had a bad round on the Saturday last week but other than that was impressive, 2nd in GIR% but and 6th in driving accuracy putting let him down slightly perhaps. T15 in the Dubai Invitational where he was 1st in putting so perhaps the bentgrass greens last week at the emirates gc wasn’t his favourite. If he can putt well on these paspalum greens he could go well this week.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Course was interesting, you could miss it off the tee and be lucky with a good lie in the desert, rough was relatively penal in that you can easily catch fliers. Greens were big but very moundy with some pins tucked away so you have to hit it in the right areas for birdie opportunities.  A typical DPWT finish with so many doing their best NOT to win. Callum Hill looked firmly in control after a Friday 61 but never looked convincing over the weekend and faltered in the playoff by shanking it in the water to hand the win to Freddy Schott. Patrick Reed also made the playoff but boggied the first hole. Calum Hill shanked it on the next into the water to hand the title to Freddy. Oliver Lindell came close for me T9, which included an 8 in R1, didn’t really threaten at all. Hoshino missed the cut
- Eye-catchers: Lindell continues his good form, Ugo Cossaud hit the ball really well and interesting to see Josh Berry play well with a T13 result.

Grass type: Bentgrass (North) / Poa Annua (South)
Course overview Torrey Pines has hosted since 1968. The South, opened in 1957, was redesigned by Rees Jones in 2001 to make it tougher for the 2008 US Open, which was won by Tiger Woods. Jon Rahm won the US Open there in 2021. It is a severe test and has traditionally been at least two shots harder than the North in this event. Tom Weiskopf redesigned the North in 2015, while the South had five holes redesigned prior to the 2020 Farmers. The greens are bentgrass at the North and Poa annua at the South. The North is used for only one round and the final 36 holes are played at the South once the cut has been made. The small greens on the South are undulating and tricky
Story of last year PGA Tour rookie Matthieu Pavon fended off Nicolai Hojgaard to secure a sensational maiden title
Weather forecast Sunny, calm and pleasant over the first three days, before a cloudy, cool, breezy final round on Saturday
Type of player suited to the challenge The historic honours board at Torrey Pines indicated the venue is a big-hitters' paradise, but three of the last four winners have bucked the trend, and accurate approach-play seems most important of all since the course revisions
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seven of the last eight winners have ranked inside the top five for strokes-gained on approach
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks sunny with no rain and little wind all week.
- First two days are played over North & South with South playing the last 36 holes. South is the much harder playing at 7,700+ yards and on average two shots harder than the South.
- The North course also hosted the Genesis Invitational last year (due to LA fires) with Ludvig Aberg winning with -12.
- Interesting field here, headed by Xander Schauffele, JJ Spaun, Cameron Young, Ludvig Aberg & Patrick Cantlay. A lot of eyes will be on Brooks Koepka’s first PGA Tour event since his return from LIV.
- [ ] Sam Stevens has a good record here, finishing a shot behind in last year’s event won by Harris English. T6 last week in La Quinta ranking 8th OTT & 11th ATG. In last years event he ranked 1st T2G, 3rd APP 
- [x] Hideki Matsuyama - I’m looking for players this week who are good T2G, above average length but perhaps more importantly are topping the APP stats. Hideki fits the criteria, he has a decent start to the season in Hawaii finishing 13th with a good weekend scoring. That week he ranked 7th in APP, 3rd T2G and 6th ARG. In the last 6 months, his SG data in the field this week ranks him 12th in APP, 7th in T2G & 4th ARG. If he can get the putter going this week he could go real close, his SG data isn’t that great in that department but he was 5th in PUTT in the genesis last year (all on the south course). Course History is fairly strong here.
- [x] Jake Knapp - Had a great 2025 with some near misses and strong top10s. He started this season off well in Hawaii with a T11, perhaps not his best week T2G but 10th in PUTT & 19th in OTT is promising. He was T17 in the Genesis on the south course last year and 5th OTT, 6th T2G & 15th APP along with a poor putting performance, but in a strong field that was a good performance. He was T3 here in 2024 with almost similar SG stats but slightly better putting. If he putts like he did in Hawaii but displays his usual great T2G game he could go very close this week
- [x] Nicolai Hojgaard - Played great a course he is comfortable on last week in Dubai, he wants “bombers” courses to allow him to benefit from his length. This place also fits the bill. One concern you might have is the travel from Dubai to the west coast US in a short space of time, however he made the same move in 2024 when he finished T7 in Dubai and 2nd at Torrey just behind Matthieu Pavon. That was his sole appearance at Torrey which is quite impressive considering. He ranked 8th in putting last week which is a good sign on bentgrass greens. His SG stats in the past 6 months rank him 40th APP, 49th T2G and 3rd in distance. He could go one closer than in 2024 and break his maiden PGA tour tag.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Perfect weather offered little defence for Torrey Pines which was quite inspiring other than the south course is very long and the rough was thick. If you miss it off the tee here most holes are very hard to hit. Poa-anna grass greens did make a difference with some players struggling to get any momentum. Justin Rose won wire-to-wire in dominant fashion, he was 7 shots clear before the Sunday round and never looked like faltering winning by 7 clear in the end. Jake Knapp was the only player to return some money for me finishing T5, 15 under, he looks class at the moment and would have been in contention had it not been for Rose pulling clear. Promising to see the putter work well. Matsuyama was disappointing finishing T11, looked good T2G but missed a lot of short putts, he did similar in the Sony Open so something to look out for. Nicolai Hogjaard looked like he could potentially place but had an average back nine on Sunday to ultimately finish T22 but his game looks good and I’m expecting a good go at the PGA tour this year.
- Eye-catchers I continue to be bullish on Jake Knapp he could pick up a big win if he continues to play well, Pierceson Coody finished T2 at a course that suits his game. Sahith Theegala looks back to his best with a T7 finish.S9

Course Details:
Course Royal Golf Club, Kingdom of Bahrain, Bahrain
Length 7,302 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Bahrain is three hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Grass type Paspalum
Course overview The 2011 Volvo Golf Champions – a DP World Tour event won by Paul Casey – was the only previous DPWT course form for punters to study prior to last year's inaugural Bahrain Championship. Royal GC yielded a 20-under-par winning total in 2011, but only 13 under par topped the leaderboard 12 months ago. The Bahrain Open was played on the MENA Tour at this venue in 2019 and 2020. The Colin Montgomerie-designed layout, opened in 2009 and made entirely from paspalum grass, is lined by sandy waste bunkers. Man-made lakes punish the errant on the final four holes. The back nine has floodlights, affording after-dark golf. The fairways and greens have lots of undulations
Weather forecast Sunny and windy, particularly over the first two days
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy into heavily contoured greens appears the key to success. Hitting the right sections of the dancefloors should yield low scores
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
- As of Monday morning the weather dry, perhaps a little on the colder side with it being in the Middle East. Thursday & Friday looks relatively windy with gusts uptown 18mph on Thursday & 16mpg on Friday.
- Fairly long course but plays firm, emphasis on good accuracy and APP & T2G. Laurie Canter around top 10 in both categories. 
- DPWT have played here three times, first in 2011 then in 2024 (Dylan Fritelli 2 shots clear -13) & 2025 (Laurie Canter in a Playoff -14). Both the previous two years have been blustery.
- Ok field with plenty of DPWT regulars, based on DG rankings: Jayden Schaper (29), Angel Ayora (61), Daniel Hillier (65), Thomas Detry (90) & fresh off a win in Dubai Patrick Reed (97). 
- [ ] Jayden Schaper has really impressed since his two victories in SA & Mauritius at the back end of last year. As those were effectively home events for him there were question marks on how he would perform in the bigger events which he has put to bed. T4 in the Dubai Desert Classic & a respectable T20 in the Dubai Invitational. He’s almost already guaranteed a tour card come the end of the season, given how early it is in the season that is some feat. He is playing fantastic so far this year topping the Strokes gained as well as third in putting. Course history round here isn’t great missing the cut on both appearances however his game has levelled up.
- [ ] Julien Guerrier had a strong finish to his tournament in Dubai last week shooting a low round of the day 65 on Saturday and finishing up with a 69 on Sunday to finish T3. He finished the Dubai Invitational the week before T3 so he’s bang in form at the moment. Course history is also not bad , T8 in 2024 & T21 in 2025. 
- [x] Rikuya Hoshino has been a player I’ve followed for a little while. He gained a PGA tour card in 2024 after a win in the Qatar Masters with a few other good results on the DPWT. Struggled on the PGA tour and I think he had some injury troubles which didn’t help last year resulting in him loosing his card. Looks like he’s back playing a full DPWT schedule this year, after a slowish start this year, he finished T14. That with 11th APP last week shows a positive trend back to form which should give him some confidence heading into an event he finished T12 back in 2024.
- [x] Oliver Lindell is starting to show glimpses of the form he displayed last year with some very good top10/20 results. He had a bad round on the Saturday last week but other than that was impressive, 2nd in GIR% but and 6th in driving accuracy putting let him down slightly perhaps. T15 in the Dubai Invitational where he was 1st in putting so perhaps the bentgrass greens last week at the emirates gc wasn’t his favourite. If he can putt well on these paspalum greens he could go well this week.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Course was interesting, you could miss it off the tee and be lucky with a good lie in the desert, rough was relatively penal in that you can easily catch fliers. Greens were big but very moundy with some pins tucked away so you have to hit it in the right areas for birdie opportunities.  A typical DPWT finish with so many doing their best NOT to win. Callum Hill looked firmly in control after a Friday 61 but never looked convincing over the weekend and faltered in the playoff by shanking it in the water to hand the win to Freddy Schott. Patrick Reed also made the playoff but boggied the first hole. Calum Hill shanked it on the next into the water to hand the title to Freddy. Oliver Lindell came close for me T9, which included an 8 in R1, didn’t really threaten at all. Hoshino missed the cut
- Eye-catchers: Lindell continues his good form, Ugo Cossaud hit the ball really well and interesting to see Josh Berry play well with a T13 result.

Course Details:
Course Majlis Course, Emirates Golf Club, Dubai, United Arab Emirates
Length 7,428 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Dubai is four hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Course overview Emirates Golf Club has been used for the Desert Classic since 1989 apart from a two-year switch to Dubai Creek from 1999 to 2000. Traditionally the Emirates has been a low-scoring track where something approaching or passing 20 under par has been required for victory. The back nine is the scoring half, with three par-fives (the tenth, 13th and 18th) and two relatively short par-threes (11th and 15th). Water comes into play on ten holes, but fairly straightforward, flat greens mean the competitors can relax once they have got putter in hand
Type of player suited to the challenge The Emirates has traditionally been a big-hitters' track and the champions usually make their score on the par-fives. Those who can hit it long and fairly straight, and are comfortable in desert conditions, should thrive
Key attribute Power
Insight Eight of the last 12 Dubai Desert Classic winners finished in the top ten of the driving distance stats
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather is sunny and dry but windy particularly on Thursday gusting up to 25mph
- Typically a course suited to bombers of the tee, some water about but T2G is the main thing here with relatively flat greens
- Good field with plenty of Livrs playing, such as Jaoquin Niemann, David Puig, Patrick Reed to name a few.
- Typical winning score varies between 10-15 under.
- [x] Rory McIlroy - The king of this event, winning round here 4 times already and in the top 10 here in his last 11 starts. He admitted last week in the Dubai Invitational was a practise event for this one, using a new driver which he admitted was in preparation for this week. He looked brilliant last week and came close despite some careless mistakes. I don’t think that happens this week and he could blow away the field. Needless to say with Rory he superior OTT and despite a few small errors last week looks like he will continue in that fashion.
- [x] David Puig - Astonistioningly David turned up late in the evening last Wednesday evening to the Dubai Invitational without any practise and still finished 3rd. Plenty of silly errors in there as well in particular some shot misses on the greens. He won the Australian Open in great fashion seeing off danger from talented Wenyi Ding. He’s a class act and could push Rory all the way this week and for me is the main danger. Fits into the bombers category for the last 3 months he’s ranked 10th in distance and 1st OTT.
- [ ] - Daniel Hillier this past few months has been playing top quality golf, a quality 65 in the last round last week saw him finish a close second to Nacho Elvira. Want to continue backing him but just missed the shortlist.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Always a great event at the start of the year in Dubai. The course was setup very hard this year, harder than ever before according to some. Rough was very thick in places and greens hard and firm, where there was wind it was tough to keep the ball on the dance floor. Patrick Reed prevailed winning by four shots over Andy Sullivan, took the lead on Saturday and was very hard to catch, playing great golf round a track he has gone close to winning in the past. Rory never really got going, not his best week OTT and the putter was cold all week, David Puig had a decent chance playing in the final group on Sunday, at one stage he closed the gap to one for a short while on the par 3 11th but then missed 4ft putt for par and then a bogie on the par 5 13th put an end to his chances. He is a quality talent but needs to refine his game slightly and learn to adapt in a position like that in the final group on a Sunday in a big event.
- Eye catchers: Martin Couvra returning to good form, Nicolai Hojgaard looked more comfortable here compared to Dubai Creek and looks to be on his game.

Course: Pete Dye Stadium Course;La Quinta Country Club;Nicklaus Tournament Course
Course Details:
Courses Pete Dye Stadium Course at PGA West, Nicklaus Tournament Course at PGA West, La Quinta Country Club, La Quinta, California
Lengths Stadium 7,210 yards, Nicklaus 7,147 yards, La Quinta 7,060 yards
Par All 72
Time difference California is eight hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Gass type N/A
Course overview Various layouts have been used for this multi-course pro-am through the years, which was previously the Bob Hope Classic, Humana Challenge, CareerBuilder Challenge and Desert Classic. The Stadium Course returned after a 29-year absence to act as host in 2016, and the Nicklaus Tournament Course made its debut, joining La Quinta CC for a three-course event. The same rota has been used every year since (apart from 2021 – for which La Quinta took a year off) with one round played at each of the tracks over the first three days, and the cut-makers gathering at the Stadium Course on Sunday. The Stadium Course, designed by Pete Dye, is full of deep bunkers and water, and has similarities to the TPC Sawgrass. It usually plays harder than the Nicklaus Tournament Course, with La Quinta the easiest of the trio. The Stadium Course and the Nicklaus Tournament Course were both used for the Tour Qualifying School Finals in 2002, 2006 and 2008
Weather forecast Sunny and calm, with temperatures peaking at 22C
Type of player suited to the challenge A hot putter seems almost essential in this event, with the courses set up to with hardly any rough to allow the amateurs to get round, encouraging low scoring
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight Only two of the last 17 winners of this event were making their first start of the year
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks sunny, dry all week with not a lot of wind, upto 10mph on Saturday.
- Played over three different courses Pete Dye Stadium Course, La Quinta Country Club, Nicklaus Tournament Course with a cut after 3 rounds and final round played on the (harder) stadium course on Sunday.
- Last three winning scores have won with sub 20 under scores.
- Stacked field, including Scottie, Ben Griffin, Russell Henley, Patrick Cantlay to name a few.

Course Details:
Course Majlis Course, Emirates Golf Club, Dubai, United Arab Emirates
Length 7,428 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Dubai is four hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Course overview Emirates Golf Club has been used for the Desert Classic since 1989 apart from a two-year switch to Dubai Creek from 1999 to 2000. Traditionally the Emirates has been a low-scoring track where something approaching or passing 20 under par has been required for victory. The back nine is the scoring half, with three par-fives (the tenth, 13th and 18th) and two relatively short par-threes (11th and 15th). Water comes into play on ten holes, but fairly straightforward, flat greens mean the competitors can relax once they have got putter in hand
Type of player suited to the challenge The Emirates has traditionally been a big-hitters' track and the champions usually make their score on the par-fives. Those who can hit it long and fairly straight, and are comfortable in desert conditions, should thrive
Key attribute Power
Insight Eight of the last 12 Dubai Desert Classic winners finished in the top ten of the driving distance stats
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather is sunny and dry but windy particularly on Thursday gusting up to 25mph
- Typically a course suited to bombers of the tee, some water about but T2G is the main thing here with relatively flat greens
- Good field with plenty of Livrs playing, such as Jaoquin Niemann, David Puig, Patrick Reed to name a few.
- Typical winning score varies between 10-15 under.
- [x] Rory McIlroy - The king of this event, winning round here 4 times already and in the top 10 here in his last 11 starts. He admitted last week in the Dubai Invitational was a practise event for this one, using a new driver which he admitted was in preparation for this week. He looked brilliant last week and came close despite some careless mistakes. I don’t think that happens this week and he could blow away the field. Needless to say with Rory he superior OTT and despite a few small errors last week looks like he will continue in that fashion.
- [x] David Puig - Astonistioningly David turned up late in the evening last Wednesday evening to the Dubai Invitational without any practise and still finished 3rd. Plenty of silly errors in there as well in particular some shot misses on the greens. He won the Australian Open in great fashion seeing off danger from talented Wenyi Ding. He’s a class act and could push Rory all the way this week and for me is the main danger. Fits into the bombers category for the last 3 months he’s ranked 10th in distance and 1st OTT.
- [ ] - Daniel Hillier this past few months has been playing top quality golf, a quality 65 in the last round last week saw him finish a close second to Nacho Elvira. Want to continue backing him but just missed the shortlist.
Post Tournament Notes:
- Always a great event at the start of the year in Dubai. The course was setup very hard this year, harder than ever before according to some. Rough was very thick in places and greens hard and firm, where there was wind it was tough to keep the ball on the dance floor. Patrick Reed prevailed winning by four shots over Andy Sullivan, took the lead on Saturday and was very hard to catch, playing great golf round a track he has gone close to winning in the past. Rory never really got going, not his best week OTT and the putter was cold all week, David Puig had a decent chance playing in the final group on Sunday, at one stage he closed the gap to one for a short while on the par 3 11th but then missed 4ft putt for par and then a bogie on the par 5 13th put an end to his chances. He is a quality talent but needs to refine his game slightly and learn to adapt in a position like that in the final group on a Sunday in a big event.
- Eye catchers: Martin Couvra returning to good form, Nicolai Hojgaard looked more comfortable here compared to Dubai Creek and looks to be on his game.
