Tournament: Dubai Desert Classic / The American Express
Course Details: N/A

Course Dubai Creek Resort, Dubai, United Arab EmiratesLength 7,059 yardsPar 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Dubai is four hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Grass Type Bermuda grass has been used throughout the course with the greens being TifEagle Bermuda and running firm and fast in ideal conditions.
Course overview Dubai Creek hosted the Dubai Desert Classic in 1999 and 2000, with David Howell and Jose Coceres winning the events. The front nine has been revised since then, but the back nine is a layout which has stood the test of time. The course opened in 1993 and has always been in immaculate condition. Lush fairways run alongside the creek. There is more water in play than for typical desert layouts and the rough is grown to counter the course's lack of length. This is a pro-am, with 60 amateurs taking part
Weather forecast Sunny and pleasant with gentle breezes throughout
Type of player suited to the challenge The leaderboards from the Desert Classics at Dubai Creek show that accuracy wins over power at this venue. Jose Coceres, the 2000 champion, was a neat and tidy player, and just behind him that week were Paul McGinley, Patrik Sjoland, Jamie Spence and Paul Lawrie. Support straight-hitters who putt well
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather is sunny all week, somewhat blustery on Friday getting up to 19-20mph.
- Course looks suited to accuracy, emphasis on finding fairways and good putting.
- Course history for this event only goes back to the last time it was played in 2024 (Tommy Fleetwood won with -19).
- Strong field with Tommy, Rory, Lowry, Nicolai Hojgaard, just to name a few.

- [ ] Ewen Ferguson – Local to Dubai, had a mediocre 2025 season but does shine in the Middle East. Finished T10 in the Nedbank last time out with strong APP and accuracy OTT. Finished T11 here in 2024 and could go even better this year.
- [x] Daniel Hillier – Upcoming talent who could obtain a PGA Tour card by season's end. Had a strong close to 2025, stayed busy in the off-season, playing both events in Australia, 4 top 10s out of 7 tournaments, including a T5 at Abu Dhabi HSBC. High-level record in UAE, including T2 at the Dubai Desert Classic (1 shot behind Tyrell).
- [x] Tommy Fleetwood – Arguably the second best player in the world right now behind Scottie. Won this event in 2024, and finished 2025 in spectacular fashion (Tour Championship win in August, DPWT Indian Championship win in October, one shot from playoff in the DPWT Tour Championship). Game suits this track: accuracy OTT, impressive iron play, sneaky good putting.
Post Tournament Notes:
- An entertaining event on a different sort of track that you usually find in the Middle East. Wind played a big factor most of the week, typically in Dubai its calm in the mornings with the breeze picking up in the after. The wind on Friday was brutal as the scoring showed, Saturday some let up but still a big factor. The Majlis Course really emphasises accuracy, with lots of water and tough Bermuda rough. If you don’t find the fairway here it becomes hard to hit these firm greens and on some holes it may run off into the drink. Nacho Elvira won on -10 having seemingly thrown away his chances with some poor swings around the 8/9/10/11 but turned it around including a really nice birdie on 17 to go a shot clear of Hillier. Hillier one of my two picks of the week played fantastic, steady all week without any big numbers on the scorecard, he turned up the heat on Sunday with a -6 including an eagle on the par 5 13th. A win for him must be coming soon and I expect a big season from him. Tommy blew his chances with a +7 round on Friday, however some decent golf throughout the weekend got him back to level for the tournament. I guess he was rusty after the winter break but some signs he’s getting back to some decent form.
- Eye-catchers: David Puig continues to impress, turned up late on Wednesday evening after a LIV event in the US. Stranger to the course but still finished 3rd and left plenty out there.

Course Details:
Course Waialae Country Club, Honolulu, Hawaii
Length 7,044 yards
Par 70 – two par-five holes, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Hawaii is ten hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Grass Type Fairways Bermudagrass; Rough: Bermudagrass, Greens: TifDwarf Bermudagrass; Stimpmeter: 11ft.
Course overview Waialae, host since 1965, contrasts greatly with the wide-open Plantation Course where Hideki Matsuyama won The Sentry in Maui last week. The fairways are thin, the greens are small and accuracy is the key to success. The Hawaii turf and trade winds are similar to Kapalua, though, and Sentry runners have an excellent record of Sony success
Type of player suited to the challenge Pure, accurate ball-striking has typically been the key to success at Waialae, as demonstrated by wins for David Toms, Paul Goydos, KJ Choi, Zach Johnson, Mark Wilson, Johnson Wagner, Fabian Gomez and Matt Kuchar
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seventeen of the last 26 Sony Open winners played at Kapalua the previous week
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks warm and relatively settled. Small chance of rain Thursday morning but dry rest of the week.
- Course suited towards accuracy both OTT & APP.
- Course fit similar to tracks like Harbour Town, Colonial.
- Mid-high winning score average. Wind can play a factor.
- [ ] Russell Henley - This track requires accuracy off the tee and good approach play which Russell has in abundance. Won this event back in 2013 in his rookie year and his record since 2021 is impressive; T11, 2, T32, T4, T10.
- [ ] Nico Echavarria - T2 here last year and has been in good form towards the end of 2025. Already a 2 time PGA tour champion one of those being at the Puerto Rico Open which I think is similar to this test.
- [ ] Si Woo Kim - Great form heading into the back end of last year finishing off with a 3rd in the Australia open. Won this back in 2023 and should go well here again.
- [x] JJ Spaun - Top 10 on APP sg on tour. 1st APP sg here last year when finishing 3rd. His career is on a new level since his US Open win and his results have not dipped.
- [x] Adam Schenk - T6 in this last year with a Sunday 65. Picked up his first win in Bermuda last year in hurricane like conditions if the wind does get up he should cope.
- [x] Chris Kirk - Popping up in a lot of queries I see regarding approach play. Has a fantastic record here including T2 in 2021 & 3rd 2023.
Post Tournament Notes:
- I didn’t watch too much of this due to the time difference but seemed an interesting tournament from the little I did see. An intricate course where you need to be in the fairway to score. Wind played a huge factor all throughout the week, especially in round 2. Chris Gotterup prevailed on -16 to win by two over Ryan Gerard, Gotterups low ball flight seemed to have been a huge benefit to him this week he ranked 1st OTT and 7th in Putting. Only one of my three made the cut, JJ Spaun and he was disappointing with a T40 finish, seemed a bit rusty with plenty of mistakes. 
- Eye-catchers: Ryan Gerard continues to play well after his near miss in Mauritius Open last month. Robert MacIntyre shot a closing 63 on Sunday and snapped his putter on 17? Looks fired up. Also keep an eye on Patrick Rodgers, closing 65 to close up a good week in 3rd, sure a win is on the way for him soon.S7

Course Details:
Course Dubai Creek Resort, Dubai, United Arab EmiratesLength 7,059 yardsPar 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Dubai is four hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Desert
Grass Type Bermuda grass has been used throughout the course with the greens being TifEagle Bermuda and running firm and fast in ideal conditions.
Course overview Dubai Creek hosted the Dubai Desert Classic in 1999 and 2000, with David Howell and Jose Coceres winning the events. The front nine has been revised since then, but the back nine is a layout which has stood the test of time. The course opened in 1993 and has always been in immaculate condition. Lush fairways run alongside the creek. There is more water in play than for typical desert layouts and the rough is grown to counter the course's lack of length. This is a pro-am, with 60 amateurs taking part
Weather forecast Sunny and pleasant with gentle breezes throughout
Type of player suited to the challenge The leaderboards from the Desert Classics at Dubai Creek show that accuracy wins over power at this venue. Jose Coceres, the 2000 champion, was a neat and tidy player, and just behind him that week were Paul McGinley, Patrik Sjoland, Jamie Spence and Paul Lawrie. Support straight-hitters who putt well
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather is sunny all week, somewhat blustery on Friday getting up to 19-20mph.
- Course looks suited to accuracy, emphasis on finding fairways and good putting.
- Course history for this event only goes back to the last time it was played in 2024 (Tommy Fleetwood won with -19).
- Strong field with Tommy, Rory, Lowry, Nicolai Hojgaard, just to name a few.

- [ ] Ewen Ferguson – Local to Dubai, had a mediocre 2025 season but does shine in the Middle East. Finished T10 in the Nedbank last time out with strong APP and accuracy OTT. Finished T11 here in 2024 and could go even better this year.
- [x] Daniel Hillier – Upcoming talent who could obtain a PGA Tour card by season's end. Had a strong close to 2025, stayed busy in the off-season, playing both events in Australia, 4 top 10s out of 7 tournaments, including a T5 at Abu Dhabi HSBC. High-level record in UAE, including T2 at the Dubai Desert Classic (1 shot behind Tyrell).
- [x] Tommy Fleetwood – Arguably the second best player in the world right now behind Scottie. Won this event in 2024, and finished 2025 in spectacular fashion (Tour Championship win in August, DPWT Indian Championship win in October, one shot from playoff in the DPWT Tour Championship). Game suits this track: accuracy OTT, impressive iron play, sneaky good putting.
Post Tournament Notes:
- An entertaining event on a different sort of track that you usually find in the Middle East. Wind played a big factor most of the week, typically in Dubai its calm in the mornings with the breeze picking up in the after. The wind on Friday was brutal as the scoring showed, Saturday some let up but still a big factor. The Majlis Course really emphasises accuracy, with lots of water and tough Bermuda rough. If you don’t find the fairway here it becomes hard to hit these firm greens and on some holes it may run off into the drink. Nacho Elvira won on -10 having seemingly thrown away his chances with some poor swings around the 8/9/10/11 but turned it around including a really nice birdie on 17 to go a shot clear of Hillier. Hillier one of my two picks of the week played fantastic, steady all week without any big numbers on the scorecard, he turned up the heat on Sunday with a -6 including an eagle on the par 5 13th. A win for him must be coming soon and I expect a big season from him. Tommy blew his chances with a +7 round on Friday, however some decent golf throughout the weekend got him back to level for the tournament. I guess he was rusty after the winter break but some signs he’s getting back to some decent form.
- Eye-catchers: David Puig continues to impress, turned up late on Wednesday evening after a LIV event in the US. Stranger to the course but still finished 3rd and left plenty out there.

Course Details:
Course Waialae Country Club, Honolulu, Hawaii
Length 7,044 yards
Par 70 – two par-five holes, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Hawaii is ten hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Grass Type Fairways Bermudagrass; Rough: Bermudagrass, Greens: TifDwarf Bermudagrass; Stimpmeter: 11ft.
Course overview Waialae, host since 1965, contrasts greatly with the wide-open Plantation Course where Hideki Matsuyama won The Sentry in Maui last week. The fairways are thin, the greens are small and accuracy is the key to success. The Hawaii turf and trade winds are similar to Kapalua, though, and Sentry runners have an excellent record of Sony success
Type of player suited to the challenge Pure, accurate ball-striking has typically been the key to success at Waialae, as demonstrated by wins for David Toms, Paul Goydos, KJ Choi, Zach Johnson, Mark Wilson, Johnson Wagner, Fabian Gomez and Matt Kuchar
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seventeen of the last 26 Sony Open winners played at Kapalua the previous week
Pre Tournament Notes:
- Weather looks warm and relatively settled. Small chance of rain Thursday morning but dry rest of the week.
- Course suited towards accuracy both OTT & APP.
- Course fit similar to tracks like Harbour Town, Colonial.
- Mid-high winning score average. Wind can play a factor.
- [ ] Russell Henley - This track requires accuracy off the tee and good approach play which Russell has in abundance. Won this event back in 2013 in his rookie year and his record since 2021 is impressive; T11, 2, T32, T4, T10.
- [ ] Nico Echavarria - T2 here last year and has been in good form towards the end of 2025. Already a 2 time PGA tour champion one of those being at the Puerto Rico Open which I think is similar to this test.
- [ ] Si Woo Kim - Great form heading into the back end of last year finishing off with a 3rd in the Australia open. Won this back in 2023 and should go well here again.
- [x] JJ Spaun - Top 10 on APP sg on tour. 1st APP sg here last year when finishing 3rd. His career is on a new level since his US Open win and his results have not dipped.
- [x] Adam Schenk - T6 in this last year with a Sunday 65. Picked up his first win in Bermuda last year in hurricane like conditions if the wind does get up he should cope.
- [x] Chris Kirk - Popping up in a lot of queries I see regarding approach play. Has a fantastic record here including T2 in 2021 & 3rd 2023.
Post Tournament Notes:
- I didn’t watch too much of this due to the time difference but seemed an interesting tournament from the little I did see. An intricate course where you need to be in the fairway to score. Wind played a huge factor all throughout the week, especially in round 2. Chris Gotterup prevailed on -16 to win by two over Ryan Gerard, Gotterups low ball flight seemed to have been a huge benefit to him this week he ranked 1st OTT and 7th in Putting. Only one of my three made the cut, JJ Spaun and he was disappointing with a T40 finish, seemed a bit rusty with plenty of mistakes. 
- Eye-catchers: Ryan Gerard continues to play well after his near miss in Mauritius Open last month. Robert MacIntyre shot a closing 63 on Sunday and snapped his putter on 17? Looks fired up. Also keep an eye on Patrick Rodgers, closing 65 to close up a good week in 3rd, sure a win is on the way for him soon.S7

Course La Reserve Golf Links, Heritage Golf Club, Bel Ombre, Mauritius
Length 7,146 yards
Par 72– Five par-fives, eight par-fours, five par-threes
Course type Links
Course overview La Reserve Golf Links opened on December 1, 2023, so the Mauritius Open competitors were tackling a brand-new course that year. It is the first and only contemporary links track in the Indian Ocean. South African Peter Matkovich, with assistance from Louis Oosthuizen, designed the course. It has been crafted as a “strategic, undulating course that plays in the traditional links style, with running fairways, pot bunkers and long grasses”. The neighbouring Heritage Golf Club staged the Mauritius Open in 2015, 2017 and 2019. Mont Choisy Le Golf was last year's Mauritius Open venue.Open winners there (three rounds at East) finished first and second for GIR .
Type of player suited to the challenge The Renaissance Club, home of the Scottish Open since 2019, is an example of a contemporary links used on the DP World Tour. Yas Links (2022-2025 Abu Dhabi Championship) is another. Controlling your ball in a breeze, plus touch and control around the greens, are the keys to success.
Insight Three of the eight Mauritius Opens have been won by a South African.

Course East Course, Royal Johannesburg Club, Johannesburg, South Africa.
Length 7,146 yards
Par 72– Three par-fives, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Alfred Dunhill Championship is returning to its historical roots in Johannesburg this week, with Royal Johannesburg taking over from Leopard Creek, Mpumalanga. Leopard Creek has hosted the Alfred Dunhill since December 2004 but the course is not in good condition after an intensely hot summer, so the East Course at Royal Johannesburg, formerly Royal Johannesburg and Kensington, is acting as a substitute. The East and West used to stage the Joburg Open on the DP World Tour from 2007 until 2017. The parkland East has a stream running through it, with water coming into play on seven holes, as well as countless bunkers. The fairways are kikuyu grass and the greens are bentgrass. Some low-grade Sunshine Tour events have also been staged at the East – the 2002 Vodacom Classic, the 2021 Kit Kat Group Pro Am and the 2024 and 2025 Tournament of Champions.
Type of player suited to the challenge Churning out greens in regulation has been the key to success at the East Course in the past – the last two Joburg Open winners there (three rounds at East) finished first and second for GIR .
Insight Ten of the last 12 Alfred Dunhill winners have been South African.

The parkland East has a stream running through it, with water coming into play on seven holes, as well as countless bunkers. The fairways are kikuyu grass and the greens are bentgrass. Some low-grade Sunshine Tour events have also been staged at the East – the 2002 Vodacom Classic, the 2021 Kit Kat Group Pro Am and the 2024 and 2025 Tournament of Champions.
Type of player suited to the challenge Churning out greens in regulation has been the key to success at the East Course in the past – the last two Joburg Open winners there (three rounds at East) finished first and second for GIR .

Course Gary Player Country Club, Sun City, South Africa
Length 7,819 yards
Par 72– Four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Gary Player Country Club, which hosted the Million Dollar Challenge and the Nedbank Challenge from 1981 to 2019, staged the South African Open in 2020 (December) and 2021, then the Nedbank returned in 2022. The Dimension-Data Pro-Am (1996 to 2009), the Sun City Challenge (2007-11 and 2016-21), the Royal Swazi Open (2020), the Blue Label Challenge (2021, 2022, 2024, 2025), the Vodacom Origins of Golf Final (2022), the SunBet Challenge (2022, 2024) and the Gary & Vivienne Player Invitational (2024, 2025) are low-grade Sunshine Tour events also staged at this venue. The course is enormous, but a variety of tees can be used to alter the yardage if bad weather makes the full length unplayable. The layout boasts kikuyu fairways and fast, clover-shaped, bentgrass greens surrounded by bunkers, swales and mounds. Some extremely low scores were carded many years ago – Padraig Harrington shot an 11-under-par 61 in 2001 playing with preferred lies, while Ernie Els (25 under par in 1999) and Nick Price (24 under in 1993) have also butchered Player's design. The lengthening of the course, though, has made life tougher for visitors. The layout is at altitude, so the ball flies further than usual.
Type of player suited to the challenge Quality ball-strikers have asserted themselves on the Gary Player Country Club over the last decade - churning out greens in regulation is the key to success.
Insight Nine of the last 13 Nedbank winners have been European.

Course Royal Melbourne Golf Club, Melbourne, Australia
Length 7,086 yards
Par 71– Two par-five holes; 13 par-fours; three par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Royal Melbourne, founded in 1891, is widely regarded as one of the world's premier golfing venues. It has staged numerous Australian Opens, Australian Amateur Championships, Australian PGA Championships and Heineken Classics. The last Australian Open there was in 1991. The Presidents Cup was at Royal Melbourne in 1998, 2011 and 2019, as well as the World Cup of Golf in 2013. A composite course using the East and West courses is used for tournaments. The fairways are generous, but flanked by waste areas and heather. Finding the right angle to attack well-guarded greens is the main challenge. The greens are large and fast.
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate iron-play and short-game class is the key to success at Royal Melbourne.
Insight Australians have won only three of the last ten Australian Opens.

Course Royal Queensland Golf Club, Brisbane, Queensland, Australia
Length 7,085 yards
Par 71– Three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Royal Queensland was redesigned by Michael Clayton and rebuilt, opening in December 2007 and staging two Australian PGAs in 2022 – one in January on the Australasian Tour and one in November co-sanctioned with the DP World Tour. In 2023, there was only one Australian PGA played at Royal Queensland – co-sanctioned by the DP World Tour in November – and that was also the case last year. The Australian Amateur was staged at the Brisbane venue in 2009 and 2020. This flat course is on the northern bank of the Brisbane River. The fairways are wide and there is no rough, but bunkers are strategically placed, with some even in the middle of fairways at the three par-fives.
Type of player suited to the challenge Finding the right sections of the fairways to get the best angle of attack to the greens is important, but putting well is arguably the key on a low-scoring track which will probably get sprinkled with some pre-tournament showers.
Insight 23 of the last 24 winners of this event have been Australian

Course: Seaside Course and Plantation Course, Sea Island Resort
Course Details:
Course Seaside Course and Plantation Course, Sea Island Resort, Sea Island, Georgia
Length Seaside 7,005 yards; Plantation 7,060 yards
Par 71– Three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Links/Parkland
Course overview The Seaside Course, crafted by Tom Fazio and used as regular practice turf by local residents such as Matt Kuchar, Zach Johnson, Harris English, Keith Mitchell, JT Poston, Davis Love, Patton Kizzire and Brian Harman, became a PGA Tour venue for the first time in 2010. The strength of the sea breezes impacts greatly on the scoring at this exposed layout, while heavily undulating fairways add to the test. At least 14 under par has been needed to top the 72-hole leaderboard in this event. This is a fiddly layout where precision iron-play and sharp scrambling around tricky greens are crucial. The fairways on this links, which is similar to the more famous Harbour Town Links in South Carolina, are flanked by enormous bunkers, waste areas and marshland. In 2015, for the first time, the field played one of the first two rounds at the nearby Plantation Course, an old parkland track upgraded by Rees Jones in 1998, then again by Davis Love and his brother Mark in 2019. Large creeks and lakes are a feature of the Plantation Course, along with dense sets of oak and cedar trees, but it has four par-fives and is easier than the Seaside Course. The final two rounds are on the Seaside
Type of player suited to the challenge The Seaside Course lends itself to precision players and those with a tidy short-game. It is not a links which can be overpowered. The Plantation Course is a parkland layout, creating an all-round test for the first 36 holes
Insight Twelve of the 15 editions of this event have been won by an American.

Course Earth Course, Jumeirah Golf Estates, Dubai, United Arab Emirates
Length 7,706 yards
Par 72– Four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview The Greg Norman-designed Earth Course was specifically built to be the venue for the European Tour's season finale. The Volvo Masters at Valderrama traditionally signalled the end of the European Tour campaign, but in 2009 the Race to Dubai started and the Jumeirah Golf Estates became home for the curtain-closer. The fairways are wide and there is little in the way of tree trouble. The 651-yard par-five 18th has a stream which splits the fairway in two, so much late drama can occur. The greens are large, undulating and quick, with plenty of run-off areas. The winning score has been between 14 and 25 under par in all 16 previous editions
Type of player suited to the challenge This long layout with wide fairways typically plays into the hands of big-hitters.
Insight Twelve of the last 13 winners of this event are Major champions.

Course Port Royal Golf Course, Southampton, Bermuda
Length 6,828 yards
Par 71– Three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Bermuda Championship was staged for the first time in 2019, played as an 'alternate' PGA Tour event in the same week as the WGC-HSBC Champions, but it has got increased status since. The course was designed by Robert Trent Jones on high ground overlooking the Atlantic Ocean in 1970, then renovated by Robert Rulewich in 2009. Most of the early holes are flat, before the course moves to undulating terrain and some ocean-side cliffs. The fairways are generous but the huge, undulating greens can cause plenty of three-putts. The course hosted the Grand Slam of Golf from 2009-2014 – an event for the year's Major winners. The 235-yard, par-three 16th is the signature hole and the toughest on the track, with a forced carry across the ocean.
Type of player suited to the challenge Comfort in a breeze seems the ticket to success this week, given the expected consistency of the wind,
Insight All six Bermuda Championship winners had missed at least two of their previous three cuts on the PGA Tour.

Most of the early holes are flat, before the course moves to undulating terrain and some ocean-side cliffs. The fairways are generous but the huge, undulating greens can cause plenty of three-putts. The course hosted the Grand Slam of Golf from 2009-2014 – an event for the year's Major winners. The 235-yard, par-three 16th is the signature hole and the toughest on the track, with a forced carry across the ocean.
Type of player suited to the challenge Comfort in a breeze seems the ticket to success this week, given the expected consistency of the wind,
Insight All six Bermuda Championship winners had missed at least two of their previous three cuts on the PGA Tour.

Course El Cardonal at Diamante, Los Cabos, Mexico
Length 7,425 yards
Par 72– Four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The PGA Tour staged this event at El Camaleon from 2007 to 2022, but the course became a LIV venue, meaning the PGA Tour moved the World Wide Technology Championship to El Cardonal in 2023. Tiger Woods is the course designer. The fairways are wide, there is a lack of rough, and the greens are large, but the terrain is extremely undulating and often starved of rain, so the paspalum fairways and greens typically run firm and fast
Type of player suited to the challenge Woods says that “angles of approach are very important”, but El Cardonal is an open and straightforward course for professionals, with wind as the only defence. With four par-fives and a driveable par-four, attacking big-hitters can make merry
Insight Fourteen of the 18 editions of this tournament have been won by an American.

Course Yas Links, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates
Length 7,425 yards
Par 72– Four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview Abu Dhabi GC hosted the Abu Dhabi Championship for 16 years, but Yas Links took over in 2022. The event was previously staged in January. The Kyle Phillips design, opened in 2010, has eight holes running directly along the coastline of the Arabian Gulf. Fescue grass rough adds to the 'desert links' feel, although the fairways are paspalum. Phillips also designed Kingsbarns and the Dundonald Links in Scotland, The Grove in London, Verdura in Sicily and Bernardus in the Netherlands. Yas Links closes with a 646-yard par-five.
Type of player suited to the challenge Links-lovers enjoy the Yas Links assignment, with fast-running fairways and firm greens. In calm conditions like those forecast this week, the targets off the tee are generous and birdies plentiful. Thomas Pieters and Victor Perez, two powerful Europeans, won the first two events at Yas Links.
Insight The three Yas Links champions finished in the top 20 for greens in regulation.

Course Woo Jeong Hills CC, Cheonan, South Korea
Length 7,367 yards
Par 71– Three par-five holes, 11 par-fours & four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Genesis Championship was a Korean Tour event staged at Jack Nicklaus Golf Club Korea from 2017 through 2023. The Korea Championship was a co-sanctioned DP World Tour and Korean Tour event in April 2023, also at JNGCK, then the Genesis Championship returned last year at the same venue. Woo Jeong Hills CC takes over as venue this time. The track hosted the Korea Open on the Asian Tour or Korean Tour from 2003 through 2019, then from 2021 through 2023. Thirteen of the Korea Open winning totals were with a single-digit under-par 72-hole score, which underlines the difficulty of the layout. This is a long, hilly track with water in play on most holes, including the 13th, which has an island green
Type of player suited to the challenge Woo Jeong Hills provides a severe ball-striking test, with finding greens in regulation the main challenge on the long, undulating terrain

Course Black Desert Resort Golf Course, Ivins, Utah.
Length 7,421 yards
Par 71– Three par-five holes, 11 par-fours & four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview Tom Weiskopf's final course design was Black Desert, which opened in May 2023. It hosted the inaugural Black Desert Championship last year. New sponsors mean a new tournament name for the second edition. The flat track is visually striking, with the black of lava rock alongside blue water and sky, white sand bunkers and pristine green grass. The fifth and 14th holes are short par-fours, a feature of Weiskopf designs, which will tempt the bigger hitters to drive the green. The par-three 17th is a spin-off of the famous Postage Stamp at Royal Troon, where Weiskopf won the Open in 1973. The greens are large and bentgrass.
Type of player suited to the challenge This open, desert layout encourages big-hitters to open their shoulders. Attacking players scrambling well should emerge on the leaderboard.
Key attribute Power

Course Delhi Golf Club in New Delhi, India.
Length 6,912 yards
Par 72– Four par-fives, ten par-fours & four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Delhi Golf Club has been in existence since the 1930s, but the championship course was redesigned in 2019 by Gary Player Design. The venue has hosted three previous DP World Tour events – the 2008 Indian Masters, the 2015 and 2016 Indian Opens – but the course has also staged numerous Asian Tour events. Much of the 2019 renovation was on the greens, which were enlarged and made MiniVerde Bermuda. The fairway bunkers were redesigned, a new irrigation system installed, and contouring added to the fairways. Since the changes, there have been four Asian Tour events at Delhi GC – the 2021 MP Cup, the 2022 and 2023 DGC Open, and the 2024 Vishwa Samudra Open. This is a tight, tree-lined track.
Type of player suited to the challenge Delhi GC remains an extreme test of precision, particularly from the tee, and only the most accurate of drivers can be confident of succeeding on the firm, fast terrain.
Insight Anirban Lahiri has won four of the last 19 tournaments at Delhi GC.

Course: Club de Campo Villa de Madrid
Course Details:
Course Club de Campo Villa de Madrid in Madrid, Spain
Length 7,112 yards 
Par 71 – three par-fives, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview There have been 16 DP World Tour events at Club de Campo Villa de Madrid since 1990, including the last five Spanish Opens. It is an undulating tree-lined track with small greens that offers fine views of the Spanish capital. It is a short course with four par-fours of less than 420 yards but the three par-fives should create few issues. The fairways are Bermuda grass while the greens are bentgrass with meadowgrass mix.
Type of player suited to the challenge This is not a difficult course by DP World Tour standards and it does not take much to overwhelm it. The short par-fours and trio of par-fives are there for the taking for players of this calibre.A hot putter is therefore a prerequisite for success and tip the balance in what should be another week of low-scoring at the Madrid venue.
Key attribute Touch/Putting
Insight Home players have won five of the last six Spanish Opens

Course Yokohama Country Club, Yokohama, Japan.
Length 7,315 yards 
Par 71 – two par-fives, 13 par-fours, three par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Yokohama Country Club is a 36-hole complex consisting of a West and East course. The West course hosted the 2018 Japan Open, won by Yuki Inamori, and two Yokohama Minato Championships in 2023 and 2024 – won by Keita Nakajima and Ren Yonezawa – but a composite course will be played this week that will include two holes from the East. The layout consists of tree-lined fairways with few bunkers and the greens are bentgrass. Both par-fives on the front nine, the 536-yard fourth and the 529-yard six, can be reached in two and they should be attacked by players of this calibre. There are two par-fours on in the inward nine – the 11th and 14th – measuring in excess of 500 yards. This is the first time the course has been used for this tournament, formerly known as the Zozo Championship, which was played five times at Narashino Country Club and once, during the Covid-19 pandemic, at Sherwood Country Club in California. It has moved forward three weeks in the schedule from last year.
Type of player suited to the challenge With few statistics available for the new course, it is difficult to summarise what qualities will be needed, particularly as it is not exactly the same layout as that used for the three previous Japanese Tour tournaments. Some of the tee shots look to be pretty tight, so finding the fairways and avoiding the trees could be a passport to success and strong course management will also be an asset.
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Four of the six winners of this tournament have been American

The short par-fours and trio of par-fives are there for the taking for players of this calibre.A hot putter is therefore a prerequisite for success and tip the balance in what should be another week of low-scoring at the Madrid venue.

Course Country Club of Jackson in Mississippi, USA
Length 7,461 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Country Club of Jackson made its PGA Tour debut in 2014. The most prestigious events held previously were the Mississippi State Amateur and the Southern Junior. Country Club of Jackson, tree-lined and traditional, has one par-five which is comfortably reachable in two shots (the third), but the other three (the fifth, 11th and 14th) are long and relatively difficult. Slick greens are a strength of Jackson, with Champions UltraDwarf Bermuda grass making for immaculate putting surfaces, with tight lies around them. Scoring has been low, though, with the winning score between 16 and 23 under par every year
Type of player suited to the challenge Ryan Armour and Cameron Champ – back-to-back winners in 2017 and 2018 – are the chalk and cheese of golfing profiles. There is clearly more than one way to skin this cat, but eight of the 11 Sanderson Farms champions finished in the top ten for greens in regulation, so that seems the most important statistic
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight Seven of the 11 Sanderson Farms champions were PGA Tour maidens

Course: Old Course at St Andrews; Carnoustie Golf Links; Kingsbarns Golf Links
Course Details:
Course(s) Scotland, with two rounds at St Andrews (Fife) and one each at Carnoustie (Angus) and Kingsbarns (Fife)
Length St Andrews 7,318 yards; Carnoustie 7,402 yards; Kingsbarns 7,227 yards – All Par 72 
Course type Links
Course overview This Pro-Am features two rounds at St Andrews and one each at Carnoustie and Kingsbarns. There is a round at each of the three links before a cut is made and the leaders tee up on Sunday at St Andrews. The host course, with its generous fairways and a few driveable par fours, is easy in calm conditions. But St Andrews can be much tougher in strong winds. It staged the Open in 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2022. Carnoustie is by far the most difficult course – the long, demanding monster brought many players to their knees in the 1999, 2007 and 2018 Opens. Kingsbarns is the shortest venue and the only one with four par-fives, so big-hitters have an opportunity to make hay. All three tracks are set up much easier than usual to give the amateurs a chance
Type of player suited to the challenge Those with banks of links experience from the British and Irish amateur scene, the Open and this event (which was first staged in 2001) will be best prepared. Links masters possess a range of shots to handle the wind and quirky terrain. A sound temperament is required to deal with the amateur element of this event, with rounds taking an age to complete
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight Seven of the last 11 Dunhill Links have been won by an Englishman

Course Black Course, Bethpage State Park, Farmingdale, New York.
Length 7,352 yards;
Par 70 – two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes 
Course type Parkland
Course overview Bethpage Black is hosting the Ryder Cup for the first time, but the famous public course has hosted three Majors and two FedEx Cup playoff events. Tiger Woods cruised to a three-shot victory in the 2002 US Open there, then Lucas Glover won the same tournament at Bethpage seven years later. The Barclays, a FedEx Cup playoffs gathering, was won by Nick Watney in 2012, then by Patrick Reed four years later. Brooks Koepka triumphed in the first US PGA at Bethpage in 2019, edging a thrilling duel with Dustin Johnson. AW Tillinghast was the main architect of Bethpage, but Rees Jones renovated the layout in 2015. There is a sign at the first tee reading: WARNING The Black Course Is An Extremely Difficult Course Which We Recommend Only For Highly Skilled Golfers. The vast layout usually has long rough, but American captain Keegan Bradley is in charge of the set-up for the Ryder Cup and has made it shorter, while getting the greens as fast as possible.
Type of player suited to the challenge Making putts on the poa annua greens may be the key to Ryder Cup success, with the course set-up being easier than is typical for Bethpage Majors.
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight USA have won only six of the last 19 Ryder Cups

Course Golf de Saint-Nom-La-Breteche, Paris, France.
Length 6,977 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Le Golf National, where Europe claimed a Ryder Cup triumph in 2018, has hosted this tournament for all but two years since 1991, but it is undergoing renovation work so this year's Open de France moves across Paris to Golf de Saint-Nom-La-Breteche, which last hosted it when Seve Ballesteros was the champion in 1982. While an unfamiliar test for this generation of players, it did stage the Trophee Lancome, which was a regular European Tour event between 1970 and 2003. The course contains tree-lined fairways but they are wide enough to ensure a decent margin for error off the tee. Deep bunkers are a prominent characteristic and the sloping greens should also provide plenty of food for thought on a course that was designed by Fred Hawtree in 1959.
Type of player suited to the challenge The fairways are quite wide for a tree-lined track so there is a chance to let rip off the tee, but the sloping greens will have to be managed well.
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Just two of the last 15 Open de France winners have been English

Course Silverado Resort and Spa (North Course), Napa, California.
Length 7,138 yards
Par 72 –four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Silverado returned to the Tour schedule in 2014, hosting the frys.com Open for two years, which became the Safeway Open from 2016-2020, then the Fortinet Championship from 2021-2023, with Procore the latest sponsors last year. Silverado is a resort course, but the fairways are reasonably tight, the rough is fairly thick, and tall trees line the cut and prepared, while the small poa annua greens are full of undulation. All the dancefloors are guarded by heavy bunkering, closely-mown run-off areas or water.
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a classic tree-lined assignment, where finding the fairways is essential, then accuracy is required to small targets on approach.
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Six of the last nine Silverado champions have been from California

Course West Course, Wentworth Club, Virginia Water, Surrey.
Length 7,267 yards
Par 72 –four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The legendary Harry Colt-designed West Course at Wentworth has been the host venue for the PGA Championship since 1984 and also staged the World Match Play from 1964 to 2007. In 2019, the event moved from its usual May slot to September. The course has been extensively remodelled over recent years (a project started in 2005) by long-time Wentworth member and seven-times Match Play champ Ernie Els. Prior to the 2017 edition, every green was stripped of its turf and re-seeded with creeping bentgrass. All the putting surfaces had a sub-air system installed to make them firmer and faster. Tall trees line the fairways, many of which are doglegs, so it is a demanding driving course. The layout closes with two par-fives – the 610-yard 17th and the 523-yard 18th, a classic risk-reward hole featuring a greenside water hazard
Type of player suited to the challenge Strong driving is demanded to find the tree-lined fairways. Long-game accuracy combined with short-game wizardry is typically the ticket to success at Wentworth.
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seven of the last 16 BMW PGA Championships have been won by an Englishman

Course The K Club in Straffan, County Kildare, Ireland.
Length 7,441 yards
Par 72 –four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Irish Open was played at Royal County Down, Northern Ireland, last year, but moves back south of the border this time. The K Club has two 18-hole courses – the Palmer North Course and the Palmer South Course. The Palmer North Course, which is being used this week, was designed by Arnold Palmer and opened in 1991. It staged the European Open on the European Tour from 1995 to 2003, and again in 2005. It also hosted the Ryder Cup in 2006, when Europe won 18.5-9.5, then the Irish Open in 2016 and 2023. It is a parkland layout with water in play on 15 holes, the River Liffey meandering through the course. There are four par-fives, with three of them on the back nine. The 548-yard par-five 18th hole provides a last-gasp eagle opportunity. Three of the four par-threes are 188 yards or shorter. Long par-fours make up the bulk of the course yardage - the second hole (412 yards) is the shortest of them. The 434-yard par-four seventh is the toughest hole on the track. The fairways are generous and receptive, but thick rough is waiting to punish the errant, and mature trees frame the fairways.
Type of player suited to the challenge Churning out greens in regulation has been the key to success at the K Club and that seems even more the case this year given the wind in the forecast and the length of the rough
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight The last six Irish Open winners were not from the UK or Ireland

Long par-fours make up the bulk of the course yardage - the second hole (412 yards) is the shortest of them. The 434-yard par-four seventh is the toughest hole on the track. The fairways are generous and receptive, but thick rough is waiting to punish the errant, and mature trees frame the fairways.
Type of player suited to the challenge Churning out greens in regulation has been the key to success at the K Club and that seems even more the case this year given the wind in the forecast and the length of the rough
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight The last six Irish Open winners were not from the UK or Ireland

Course Crans-sur-Sierre Golf Club, Crans Montana, Switzerland
Length 6,823 yards
Par 70 - three par-fives; ten par-fours; five par-threes
Time difference Switzerland is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview There has been a Tour event staged at Crans every year since 1972, apart from the 2020 Covid cancellation. A Seve Ballesteros redesign in 1999 made it tougher, creating lots of run-off areas around the greens. The sixth and seventh are driveable par-fours. Small, firm, unreceptive greens are the main problem for the competitors, although they are aided by the fact that the ball flies further than normal at the altitude. Most fairways are tree-lined and undulating
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green and course-management have been the keys to success. The honours board is full of precise operators like Miguel Angel Jimenez, Thomas Bjorn (twice), Richie Ramsay and Matthew Fitzpatrick (twice)
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Only two of the last nine European Masters were won by a DPWT maiden

Course Brabazon Course, The Belfry, Sutton Coldfield, England
Length 7,356 yards
Par 72 – three par-fives; 12 par-fours; three par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Brabazon Course hosted the Ryder Cup in 1985, 1989, 1993 and 2002. Several Tour events have been staged at The Belfry, including the International Open (2000-2003), the British Masters (2006, 2007, 2008, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024), and UK Championship (2020). The 2021 British Challenge (Challenge Tour) was also at the Brabazon. There is more water on the layout than just about any inland course in the British Isles, so scoring can be volatile and the front nine, in particular, demands accuracy. The back nine is more open although heavily bunkered
Type of player suited to the challenge Strong ball-strikers have traditionally thrived at this venue, which features a series of long, punishing par-fours
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Three of the last five winners of DP World Tour events at The Belfry have been Danish

Course East Lake Golf Club, Atlanta, Georgia.
Length 7,440 yards
Par 70 – Two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview East Lake is the curtain-closer to the PGA Tour season, with the most successful performers of the year ending their Stateside campaign in the Tour Championship. East Lake has hosted this event in 1998, 2000, 2002 and from 2004 onwards. The famous course was designed by Tom Bendelow and Donald Ross, but Rees Jones took charge of a redesign in 1994 and at the end of 2007 (he switched all greens from bentgrass to Bermuda), and an even more extensive redesign took place before last year's Tour Championship, with bunkers relocated and reshaped, trees removed, mounds added, and all 18 greens revised, with many much larger and more undulating than before. Andrew Green took charge of that project. The par changed from 70 to 71 for 2024, with the 14th playing as a par five, but that has been made a par four for this edition, meaning the overall par is back to 70.  A SubAir drainage system means firm, fast greens are almost certain. A handicap system was in place from 2019 through 2024, meaning the FedEx Cup leader started with a two-shot Tour Championship lead, and some players started ten shots behind, but that has been scrapped this year, so everyone starts on the same score
Type of player suited to the challenge Finding fairways has traditionally been extremely difficult at East Lake. The course was dramatically redesigned prior to last year's event, so previous course form counts for less, but strong ball-striking remains the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight The last seven FedEx Cup champions finished eighth or better in one of the preceding playoff events

Length 7,011 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Copenhagen is one hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview This is the first time the Danish Championship has been hosted by Fureso Golf Club and the tree-lined track should provide a challenge that can be overcome by strong course management. It was opened in 1974 but underwent a renovation by Tom Mackenzie in 2015. There are 27 holes on the complex so two of the established halves will be utilised. Water comes into play on several holes while the greens are bentgrass and the fairways fescue.
Type of player suited to the challenge This will be a new test for the vast majority of the field and the emphasis could be on strong approach play. The greens undulate, so finding the right areas should be key as well as proficiency with the putter.
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Caves Valley Golf Club, Owings Mills, Baltimore, Maryland.
Length 7,601 yards
Par 70 – two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Copenhagen is one hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Caves Valley was opened in 1991 and this will be the second time it has hosted this tournament following Patrick Cantlay's playoff victory over Bryson DeChambeau in 2021. The top 50 players in the FedEx Cup rankings are eligible to tee it up this week, although Sepp Straka has withdrawn from the event due to personal reasons. Those in the top 30 on Sunday night will head to next week's Tour Championship. The Tom Fazio design has, also hosted the 2002 US Senior Open, in which Don Pooley beat Tom Watson after a five-hole playoff, and the Senior Players Championship in 2017, which was won by Scott McCarron. This exclusive club takes in rolling hills, pastures, woods and wetlands and is characterised by tight fairways and narrow greens, many of which are multi-tiered. Length off the tee should ensure short approach shots but fairway bunkers lie in wait for the errant. Wind could also be a factor in the closing holes, which include a par-five 16th that measures the best part of 600 yards. The course has been lengthened to 7,601 yards with the 525-yard second that plays as a par five for members being a par four this week. Players will putt on bentgrass greens and the dramatic elevation changes could play havoc with yardage calculations. There is the promise that the rough will be thick as well with organisers conscious that both Cantlay and DeChambeau carded four-round totals of 27-under-par last year.
Type of player suited to the challenge Length off the tee was a big benefit in 2021, but those who ended up at the top of the leaderboard all putted superbly, so that could make the difference.
Key attribute Putting

Course Trump International Golf Links, Balmedie, Aberdeenshire, Scotland
Length 7,439 yards
Par 72 – four par-five holes, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Links
Course overview The Trump International Golf Links was designed by Martin Hawtree and opened in 2012. It is hailed as a modern links course which is carved between huge dunes up and down the Aberdeenshire coastline. Many of the tee shots are elevated and blind but the fairways are pretty generous. The traditional defence of rough awaits those errant from the tee and while this is the first time the track has been used on the DP World Tour, it has hosted the PGA Seniors Championship for the last three years, with the 2025 won by Bo Van Pelt last week.
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a new test for everyone but there will be a premium on finding fairways and those with greater knowledge of the demands of links golf have an advantage. A low ball flight to help nullify the influence of any wind gusts should also help
Key attribute Accuracy

Course TPC Southwind, Memphis, Tennessee
Length 7,243 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Playoff format This is the first of three FedEx Cup playoff events. The top 50 in the standings will progress to next week's BMW Championship, before the top 30 qualify for the season-ending Tour Championship. 
Course records - 72 holes 258 John Cook (1996) 18 holes 61 Jay Delsing (1993), Bob Estes (2001), Tom Lewis (2020), Justin Rose (2023)
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Ron Prichard-designed TPC Southwind has hosted a PGA Tour event since 1989, but the track had an extensive overhaul after the 2004 St Jude Classic. It has been perennially ranked as one of the most difficult on the PGA Tour since. The final St Jude Classic was in 2018, then the WGC-St Jude Invitational was at Southwind from 2019 through 2021, before the first FedEx St Jude Championship in 2022. The track has zoysia-grass fairways
Type of player suited to the challenge This tight, fiddly, dangerous venue demands strong, accurate ball-striking. Finding greens in regulation is the main challenge
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Sedgefield Country Club, Greensboro, North Carolina
Prize money $7.9m ($1.368m to the winner)
Length 7,131 yards
Par 70 – two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes 258 Henrik Stenson (2017), JT Poston (2019) 18 holes 59 Brandt Snedeker (2018)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Donald Ross created this layout in 1926 and it was redesigned by Kris Spence in 2007. Sedgefield has hosted from 2008 onwards, returning to the schedule after a 32-year absence, with winning scores of 20 under par or better in seven of the last eight years. The greens are small and undulating, with plenty of run-off areas around them, so quality scrambling is required
The story of last year Lucas Glover won the first of two PGA Tour titles in a fabulous fortnight, defeating Byeong Hun An by two shots
Type of player suited by challenge There are five relatively difficult holes at Sedgefield – the par fours at 11, 14 and 18, and the two long par threes (seven and 12) – but the rest of the track is straightforward. The champion is likely to have spent four days regularly rolling in birdie putts
Key attribute Touch/putting

The greens are small and undulating, with plenty of run-off areas around them, so quality scrambling is required
The story of last year Lucas Glover won the first of two PGA Tour titles in a fabulous fortnight, defeating Byeong Hun An by two shots
Type of player suited by challenge There are five relatively difficult holes at Sedgefield – the par fours at 11, 14 and 18, and the two long par threes (seven and 12) – but the rest of the track is straightforward. The champion is likely to have spent four days regularly rolling in birdie putts
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course TPC Twin Cities, Blaine, Minnesota
Length 7,431 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 260 Lee Hodges (2023) 18 holes 62 Scott Piercy, Bryson DeChambeau, Matthew Wolff, Lucas Glover (all 2019)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Twin Cities was designed by Arnold Palmer and opened in 2000. The first 3m Open was held there in 2019 while the track had previously staged a Champions Tour event where scores were low. The course was extended and fairways were narrowed but the test cannot be regarded as demanding for Tour professionals. Water comes into play on half of the holes, while the greens are large and undulating.
Story of last year Lee Hodges fired an opening 63 to take the tournament by the scruff of the neck and secured a seven-shot victory
Weather forecast Hot and sunny for three days and players may be fortunate enough to avoid the expected late rain on Sunday
Type of player suited to the challenge Big hitters have won at Twin Cities, but smart play on and around the greens can be the difference on a straightforward track
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course Dunluce Course, Royal Portrush Golf Club, Portrush, County Antrim, Northern Ireland
Prize money $17m ($3.1m to the winner)
Length 7,346 yards
Par 71 Three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 156-player field, top 70 cut.
Course winners taking part Shane Lowry
Course type Links
Course overview Royal Portrush hosted the Open in 1951, but Northern Irish golf fans had to wait until 2019 for the tournament to return there. Englishman Max Faulkner won the 1951 Open, then Shane Lowry led the field a merry dance six years ago, cruising to a six-shot triumph. Lowry reached 15 under par, but only 29 players in the 156-man field finished under par. Designed by Harry Colt in 1929, this undulating track looks out across the Atlantic Ocean, with fairways nestling in natural valleys.  There are lots of forced carries and doglegs, and the thick rough, with patches of heather, is penal. Small greens are protected by grassy mounds. There are not many bunkers, but this is a tough, fair links test. The 1993 and 2014 Amateur Championship was staged there, as well as the 2012 Irish Open on the DP World Tour.
Weather forecast Benign by Open standards. Relatively calm all week. Cloudy for the most part, with sporadic short, light showers.
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course Old Greenwood, Tahoe Mountain Golf Club, Truckee, California
Length 7,480 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes +50 Erik van Rooyen 18 holes +22 Kyle Reifers, Chad Campbell
Course winner taking part Chez Reavie
Format The Modified Stableford scoring system was introduced for the first time in 2012, allocating points based on the number of shots taken at each hole. Albatross = eight points; eagle = five; birdie = two; par = zero; bogey = minus one; double-bogey or worse = minus three
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Jack Nicklaus-designed Old Greenwood has hosted since 2020. It usually plays as a par 72, but the tenth hole is a par-four for the Tour professionals. Balls fly far at the altitude (almost 6,000 feet). The second shot is usually more difficult than the first – with water hazards and deep bunkers guarding the fast, severely undulating greens
Weather forecast Sunny, warm and calm throughout
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate approach shots are always essential on Jack Nicklaus-designed layouts but the greens at Old Greenwood are notoriously difficult and a sharp short-game may be even more important
Key attribute Touch

Course overview The Jack Nicklaus-designed Old Greenwood has hosted since 2020. It usually plays as a par 72, but the tenth hole is a par-four for the Tour professionals. Balls fly far at the altitude (almost 6,000 feet). The second shot is usually more difficult than the first – with water hazards and deep bunkers guarding the fast, severely undulating greens
Weather forecast Sunny, warm and calm throughout
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate approach shots are always essential on Jack Nicklaus-designed layouts but the greens at Old Greenwood are notoriously difficult and a sharp short-game may be even more important
Key attribute Touch

Course TPC Deere Run, Silvis, Illinois
Length 7,289 yards
Par 71 - three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 257 Michael Kim (2018) 18 holes 59 Paul Goydos (2010)
Course type Parkland
Course overview The John Deere Classic has been held at TPC Deere Run since 2000. The track, crafted by DA Weibring in 1999, is one of the easiest the Tour stars face all season. The first and second holes are extremely simple, so there is scope for a fast start, and this is a birdiefest where only the hottest of putters will contend
Story of last year Sepp Straka needed a birdie at his final hole for a 59, but found water with his approach. The resulting double-bogey meant a two-shot victory
Weather forecast A mixture of sunshine and cloud. Warm throughout, calm for round one, with moderate breezes from Friday onwards
Type of player suited to the challenge Steve Stricker, awesome with wedge in hand and one of the best putters in the world, won this event three times in a row before Zach Johnson, a player of a similar ilk, ended that streak. The John Deere lends itself to low scoring, but three breezy days should check scoring a little this time
Key attribute Touch

Length 7,347 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Eichenried has hosted this event 27 times, most recently from 1997 to 2011, then 2013, 2015, 2017 and from 2019 onwards. The flat, straightforward layout, with four par-fives reachable in two shots, has staged 11 rounds of 62
Story of last year Thriston Lawrence edged Joost Luiten to claim a fourth DP World Tour title
Weather forecast Cloudy and calm, with a chance of showers on Sunday. Temperatures peaking at 23C
Type of player suited to the challenge Eichenried is a doddle for professionals – attacking golf and a hot putter is the route to success
Key attribute Touch

Course TPC Deere Run, Silvis, Illinois
Length 7,289 yards
Par 71 - three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 257 Michael Kim (2018) 18 holes 59 Paul Goydos (2010)
Course type Parkland
Course overview The John Deere Classic has been held at TPC Deere Run since 2000. The track, crafted by DA Weibring in 1999, is one of the easiest the Tour stars face all season. The first and second holes are extremely simple, so there is scope for a fast start, and this is a birdiefest where only the hottest of putters will contend
Story of last year Sepp Straka needed a birdie at his final hole for a 59, but found water with his approach. The resulting double-bogey meant a two-shot victory
Weather forecast A mixture of sunshine and cloud. Warm throughout, calm for round one, with moderate breezes from Friday onwards
Type of player suited to the challenge Steve Stricker, awesome with wedge in hand and one of the best putters in the world, won this event three times in a row before Zach Johnson, a player of a similar ilk, ended that streak. The John Deere lends itself to low scoring, but three breezy days should check scoring a little this time
Key attribute Touch

Course Detroit Golf Club, Detroit, Michigan
Length 7,370 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Michigan is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The inaugural Rocket Mortgage Classic at the Donald Ross-designed Detroit GC took place in 2019 and it has been played every year since. Despite tree-lined fairways, heavy bunkering and small, poa annua greens, the course has proved a doddle for the Tour stars, with an average winning score of better than 22 under par
Story of last year Cam Davis won the Rocket for a second time, repelling a quartet of challengers by a shot
Type of player suited to the challenge Big-hitters have generally been successful in the Rocket Mortgage Classics – Bryson DeChambeau, Cam Davis and Tony Finau are on the honours board – but every Rocket winner has putted well in the week of their victory
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight The average strokes-gained putting ranking of the six Rocket winners is 11.8

Course PGA National Italy, Argentario Golf Club, Monte Argentario, Grosseto, Italy
Length 6,857 yards
Par 71 – four par-fives; nine par-fours; five par-threes
Time difference Italy is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview PGA National Italy, designed by David Mezzacane and Baldovino Dassu and opened in 2006, hosts the Italian Open for the first time this week. It staged the Italian Challenge Open on the Challenge Tour last September, with John Parry taking the title. Parry reached 18 under par, highlighting how this short course can be overwhelmed by professionals. Elevated tees and are a theme of the track. The front nine is fairly open, but the back nine has tight, tree-lined fairways
Weather forecast Sunny and hot, with light to moderate breezes
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy seems the key to success on a short, fiddly layout, with some breeze adding to the test
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Five of the last seven Italian Open winners finished 50th or worse in their preceding event

Course TPC River Highlands, Cromwell, Connecticut
Length 6,844 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview This Pete Dye creation has hosted this event since 1984 but it was redesigned in 1989, its name changing from TPC Connecticut to TPC River Highlands. Only the Phoenix Open attracts more spectators to a PGA Tour event than the Travelers
Type of player suited to the challenge All types of player have succeeded at this venue – short-hitters such as Olin Browne, Woody Austin, Ken Duke, Kevin Streelman, Russell Knox and Chez Reavie have won on the same terrain as bombers Phil Mickelson, Bubba Watson and Dustin Johnson. Hitting plenty of greens and putting well is the route to success. Birdies aplenty are up for grabs, so weak putters will be left behind
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight The last PGA Tour maiden to win the Travelers Championship was Ken Duke in 2013

Course Oakmont Country Club, Oakmont, Pennsylvania
Length 7,372 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Playoff format Two-hole aggregate playoff, with sudden-death from there if tied
Course type Parkland
Course overview Oakmont is widely regarded as one of the toughest tests in golf – a long par-70 with thick rough, 168 bunkers, firm fairways and lighting-fast greens – and any under-par 72-hole total should bank a healthy cheque. Oakmont is staging the US Open for a record tenth time – the first was in 1927 and the last was in 2016, when Dustin Johnson triumphed with a four-under-par total. Angel Cabrera won the 2007 US Open there with a five-over-par total, with 18 bogeys on his scorecard. The Henry Fownes design is renowned for its severe poa annua green complexes – the pace and undulations were once described as “almost impossible” by Rocco Mediate. Gil Hanse and Jim Wagner renovated the course in 2023 to prepare it for this championship, fitting a sub-air system under the greens. There are almost no trees left in the interior of the course, giving Oakmont an open, linksy feel
Story of last year Bryson DeChambeau produced an awesome up and down from a bunker at the 72nd hole to edge Rory McIlroy by a shot at Pinehurst
Type of player suited to the challenge Rain in the US Open build-up meant Oakmont went into US Open week playing soft, with further downpours arriving on Monday, so the track has become more appealing to long drivers. Finding fairways is crucial, though, given the length of the rough, and a sharp short-game essential on fast greens, dried by sub-air technology
Key attribute Power
Insight Seven of the last nine US Open winners had never won a Major before

Length 7,372 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Playoff format Two-hole aggregate playoff, with sudden-death from there if tied
Course type Parkland
Course overview Oakmont is widely regarded as one of the toughest tests in golf – a long par-70 with thick rough, 168 bunkers, firm fairways and lighting-fast greens – and any under-par 72-hole total should bank a healthy cheque. Oakmont is staging the US Open for a record tenth time – the first was in 1927 and the last was in 2016, when Dustin Johnson triumphed with a four-under-par total. Angel Cabrera won the 2007 US Open there with a five-over-par total, with 18 bogeys on his scorecard. The Henry Fownes design is renowned for its severe poa annua green complexes – the pace and undulations were once described as “almost impossible” by Rocco Mediate. Gil Hanse and Jim Wagner renovated the course in 2023 to prepare it for this championship, fitting a sub-air system under the greens. There are almost no trees left in the interior of the course, giving Oakmont an open, linksy feel

Course The International, Amsterdam, Netherlands
Length 6,914 yards
Par 71 - four par-fives; nine par-fours; five par-threes
Time difference The Netherlands is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The International has hosted the KLM Open twice before – the 2019 edition was won by Sergio Garcia, then Guido Migliozzi triumphed last year. The only significant change between those renewals was at the 11th hole, which used to be a 322-yard par-four, but was changed to a 127-yard par-three last year. Garcia won with an 18-under-par total on the par 72 layout, beating Nicolai Hojgaard by a shot. Ian Woosnam, working with Bruno Steensels, was the course architect. This links-like layout, with undulating fairways, works through dunes and swales. There are water hazards to punish the errant. The greens are large and full of humps and hollows
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a test of accuracy and strategy rather than power and players need to plot their way along one of the most undulating courses on the schedule, which will be windswept all week
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Only one of the last 16 editions of the Dutch Open has been won by a Tour maiden

Course: North Course, TPC Toronto at Osprey Valley
Course Details:
Course North Course, TPC Toronto at Osprey Valley, Caledon, Ontario, Canada
Length 7,389 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Canada is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Canadian Open competitors must adjust to another new venue as TPC Toronto makes its PGA Tour bow. It has hosted events on the Canadian Tour. Ian Andrew has led a recent restoration to toughen it up for the Tour, lengthening holes and tightening fairways at the professionals' landing spots, as well as adding chipping areas around the large greens. Most of the fairways remain generous, though, while the sixth and the tenth are potentially driveable par-fours
Story of last year Robert MacIntyre edged Ben Griffin by a shot at Hamilton Golf Club
Type of player suited to the challenge A sharp short-game may be the key to success at the North Course, with run-off areas around the large putting surfaces
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight Only one of the last nine Canadian Opens has been won by an American

Time difference Austria is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Gut Altentann Golf Club was the first European course designed by Jack Nicklaus and opened in 1989. The following year, it hosted the first of three Austrian Opens, which was won in a playoff by Bernhard Langer, but there have been no significant tournaments there since and the DP World Tour has not been in Austria since 2021. Water comes into play on 11 holes and the scenic backdrop dominates the tree-lined fairways that lead to large undulating greens. Both halves close with a par five, the only two on the course, and the 18th is regarded as the tougher assignment. However, it should not be a huge test for players of DPWT standard.

Course Colonial Country Club, Fort Worth, Texas
Length 7,289 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Texas is six hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The PGA Tour has held an event at Colonial since 1946. Ben Hogan won at Colonial five times, hence the track's nickname 'Hogan's Alley'. Gil Hanse renovated the course prior to last year's edition. There are only two par-fives – the first and the 11th – and the latter is a whopper at 639 yards. In-play punters should be aware that the first two holes on the course are the easiest, immediately followed by the three most difficult. The layout has been lengthened through the years but remains much more of a test of accuracy over power
Story of last year Davis Riley turned the event into a procession, winning by five shots
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy has always been crucial at Colonial – a straight hitter putting well will probably win. Heavy rough and numerous creeks are waiting to punish the errant, while the bentgrass greens are small
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Fourteen of the last 20 winners ranked inside the top five for putting average

Course Rinkven International Golf Club, Antwerp, Belgium
Length 6,940 yards
Par 71 - two par-fives; 13 par-fours; three par-threes
Time difference Belgium is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The DP World Tour returned to Belgium after an 18-year absence with the inaugural Belgian Knockout at Rinkven International in 2018 - a strokeplay event for two rounds before a series of nine-hole matches - then there was a repeat in 2019. The tournament returned as a 72-hole strokeplay event in 2022 and has been there every year since. The 2005 European Amateur Championship was staged at Rinkven, as well as the 2010 Telenet Trophy on the Challenge Tour

Course Quail Hollow Club, Charlotte, North Carolina
Length 7,626 yards
Par 71 – three par-five holes; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Quail Hollow staged a PGA Tour event from 2003 through 2016, starting off as the Wachovia Championship, becoming the Quail Hollow Championship, then the Wells Fargo Championship. The Wells Fargo moved to Eagle Point in 2017, the year that Quail Hollow staged the US PGA, which was won by Justin Thomas. Quail Hollow hosted the Wells Fargo from 2018 through 2021, then TPC Potomac took over in 2022 as Quail Hollow was needed for the Presidents Cup, which saw a 17.5-12.5 USA victory. The course was used for the 2023 and 2024 Wells Fargo. This will be the first Quail Hollow US PGA in May as the 2017 event was held in August. The track was entirely Bermuda grass in 2017 and had lightning-fast greens, but has since switched to a new custom perennial ryegrass blend for the fairways and TifEagle Bermuda for the greens, a move completed prior to the 2024 Wells Fargo. Quail Hollow was laid out by George Cobb in 1961, tweaked by Arnold Palmer in 1986 and redesigned by Tom Fazio in 1997, 2003, 2016 and 2023. Low scores are hard to come by, although Rory McIlroy has had a 61 and a 62 there. The start and the finish of the course are the toughest stretches, with scoring holes from seven through 15. The closing three holes, known as the Green Mile, are particularly difficult.
Type of player suited to the challenge Strong drivers have traditionally thrived at Quail Hollow – a long par-71 demanding solid ball-striking from tee to green
Key attribute Power
Insight Twenty three of the last 39 US PGA champions were breaking their Major duck

Course Wissahickon Course, Philadelphia Cricket Club, Flourtown, Pennsylvania
Length 7,119 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Pennsylvania is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview This tournament has new sponsors, having previously been the Wells Fargo Championship. Quail Hollow is the traditional venue for the event, but it is staging the US PGA this year. The Wissahickon Course at Philadelphia Cricket Club, designed by A.W Tillinghast, has stepped in as a replacement. Keith Foster led a renovation which was completed in 2014. The course hosted the Senior Players Championship in 2016 and Bernhard Langer won a windy event with a one over par total. A creek runs through the course. Well-guarded bentgrass greens are difficult to find. Fast, undulating dancefloors
Type of player suited to the challenge The fairways are fairly generous, but the approach shots are extremely difficult, so precise irons are the key to success. The best wedge player may win
Key attribute Accuracy

Course The Dunes Golf and Beach Club, Myrtle Beach, South Carolina
Length 7,347 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference South Carolina is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The largely tree-lined Robert Jones Senior designed Dunes Club made its PGA Tour debut last year. It runs alongside Lake Singleton and water comes into play on most holes. The average winning total in the six editions of the Champions Tour event staged there (Senior Tour Championship, 1994 through 1999) was 12 under par, but Chris Gotterup romped to 22 under par 12 months ago
Type of player suited to the challenge Generous fairways and wide greens should result in low scoring, so expect an attacking powerhouse to prevail, as was the case last year

Course Regnum Carya, Antalya, Turkey
Length 7,220 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Peter Thomson-designed Regnum Carya hosted this event from 2016 through 2018, with Thorbjorn Olesen winning the first Turkish Airlines Open there, then Justin Rose claiming back-to-back triumphs in the other two editions. This event has not taken place since the last renewal at the Montgomerie Maxx Royal in 2019. There was also a Challenge Tour event at Regnum in 2010 – won by Charlie Ford. One million heather plants were added when this course was crafted, with heathland tracks like Walton Heath and Sunningdale the inspiration behind the venue. The course runs through a pine forest, with tight fairways and water coming into play on eight holes. The greens are large, fast and undulating. The tenth plays as a par-five for the members and is the toughest hole on the course
Type of player suited to the challenge Given the benign weather, 20 under par may be needed to win this, with accurate players likely to set up numerous birdie opportunities
Key attribute Accuracy

Course TPC Louisiana, Avondale, Louisiana
Length 7,425 yards
Par 72 – four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Field 80 teams of two The cut Top 33 teams and ties qualify for round three
Format A four-day team event. The pairs play best-ball competition (both players play own ball, with only the best score on each hole counting towards the 72-hole total) in rounds one and three, and alternate-shot competition (one player in the team hits a shot, then the other player, until the ball is holed) in rounds two and four
Course records (individual strokeplay) 72 holes 266 Justin Rose (2015) 18 holes 62 Ben Martin (2014)
Course records (pairs play) 72 holes 258 Nick Hardy & Davis Riley (2023) 18 holes best-ball 59 Patrick Cantlay & Xander Schauffele (2022); 18 holes alternate-shot 63 Xander Schauffele & Patrick Cantlay (2023), Adam Hadwin & Nick Taylor (2023), Chad Ramey & Martin Trainer (2024)
Time difference Louisiana is six hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Designed by Pete Dye in 2003, it made its PGA Tour debut in 2005. The Zurich became a pairs event in 2017. There was no event in 2020. The rough is light and birdies are plentiful
Type of player suited to the challenge Greens in regulation is the theme which runs through the victors at TPC Louisiana, with finding the right sections of heavily undulating greens the key to going low. Strong winds seem set to make the foursomes rounds difficult. Ryder Cup, Presidents Cup and QBE Shootout pairs form should be studied
Key attribute Accuracy
