Course: Blackstone Course, Mission Hills Resort Haikou
Course Details:
Course Blackstone Course, Mission Hills Resort Haikou, Hainan Island, China
Length 7,711 yards
Par 72 – four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
When to bet By 11.30pm on Wednesday night
Time difference China is seven hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview A layout crafted by Schmit-Curley Design, led by Brian Curley, it hosted the 2011 World Cup of Golf – won by the American team of Matt Kuchar and Gary Woodland. The biennial World Celebrity Pro-Am, which was won by Haotong Li in 2016, also takes place at this venue. The Hainan Classic is a new DP World Tour event, starting this week. This natural layout has 147 bunkers. There is no rough, with jungle and black lava rock waiting to punish the seriously errant. There are also some small lakes to avoid on the back nine. Two of the five par-fives are enormous – the 623-yard second and the 681-yard 13th
Type of player suited to the challenge Generous fairways will have the powerhouses licking their lips, particularly with some thundery showers set to keep the terrain soft
Key attribute Power

Course Club de Golf Chapultepec, Naucalpan, Mexico
Length 7,385 yards
Par 71 - three par-five holes, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Time difference Mexico City is seven hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Chapultepec hosted the WGC-Mexico Championship from 2017 through 2020, with players who have since become LIV golfers winning every event. The average winning score from those events (over 72 holes) was 17.25 under par. The tree-lined layout has tight, undulating fairways, but the venue is more than 7,800 yards above sea level, so balls fly further at the altitude. The par-threes are difficult, but the rest of the course serves up plenty of birdies. The fairways are kikuyu grass, but the greens are a mix of Poa and bentgrass
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green is required, given the narrow fairways, but the WGC winners at Chapultepec all had great scrambling stats, hinting that short-game class is necessary for success
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course Augusta National, Augusta, Georgia
Length 7,555 yards
Par 72 - four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Georgia is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Home of the Masters since 1934, Augusta is the only Major venue which is used every season, typically in the spring. The 2020 (Covid-delayed) edition was in November. The course demands accurate iron shots to the fastest greens in golf. Avoiding three-putts is challenging given the severity of the undulations. Sound course management is required to find the easiest spots from which to putt. Three par-five holes – the second, 13th and 15th – have been lengthened over recent years, making them more difficult to reach in two shots. In the wake of Hurricane Helene last autumn, four greens were re-seeded and relaid, so there is potential for fresh pin positions. Trees were lost around the par-three 16th hole, but it presents the same challenge. The benign weather forecast probably means Augusta bigwigs will set the greens up as firm and fast as possible to keep a check on scoring
Type of player suited to the challenge Length is a significant advantage, with grass cut towards the tees and balls stopping quickly on generous fairways which are flanked by thin trees. Only two of the last 25 Masters have been won by a short-hitter (Mike Weir in 2003 and Zach Johnson in 2007). Powerhouses are able to attack the small targets with loft in hand. Accurate approaches to well guarded and undulating greens are the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight The last 15 Masters winners were inside the top 30 of the world rankings at the time of their victory

The benign weather forecast probably means Augusta bigwigs will set the greens up as firm and fast as possible to keep a check on scoring

Only two of the last 25 Masters have been won by a short-hitter (Mike Weir in 2003 and Zach Johnson in 2007). Powerhouses are able to attack the small targets with loft in hand. Accurate approaches to well guarded and undulating greens are the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Oaks Course, TPC San Antonio, San Antonio, Texas
Length 7,438 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes 
Time difference Texas is six hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Greg Norman-designed Oaks Course has been used since 2010. A Korn Ferry Tour event (TPC San Antonio Championship) was staged at the Oaks in July, 2020. Course changes before the 2018 edition made the set-up more generous, but accurate drivers have continued to thrive along the tree-lined fairways. The greens are large and undulating. There was no 2020 event due to the Covid pandemic
Type of player suited to the challenge Andrew Landry, Corey Conners (twice) and JJ Spaun have won this title in recent years with neat and tidy golf, but strong putting seems essential on these large, sloping greens
Key attribute Touch/putting
Insight Seven of the last ten winners have ranked inside the top five of the putting stats

Type of player suited to the challenge Andrew Landry, Corey Conners (twice) and JJ Spaun have won this title in recent years with neat and tidy golf, but strong putting seems essential on these large, sloping greens

Course Memorial Park Golf Course, Houston, Texas
Length 7,475 yards
Par 70 – three par-five holes, ten par-fours, five par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 264 Tony Finau (2022) 18 holes 62 Scottie Scheffler (2021), Tony Finau 2024)
Course winners taking part Tony Finau, Stephan Jaeger
Course type Parkland
Course overview Memorial Park has hosted the Houston Open since November, 2020 – an event won by Carlos Ortiz. Jason Kokrak won the event 12 months later, before both him and Ortiz departed the PGA Tour for LIV Golf. There was no Houston Open in 2023, then the event returned last year, switching from its November slot to a March one. The fairways are generous and there are only 21 bunkers on the course, but the greens are full of slope
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate approaches to elevated greens is the main challenge of Memorial Park. This is a second-shot course where finding the right sections of heavily undulating greens, as well as putting well, is the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight All four winners of the Houston Open at Memorial Park finished in the top five for strokes-gained when putting

Par 71 - four par-five holes; nine par-fours; five par-threes
When to watch Live on Sky Sports Main Event and Golf from 11.30am on Thursday
Time difference Florida is four hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Tough, tree-lined layout, hosting for the 24th time (there was no tournament in 2001 or 2020). The event was formerly known as the Tampa Bay Classic, Chrysler Championship, PODS Championship, Transitions Championship and Tampa Bay Championship
The story of last year Peter Malnati took advantage of a faltering Cameron Young, winning by two shots for a second PGA Tour title
Type of player suited to the challenge Straight hitting and churning out greens in regulation is the key to success, which is why the likes of KJ Choi, Retief Goosen, Jim Furyk, Kevin Streelman, John Senden and Adam Hadwin have flourished in the past. An early-late draw could be favourable over the first two rounds
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Sixteen of the 23 Valspar winners finished inside the top 15 for greens in regulation

Course Stadium Course, TPC Sawgrass, Ponte Vedra Beach, Florida
Length 7,352 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Florida is four hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Pete Dye design, which has hosted since 1982. The island-green 17th is one of the most famous holes in golf. Longer clubs are required on approach, but the small, contoured greens are more receptive. The 2020 event was cancelled after one round due to Covid
Type of player suited to the challenge Plodders traditionally succeeded at this venue in May, but the switch to March effectively lengthened the track and played into the hands of big-hitters
Key attribute Power
Insight The last 13 Sawgrass champions finished in the top 35 in their preceding tournament.

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: THE PLAYERS Championship
Course: Stadium Course, TPC Sawgrass
Yardage: 7,352

Justin Thomas
Ludvig Aberg
Sepp Straka
Will Zalatoris
Cam Davis
Jesper Svenson
Justin Rose
Aaron Rai

Notes:
Sunny, warm and calm for the first two days, but moderate breezes for the weekend
Lots of players on form took my interest this week, however a few trends do seem to stand out here:
14 in the last 16 winners had a AM/PM tee split
The last 5 winners ranked top10 in the world rankings: 2024: Scheffler 1; 2023: Scheffler 2; 2022: Smith 10; 2021: Thomas 3; 2019: McIlroy 6.
4 in the last 5 winners ranked 1st T2G
5/5 ranked top 10 APP
4/5 ranked outside top 35 putting, the other was Cam Smith 2022 ranking 1st
Experience needed, only 1 past champion in 30/40 years has won as a rookie.
A focus on T2G, OTT & APP
Ludvig Aberg finished 8th on debut last year and putted terribly ranking 57th. He is local resident here and knows this course very well. He looked good last week despite a disastrous 5/6 hole stretch on the Saturday that put his chances to bed. That was a very tough course and I can forgive him for that. Out of the top 5 in the world he’s playing the best golf for me and he fits all the trends with a Thursday morning tee time.
Sepp Straka has plenty of experience of sawgrass which is key, he also fits a lot of the trends including having a AM tee time. He’s been playing great golf this year ranking 16th DG and 13th OWGR, the numbers speak for themselves. You really have to be at the top of your game to win here and he couldn’t be playing any better.
Will Zalatoris game seems to be getting back into shape since his major back surgery. Last week at Arnies place he ranked 6th T2G, 4th OTT & 7th APP. For someone who has recently rebuilt his swing that is incredible SG stats, exactly what he will need to play well round here. He has a mixed record here, with best finish 21st in 2021 but I don’t think there is much emphasis on course history here given its unforgiveness especially down the stretch. He ranked 50th Putting in a field of 75 last week but it has to be noted those greens were baked and burnt in some places. R1 AM tee time
Aaron Rai is a ball striking master and has solidified his place on the PGA Tour as a real contender most if not every week. He has the game to do well here, as shown with T19 in 2023, T35 2024. Last week he ranked 9th T2G, 18th APP, 25th OTT. R1 AM Tee time.
Justin Rose caught my eye last week, he seems to be finding some nice form and will be wanting that to continue in this Ryder Cup year. A win here would most likely solidify that, he has a R1 PM tee time but I expect him to follow up his good finish last week.

Longer clubs are required on approach, but the small, contoured greens are more receptive. The 2020 event was cancelled after one round due to Covid

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: THE PLAYERS Championship
Course: Stadium Course, TPC Sawgrass
Yardage: 7,352

Justin Thomas
Ludvig Aberg
Sepp Straka
Will Zalatoris
Cam Davis
Jesper Svenson
Justin Rose
Aaron Rai

Notes:
Sunny, warm and calm for the first two days, but moderate breezes for the weekend
Lots of players on form took my interest this week, however a few trends do seem to stand out here:
14 in the last 16 winners had a AM/PM tee split
The last 5 winners ranked top10 in the world rankings: 2024: Scheffler 1; 2023: Scheffler 2; 2022: Smith 10; 2021: Thomas 3; 2019: McIlroy 6.
4 in the last 5 winners ranked 1st T2G
5/5 ranked top 10 APP
4/5 ranked outside top 35 putting, the other was Cam Smith 2022 ranking 1st
Experience needed, only 1 past champion in 30/40 years has won as a rookie.
A focus on T2G, OTT & APP
Ludvig Aberg finished 8th on debut last year and putted terribly ranking 57th. He is local resident here and knows this course very well. He looked good last week despite a disastrous 5/6 hole stretch on the Saturday that put his chances to bed. That was a very tough course and I can forgive him for that. Out of the top 5 in the world he’s playing the best golf for me and he fits all the trends with a Thursday morning tee time.
Sepp Straka has plenty of experience of sawgrass which is key, he also fits a lot of the trends including having a AM tee time. He’s been playing great golf this year ranking 16th DG and 13th OWGR, the numbers speak for themselves. You really have to be at the top of your game to win here and he couldn’t be playing any better.
Will Zalatoris game seems to be getting back into shape since his major back surgery. Last week at Arnies place he ranked 6th T2G, 4th OTT & 7th APP. For someone who has recently rebuilt his swing that is incredible SG stats, exactly what he will need to play well round here. He has a mixed record here, with best finish 21st in 2021 but I don’t think there is much emphasis on course history here given its unforgiveness especially down the stretch. He ranked 50th Putting in a field of 75 last week but it has to be noted those greens were baked and burnt in some places. R1 AM tee time
Aaron Rai is a ball striking master and has solidified his place on the PGA Tour as a real contender most if not every week. He has the game to do well here, as shown with T19 in 2023, T35 2024. Last week he ranked 9th T2G, 18th APP, 25th OTT. R1 AM Tee time.
Justin Rose caught my eye last week, he seems to be finding some nice form and will be wanting that to continue in this Ryder Cup year. A win here would most likely solidify that, he has a R1 PM tee time but I expect him to follow up his good finish last week.

Course Details:
Course Arnold Palmer's Bay Hill Club and Lodge, Orlando, Florida
Length 7,466 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference Florida is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Bay Hill has hosted a PGA Tour event every year since 1979. Arnold Palmer made significant changes to the track before the 2010 edition, so form prior is of little relevance. Water is in play on several holes and there are lots of doglegs
Weather forecast Sunny for the most part. Moderate breezes are expected on Thursday and Sunday, leaving Friday and Saturday as the best scoring days. Sunday also has the potential for some short showers
Type of player suited to the challenge There is plenty of water to trouble the competitors on this tough layout, as well as greenside run-off areas, putting a premium on accuracy and the ability to grind pars, especially with some wind in the forecast
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Seven of the last eight winners ranked inside the top ten for greens in regulation

Course Houghton Golf Club, Johannesburg, South Africa
Length 7,227 yards
Time difference South Africa is two hours ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview AM Copeland designed this classic track, which was opened in 1926, and it staged the South African Open eight times between 1951 and 1992. The South African PGA was played there from 1993 through 1999. And from 2000 through 2004, Houghton was the venue for the Alfred Dunhill Championship on the European Tour. The course was then sold, though, and completely changed by Jack Nicklaus and his team, opening in 2009. The only significant course form since the redesign is the Sunshine Tour Qualifying School in 2019 and 2020, and the Joburg Opens in 2022 and 2023. This is a short track, given it is played at altitude in the Highveld. Heavy bunkering, water hazards and undulating greens are the defence. The par was reduced from 71 to 70 last year, with the third hole becoming a par-four, rather than a five
Weather forecast A thundery build-up should be followed by sunny, warm and calm conditions for the tournament
Type of player suited to the challenge As with most Nicklaus designs, accurate iron-play is essential to greens heavily guarded by bunkers 
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Three of the last eight Joburg Opens have been won by a European

Course Details:
Course The Champion Course, PGA National Resort, Palm Beach Gardens, Florida
Length 7,167 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 144 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes 263 Austin Eckroat (2024) 18 holes 61 Brian Harman (2012), Matt Jones (2021)
Time difference Florida is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Champions, a course created by George Fazio and redesigned by Jack Nicklaus, is one of the toughest layouts on the PGA Tour. Type of player suited to the challenge The Champions, with heavy bunkering and plenty of water hazards, is a strong tee-to-green test where only accurate ball-striking will produce results
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Durban Country Club, Durban, South Africa
Length 6,780 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes 263 Ernie Els (2011) 18 holes 62 John Bland (1993)
Time difference South Africa is two hours ahead of UK and Ireland time
Course type Links
Course overview Established in 1922, Durban CC has a proud history, but course-form students should probably ignore previous results there. It has staged 16 South African Opens (the last was in 2005) and the 2022 Jonsson Workwear Open was a Challenge Tour event there, but a seven-month renovation from May, 2023, transformed the layout. The course runs alongside the Indian Ocean and plays as a links test, with undulating fairways flanked by sand dunes. Two short par-fives on the front nine represent eagle opportunities, while there are three driveable par-fours
Story of the last SA Open (2023) Dean Burmester triumphed by three shots at the Blair Atholl Estate
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a short links with soft fairways which should be overwhelmed by long drivers in the forecast calm
Key attribute Power

Course Vidanta Vallarta, Vallarta, Mexico
Length 7.436 yards
Par 71– four par-fives; nine par-fours; five par-threes
Field 144
Time difference Vallarta is six hours behind the UK & Ireland 
Course type Parkland
Course overview The flat Greg Norman design, opened in 2015 and Mexico Open host since 2022, has paspalum grass fairways and greens. The targets off the tee are generous, while the greens are large, but they are well protected by deep bunkers and have demanding undulations. There are a total of 106 bunkers and the seventh is a driveable par four measuring just 297 yards 
Story of last year Jake Knapp's even-par final round was enough to see him home by two shots from Finland's Sami Valimaki
Weather forecast A cloudy start is expected but it should be fine for the rest of the tournament with temperatures around 25C
Type of player suited to the challenge It is a pretty open track so big drivers will relish the chance to unleash their muscle, but it does get trickier the closer you get to the hole.
Key attribute Power
Insight The three past winners were ranked first, first and third in strokes-gained tee-to-green
Player: Harry Hall/ Akshay Bhatia m/ Michael Kim

Course Details:
Course Muthaiga Golf Club, Nairobi, Kenya
Prize money $2.5m ($425,000 to the winner)
Length 7,208 yards
Par 71– four par-fives; nine par-fours; five par-threes
Time difference Nairobi is three hours ahead of the UK & Ireland 
Course type Parkland
Course overview This is the fourth successive year Muthaiga has hosted the DP World Tour after previously being the venue when competition took place on the Challenge circuit. The course is characterised by tight fairways with little advantage to be had from length off the tee due to the altitude and the tall trees wide of the short grass can cause problems.
Type of player suited to the challenge Plotting a route around this tight track is the passport to success and hitting the fairways is likely to be key.
Key attribute Accuracy
Insight Six of the last eight Kenya Opens have been won by a margin of two shots or more

Course Torrey Pines South, San Diego, California
Length 7,765 yards
Par 72 - four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Time difference California is eight hours behind UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Torrey Pines South has hosted three rounds of the Farmers Insurance Open since 1968. The South, opened in 1957, was redesigned by Rees Jones in 2001 to make it tougher for the 2008 US Open, which was won by Tiger Woods. Jon Rahm won the US Open there in 2021. It is a severe test and showed its teeth in the Farmers Insurance Open last month. The Genesis Invitational is traditionally played at Riviera Country Club, but damage caused to that area by wildfires last month forced a late venue switch. The small greens on the South course are undulating and tricky
Type of player suited to the challenge Torrey Pines South looks set to play its full length, with rain-softened fairways making this a slog for short drivers. Power appears of great advantage
Key attribute Power
Key Stat Seven of the last eight Farmers Insurance Open winners at Torrey Pines have ranked inside the top five for strokes gained on approach

Course Jack Nicklaus Golf Club Korea, Incheon, South Korea

Course records - 72 holes 270 Seung-hyuk Kim (2017); 18 holes 63 Antoine Rozner (2023), Guido Migliozzi (2023)
Course type Parkland
Course overview This was a Korean Tour event staged at Jack Nicklaus Golf Club Korea from 2017 through 2023. The Korea Championship was a co-sanctioned DP World Tour and Korean Tour event at JNGCK in April last year. The 2015 Presidents Cup was at this venue, as well as the 2016 Asia-Pacific Amateur Championship. JNGCK is a Nicklaus-designed, urban, parkland venue, located near the coast. The fairways are generous, but approach shots challenging, with finding the right sections of undulating greens the most difficult task
Weather forecast Sunny and calm, with temperatures peaking at 22C
Type of player suited to the challenge With four par-fives reachable in two shots and two short par-fours, a powerhouse hitting accurate iron-shots should make merry in the forecast calm
Key attribute Accuracy

Post Tournament Notes: A good tournament where local knowledge is an advantage. It came down to a playoff between the two home favourites, Tom Kim & Byeong Hun An with the latter previeling. Tom Kim must be reeling after a lacklustre season and this showed after a dressing room meltdown and breaking a locker. A very good performance from Guido having been under some pressure to perform in order to obtain a PGA Tour card for next year. Course Accordia Golf Narashino Country Club, Chiba, Japan
Length 7,079 yards
Par 70 – three par-fives; ten par-fours; five par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The inaugural Zozo was staged at Narashino in 2019 – won by Tiger Woods – but the 2020 event was moved to Sherwood Country Club in California because of Covid travel issues. The last three Zozo Championships have been at Narashino. This is a tight, tree-lined layout with numerous doglegs, and water in play on five holes. There are two greens on each hole (only one of each will be used each day, with free drops off the other) and they are all relatively small
Type of player suited to the challenge Three of the best iron-players in the game – Tiger Woods, Hideki Matsuyama and Collin Morikawa – have triumphed at Narashino. Tee-to-green accuracy seems the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Details:
Course Real Club de Golf Sotogrande, San Roque, Andalucia, Spain
Length 7,101 yards
Par 72 – four par-five holes; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Real Club de Golf was designed by Robert Trent Jones in 1964 and restored by Roger Rulewich and David Fleury in 2016. The course hosted the European Tour Qualifying School Finals, in tandem with San Roque Club, in 1996, 1998, 1999, 2000 and 2001, taking over as Andalucia Masters venue last year. Real Club de Golf is open, exposed and undulating, with man-made lakes which give it the look of a Florida course, and the greens are fast
Type of player suited to the challenge The fairways are generous at this track, but the greens are well guarded, so accurate approach play seems the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

Course Details:
Course TPC Summerlin, Las Vegas, Nevada
Length 7,255 yards
Par 71 – three par-five holes; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview TPC Summerlin has been part of this event since 1992, but the tournament used to be played over several different tracks. Until 2004, it was a five-round affair which was called the Las Vegas Invitational and the Invensys Classic. Then, under the titles of the Michelin Championship (2004-05), the frys.com Open (2006-07) and the Justin Timberlake Open (2008-12), it became a four-round, 72-hole event. Prior to 2008, other courses were used in tandem with Summerlin, but after Timberlake became tournament host it has been staged exclusively at Summerlin. It is an easy course for professionals and always serves up a birdiefest
Type of player suited to the challenge Anyone arriving at Summerlin with a cold putter will struggle to stay the pace. Every hole bar the eighth is a solid birdie opportunity
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course Details:
Course Le Golf National, Saint-Quentin-en-Yvelines, France
Length 7,247 yards
Par 71 - three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Le Golf National is one of Europe's finest courses and it usually provides a stern test. The event used to be contested in the first week of July, but switched to autumn in 2019. The 2020 and 2021 editions were cancelled due to Covid. The course first hosted the French Open in 1991. It has been the venue since 2002. It staged the Ryder Cup in 2018 and the Olympics in August. The fairways are severely undulating, so precise tee-shots are required to keep balls on the cut and prepared. The rough is typically lush and penal, and several water hazards are lurking to punish the errant. The greens are large and pacy, with plenty of sharp slopes. Five of the last 11 champions topped the final leaderboard with a single-digit-under-par total
Type of player suited to the challenge Le Golf National is a tough track, demanding accuracy from tee to green, as the penalty for wide shots is often a watery grave. Support straight hitters
Key attribute Accuracy

Dan Bradbury was incredibly fortunate not go in the water on the 15th and somehow made a birdie and luck like that got him over the line for his first DPWT win.

Course Details:
Course Black Desert Resort Golf Course, Ivins, Utah
Length 7,371 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview Tom Weiskopf's final course design was Black Desert, which opened in May last year. It is hosting the inaugural Black Desert Championship. The flat track is visually striking, with the black of lava rock alongside blue water and sky, white sand bunkers and pristine green grass. The fifth and 14th holes are short par-fours, a feature of Weiskopf designs, which will tempt the bigger hitters to drive the green. The par-three 17th is a spin-off of the famous Postage Stamp at Royal Troon, where Weiskopf won the Open in 1973. The greens are large and bentgrass
Type of player suited to the challenge This open, desert layout encourages big-hitters to open their shoulders. Attacking players scrambling well should emerge on the leaderboard
Key attribute Power

Post Tournament Notes: An interesting course as a new addition to the PGA Tour schedule. I was under the impression prior to the Thursday that this would give some of those wayward drivers an advantage here, but I was wrong. These are large fairways but if you miss them you’re in big trouble due to the volcanic rock residing on the wasteland. This came to find out Henrick Norlander who was finished T8 but could have done better without trying to play out of this rock. Matt McCarty simply hit it longer and straighter than anyone else and made a few putts to win comfortably in the end.

Key attribute Accuracy

Course Details:
Course Black Desert Resort Golf Course, Ivins, Utah
Length 7,371 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Desert
Course overview Tom Weiskopf's final course design was Black Desert, which opened in May last year. It is hosting the inaugural Black Desert Championship. The flat track is visually striking, with the black of lava rock alongside blue water and sky, white sand bunkers and pristine green grass. The fifth and 14th holes are short par-fours, a feature of Weiskopf designs, which will tempt the bigger hitters to drive the green. The par-three 17th is a spin-off of the famous Postage Stamp at Royal Troon, where Weiskopf won the Open in 1973. The greens are large and bentgrass
Type of player suited to the challenge This open, desert layout encourages big-hitters to open their shoulders. Attacking players scrambling well should emerge on the leaderboard
Key attribute Power

Course Details:
Course The Country Club of Jackson, Jackson, Mississippi
Length 7,461 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes 
Course records - 72 holes 266 Sam Burns (2021) 18 holes 62 Roberto Castro (2015)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Country Club of Jackson made its PGA Tour debut in 2014. The most prestigious events held previously were the Mississippi State Amateur and the Southern Junior. Country Club of Jackson, tree-lined and traditional, has one par-five which is comfortably reachable in two shots (the third), but the other three (the fifth, 11th and 14th) are long and relatively difficult. Slick greens are a strength of Jackson, with Champions UltraDwarf Bermuda grass making for immaculate putting surfaces, with tight lies around them. Scoring has been low, though, with the winning score between 16 and 22 under par every year
Type of player suited to the challenge Ryan Armour and Cameron Champ – back-to-back winners in 2017 and 2018 – have completely contrasting golfing profiles. There is clearly more than one way to skin this cat, but eight of the ten Sanderson Farms champions finished in the top ten for greens in regulation, so that seems the most important statistic
Key attribute Accuracy

Course: Old Course at St Andrews; Carnoustie Golf Links; Kingsbarns Golf Links
Course Details:
Course St Andrews and Kingsbarns, Fife, Scotland, and Carnoustie, Angus, Scotland
Lengths St Andrews 7,318 yards; Carnoustie 7,394 yards; Kingsbarns 7,227 yards
Pars All 72
Course type Links
Course overview This Pro-Am features two rounds at St Andrews and one each at Carnoustie and Kingsbarns. There is a round at each of the three links before a cut is made and the leaders tee up on Sunday at St Andrews. The host course, with its generous fairways and a few driveable par fours, is easy in calm conditions. But St Andrews can be much tougher in strong winds. It staged the Open in 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2022. Carnoustie is by far the most difficult course – the long, demanding monster brought many players to their knees in the 1999, 2007 and 2018 Opens. Kingsbarns is the shortest venue and the only one with four par-fives, so big-hitters have an opportunity to make hay. All three tracks are set up much easier than usual to give the amateurs a chance
Type of player suited to the challenge Those with banks of links experience from the British and Irish amateur scene, the Open and this event (which was first staged in 2001) will be best prepared. Links masters possess a range of shots to handle the wind and quirky terrain. A sound temperament is required to deal with the amateur element of this event, with rounds taking an age to complete
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course: Club de Campo Villa de Madrid
Course Details:
Course Club de Campo Villa de Madrid, Madrid, Spain
Length 7,154 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Javier Arana designed Black Course at Club de Campo Villa de Madrid has hosted 15 European Tour events in the modern era – the Spanish Open in 1990, 1991, 1994, 1995, 1996, 2019, 2021, 2022 and 2023, the Madrid Open from 2001-2005, and the Madrid Masters in 2008. The average winning score in the last four Spanish Opens there has been 22.25 under par. The tree-lined, undulating track has small greens and some stunning views over the city of Madrid
Type of player suited to the challenge Club de Campo can be overwhelmed, with the par-fives there for the taking and some extremely short par-fours, so a hot putter and three low rounds are probably going to be needed for success once the Thursday winds disappear
Key attribute Touch/putting

#DPWorldTour 
#OpenDeEspana 
#ClubDeCampoVillaDeMadrid
Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Open de España
Course: Club de Campo Villa de Madrid
Yardage: 7154y

Debating:
Jon Rahm
Daniel Hillier
Adri Arnaus
Rasmus Neergard-Petersen
Alfredo Garcia Heredia
Matt Wallace


Notes:
Thursday looks quite breezy with gusts uptown 30mph. Rest of the weekend is sunny and calm.
Last three winners of this (Jon Rahm x2) in top10 for putting.
Jon Rahm has won three of his last five Spanish Opens and comes here in red-hot form having come off his second win in LIV, LIV Golf Chicago. It’s hard to see past a win for Jon Rahm if he turns up and plays anywhere near his best at a course he knows extremely well. There is also the added incentive valuable Ryder Cup points for a LIV player as well as all the other politics.
Daniel Hillier comes here off the back of a very good T18 in the BMW PGA last week. He should thrive in the Thursday wind which could provide an opportunity to separate yourself from those who cannot play in the wind, T19 in this years Open which proves this. He was T9 here last year on his course debut and I expect him to go better this year.
Adri Arnaus is the first Spanish fancy I have in my roster this week. The Spanish have a good success rate at this tournament with some of those who seem to relish coming back to this course despite recent form. Adri Arnaus seems to be one of those T19 last year, 2nd 2021 & T4 2020. If he can putt well this week he could go well at a big price.
Rasmus Neergard-Peterson secured DPWT membership have claimed his 3rd Challenge Tour victory of this season a few weeks ago. This is a fantastic achievement for a player who looks to be a real talent. He missed out by only a few points to qualify for the DPWT last year. He has featured on a few leaderboards already through invites and sponsors exemptions to DPWT events this year. Now he has membership in the bag he can relax somewhat knowing there isn’t the same pressure. He is clearly in great form this season and should have the game to put up a good showing this week.
Alfredo Garcia Heredia came to my attention at the Omega Masters when loosing in a play off against Matt Wallace. He is a accurate strategic player who relatively short tight courses such as crans-sur-sierre. He’s made the cut in all 3 starts here this week in his last two outings T6 2022 & T9 last year. He loves it around here and with his current form could go and win.
Matt Wallace has been playing superb golf this year, making his debut on the PGA tour this year he’s made a decent showing there. However it’s his recent stretch in Europe which has turned his mediocre season into a great one. Winning the Omega Masters a few weeks ago he also had a T12 finish at Wentworth last week. Could go close this week at a course which should suit him.

#DPWorldTour 
#OpenDeEspana
#ClubDeCampoVillaDeMadrid
#JonRahm 
#DanielHillier
#AdriArnaus
#RasmusNeergardPeterson
#AlfredoGarciaHeredia
#MattWallace
Post Tournament Notes: This turned out to be a showdown in a playoff between two locals Jon Rahm & Angel Hildago with the latter prevailing. Jon had plenty of chances to seal the deal but just could not shake off Hildago’s challenge. An interesting course with it being being relatively short and tight with a drivable par 4 to finish (this was also the playoff hole). Adri Arenas was the closest in my other picks but a very disappointing Sunday blew any chances of even placing, leaving him with a lot of work to do to secure his playing rights next year.

There is also the added incentive valuable Ryder Cup points for a LIV player as well as all the other politics.
Daniel Hillier comes here off the back of a very good T18 in the BMW PGA last week. He should thrive in the Thursday wind which could provide an opportunity to separate yourself from those who cannot play in the wind, T19 in this years Open which proves this. He was T9 here last year on his course debut and I expect him to go better this year.
Adri Arnaus is the first Spanish fancy I have in my roster this week. The Spanish have a good success rate at this tournament with some of those who seem to relish coming back to this course despite recent form. Adri Arnaus seems to be one of those T19 last year, 2nd 2021 & T4 2020. If he can putt well this week he could go well at a big price.
Rasmus Neergard-Peterson secured DPWT membership have claimed his 3rd Challenge Tour victory of this season a few weeks ago. This is a fantastic achievement for a player who looks to be a real talent. He missed out by only a few points to qualify for the DPWT last year. He has featured on a few leaderboards already through invites and sponsors exemptions to DPWT events this year. Now he has membership in the bag he can relax somewhat knowing there isn’t the same pressure. He is clearly in great form this season and should have the game to put up a good showing this week.
Alfredo Garcia Heredia came to my attention at the Omega Masters when loosing in a play off against Matt Wallace. He is a accurate strategic player who relatively short tight courses such as crans-sur-sierre. He’s made the cut in all 3 starts here this week in his last two outings T6 2022 & T9 last year. He loves it around here and with his current form could go and win.
Matt Wallace has been playing superb golf this year, making his debut on the PGA tour this year he’s made a decent showing there. However it’s his recent stretch in Europe which has turned his mediocre season into a great one. Winning the Omega Masters a few weeks ago he also had a T12 finish at Wentworth last week. Could go close this week at a course which should suit him.

Post Tournament Notes: This turned out to be a showdown in a playoff between two locals Jon Rahm & Angel Hildago with the latter prevailing. Jon had plenty of chances to seal the deal but just could not shake off Hildago’s challenge. An interesting course with it being being relatively short and tight with a drivable par 4 to finish (this was also the playoff hole). Adri Arenas was the closest in my other picks but a very disappointing Sunday blew any chances of even placing, leaving him with a lot of work to do to secure his playing rights next year.

Course: Club de Campo Villa de Madrid
Course Details:
Course Club de Campo Villa de Madrid, Madrid, Spain
Length 7,154 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Javier Arana designed Black Course at Club de Campo Villa de Madrid has hosted 15 European Tour events in the modern era – the Spanish Open in 1990, 1991, 1994, 1995, 1996, 2019, 2021, 2022 and 2023, the Madrid Open from 2001-2005, and the Madrid Masters in 2008. The average winning score in the last four Spanish Opens there has been 22.25 under par. The tree-lined, undulating track has small greens and some stunning views over the city of Madrid
Type of player suited to the challenge Club de Campo can be overwhelmed, with the par-fives there for the taking and some extremely short par-fours, so a hot putter and three low rounds are probably going to be needed for success once the Thursday winds disappear
Key attribute Touch/putting

Course Details:
Course West Course, Wentworth Club, Virginia Water, Surrey
Length 7,627 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Wentworth is the spiritual home of what was the European Tour and has hosted the PGA Championship since 1984. It was also the home of the World Match Play from 1964 to 2007. Another fantastic renewal of the BMW PGA at Wentworth. The cream seemed to rise to the top this week, concluding in a 3-way playoff between Rory McIlroy, Billy Hershel & Thriston Lawrence. Billy ultimately prevailed with an eagle on the second playoff hole. None of my players made much impression, however it was great to watch some of the best players in the world fight it out at one of Europes best courses. Some very good performances to take out of this week, the most notable you could argue would be Matteo Manessero who was a few infant on the start of Sunday but couldn’t get the 4th round going. I was particularly impressed with Bob McIntyre who seems to master any type of course in any sort of condition week in week out. 
EyeCatchers - Matteo Manessero

Course Details:
Course West Course, Wentworth Club, Virginia Water, Surrey
Length 7,627 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Wentworth is the spiritual home of what was the European Tour and has hosted the PGA Championship since 1984. It was also the home of the World Match Play from 1964 to 2007. The tournament was held in May before moving to September five years ago. The greens were reseeded with bentgrass in 2017 and the putting surfaces are fast. Trees line many fairways and a number of doglegs means the wayward driving can be troublesome. The layout closes with two par-fives, the last of which contains a greenside water hazard that can produce Sunday drama.
Type of player suited to the challenge Wentworth is a great test of course management and finding the right areas on the fairways to attack the slick greens is a must.
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
DP World Tour
Tournament: PGA Championship
Course: Wentworth (West Course)
Yardage: 7627

Debating:
Joe Dean
Adam Scott
Victor Perez
Rikuya Hoshino
Connor Syme
Nacho Elvira
Frederic Lacroix

Notes:

Weather forecast looks good, dry sunny spells Thursday-Saturday with chance of showers on Sunday. Thursday & Friday slight breeze upto 11/12mph.
In this field apart from Rory the stand out player for me is Adam Scott. T7 here last year whilst he was not in the same vein of form that he is currently in bodes well this week. Since the Scottish Open he hasn’t been outside of the top20, this is in some high class fields including The Open & all three FedEx Playoff events. He is undoubtedly one of the most inform players in the world currently and playing arguably some of the best golf of his career at the age of 44. He will be desperate to capitalise on his recent form and coming over just to play in the DPWT flag ship event signifies his intent for me, barring in mind Presidents Cup is next week this is quite a bold move in his schedule. 
Joe Dean is a debutant this week at Wentworth being it his break through year since moving on from his supermarket delivery job. His story is incredible and he is no flash in the pan, he is a rising star and has come close to breaking his maiden a few times this year. Despite a tough week in Ireland last week which is not suited to everyone I think he could go well here on an iconic British course in easier conditions. Apart from the Open this year this is probably the biggest tournament he has played to date and could really solidify himself as one of the top European players and a good performance here can catapult you into PGA tour/Q school contention which would be unbelievable considering where he was this time last year. Big price and hoping for a good result this week.
Victor Perez has excelled his career to new heights despite not winning this year. He has had a decent start to his PGA Tour career with a few top10s and just made the Playoffs in 75th position, so easily within the top 125 to maintain his 2025 card. With that in the bag he comes back to the DPWT hot in form on a course he knows well. He was 2nd here behind Tyrrell Hatton in 2020 and has decent experience having played here on 4 different occasions. Most notably for me was his 2nd in the Canadian Open earlier this year at Hamilton Country Club which played very similar to Wentworth in design and conditions. I think he will be well up for it this week and looking to bag that first 2024 win to cap off a wonderful year.
Rikuya Hoshino caught my eye when backing him at fairly lengthy odds at the BMW International Open earlier this year. That course reminded me bit of Wentworth, it was similar in length and layout. He was T25 last year in his debut at Wentworth and he could well go better this year and win. Decent recent form and a good performance here could give him a great chance of earning a PGA Tour card next year.
Whichever stats you look at in previous years at Wentworth Richie Ramsey seems to feature in most. He has a great record here and 
Connor Syme has a decent record here in his two appearances making the cut in both T68 in 2022 and T10 last year. Similar to Hoshino he caught my eye at the MW International Open finishing T4. A win here for someone like Connor would propell his career to a different league in the form of earning a PGA tour card for next year. He is yet to win on the DPWT but has the game to do it and it could be this week on the Tours flagship event 
Nacho Elvira hasn’t got the best record round here at Wentworth which is surprising given his strongest attribute - APP is usually required here. He’s having a good season and performed well at the Irish Open last week at a very long price I thought he’s undervalued.
Frederic Lacroix has had one appearance here in 2022 making the cut finishing T65. He’s is in much better form now than he was then and at a triple-digit price he’s undervalued.

Course Details:
Course Silverado Resort and Spa (North Course), Napa, California
Length 7,166 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Silverado returned to the Tour schedule in 2014, hosting the frys.com Open for two years, which became the Safeway Open from 2016-2020, then the Fortinet Championship from 2021-2023, with Procore the latest sponsors this year. Silverado is a resort course, but the fairways are reasonably tight, the rough is fairly thick, and tall trees line the cut and prepared, while the small poa annua greens are full of undulation. All the dancefloors are guarded by heavy bunkering, closely-mown run-off areas or water
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a classic tree-lined assignment, where finding the fairways is essential, then accuracy is required to small targets on approach
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Procure Championship
Course: Silverado Resort (North Course)
Yardage: 7123

Maverick McNealy
JJ Spaun
Sahith Theegala

Notes:
Formly called the Fortinet Championship, now called Procure Championship.
Sahith Theegala won the Fortinet last year winning by 2.
Weather is good throughout the week, small breeze on Thursday with rest of the weekend calm 
Top5 last year stats:
All within top10 T2G
4/5 top15 ARG
4/5 top20 Putting
Maverick McNealy did not play last year due to injury, previous to that he missed the cut in 22 when he wasn’t in particularly great form. However the 22 edition he missed out by a shot to Max Homa who was simply on a mad one that day, putting from everywhere and a hole out eagle. Since then Maverick has still yet to win on the PGA Tour but you would think he is due one. This is a relatively short course measuring 7123, Maverick does tend to perform better when there isn’t such an emphasis on distance, combining this with his exquisite short game & putting could give him his first long overdue win on tour. 
Sahith Theegala will be looking to repeat Max Homa’s feat of winning this tournament back-to back. He won this event relatively comfortably last year winning by 2 shots last year. Unbelievably it is still his only PGA tour win and he will be well up for making it 2x wins at a course he knows very well. His form this year has been flaky but a few recent top5s, one of which at the Tour Championship shooting one shot better than Scottie Scheffler to finish 3rd as well as a T4 finish at the the Scottish Open
J.J. Spaun is on fire at the moment, his last two starts are T3 in the Wyndham & T9 3M Open. From both of these last two starts he has been in 3rd, 13th T2G, 35th, 21st PUTT. His form figures at Silverado for the last 5 attempts show CUT, T9, CUT, T59, T11. Baring in mind he started last year with a 71 and progressively getting better throughout the week T11 is pretty good, so he knows his way round here and is in good form.

Post Tournament Notes:
This was the first FedEx Fall event of the season and played up to expectations. Course was fairly average for PGA Tour, a tougher Sunday setup from pin placements cooled scoring down but Thurs-Sat was very scorrable. Long-time tour player Patton Kizzire (200-1) carded 4 very good rounds to win by 5 in the end, it was good to see former DPWT player David Lipsky finish second he didn’t have the Sunday round to get close to Kizzire in the end. JJ Spaun had a disappointing weekend after a spectacular round on Friday put him in the mix, Sahith didn’t really get close enough to contend, probably keeping an eye on the Presidents Cup in 2 weeks time.
Eye Catchers: Patrick Fishburn, Tom Hoge, Neal Shipley

Course Details:
Course Royal County Down Golf Club, Newcastle, County Down, Northern Ireland
Length 7,186 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Links
Course overview Royal County Down opened in 1889 and is one of the oldest golf clubs in the world. It is rated the best course in the world by Golf Digest. It was originally designed by Old Tom Morris, refined by others including Harry Colt, with Donald Steel conducting the most recent update. The Championship Course is a famous links which hosted the Irish Open in 1928, 1935 and 2015, the Amateur Championship in 1970 and 1999, the Walker Cup in 2007 and the Palmer Cup in 2012. The rugged terrain, numerous blind shots, penal rough, gorse, heather and bunkering make for a challenging layout. Two under par was enough to top the leaderboard after 72 holes of the last Irish Open at Royal County Down. The first and 12th were the only holes which played under par in that event
Type of player suited to the challenge Royal County Down is a demanding links track, so support strong ball-strikers who are comfortable on this type of terrain
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Irish Open
Course: Royal County Down GC
Yardage: 7186

Debating:
Guido Migliozzi 
Rasmus Nergaard-Peterson
David Ravetto
Robert MacIntyre
Mathew Jordan

Notes:
Royal County Down last hosted the Irish Open in 2015 with Soren Kjeldsen winning with a score -2 (looks like scoring around here could be tough with pars being good scores?)
Weather forecast looks mixed:
Thursday dry and breezy (upto 16mph)
Friday wet from 11am for the rest of the day with moderate breeze
Saturday wet with wind uptown 17 mph
Sunday dry with low winds
Good ball striker, plays in the wind & good links record
Behind Scottie & Xander you could definitely make a case for Robert MacIntyre being the 3rd best player in the world this year. Mad to think back to the Ryder Cup last year where he was an uncertain pick and had yet to make an impression on the PGA Tour. Both of his wins this year on the PGA Tour were in very credible competitions against relatively good fields they both also show pointers to why he will do well this week. The most obvious parallel you can draw is from the Scottish Open where Bob really thought hard in some fairly tough conditions to win with a birdie at the last. The Canadian open was also fought over in tough conditions on the Sunday at least. Now he has the Scottish & Canadian Opens in the bag I’m sure he will be looking for a big performance to give him a Irish Open win to make it the treble this year.
Mathew Jordan is a links specialist, his Open form the past two years is T10 & T10. With a brutal test predicted for this Irish Open could play into his hands this week. His form figures lately aren’t much to shout about he showed glimpses last week with a 68 & 69 before a tough weekend set him back. 
David Ravetto has had a great year, picking up a challenge tour win back in Feb followed by a win at the Czech Masters seemed a crazy price for someone who has had good form lately

Post Tournament Notes:
This was probably my favourite DPWT event to watch so far this year. Royal County Down is one of the very best courses in the world and it’s great to see some of the best talent put to the test in all aspects of their games. It was dry throughout with a bit of wind here and there to keep them thinking but it was clear to me that the best ball strikers rose to the top here. Rasmus Hojgaard carded a brilliant 6 under on Sunday (included to hole outs) to beat Rory by a shot. This was undoubtedly another Rory failure to close out a tournament having 3 putted the 17th hole to put him two back, had a good opportunity for eagle on the last but wasn’t to be. Bob McIntyre gave us a good run for our money securing a full place 6th in the end but didn’t really contend on Sunday. All in all a thoroughly entertaining watch all week.
Eye Catchers: Matteo Manassero Jordan Smith

#RobertMacIntyre
#MathewJordan
Post Tournament Notes:
This was probably my favourite DPWT event to watch so far this year. Royal County Down is one of the very best courses in the world and it’s great to see some of the best talent put to the test in all aspects of their games. It was dry throughout with a bit of wind here and there to keep them thinking but it was clear to me that the best ball strikers rose to the top here. Rasmus Hojgaard carded a brilliant 6 under on Sunday (included to hole outs) to beat Rory by a shot. This was undoubtedly another Rory failure to close out a tournament having 3 putted the 17th hole to put him two back, had a good opportunity for eagle on the last but wasn’t to be. Bob McIntyre gave us a good run for our money securing a full place 6th in the end but didn’t really contend on Sunday. All in all a thoroughly entertaining watch all week.
Eye Catchers: Matteo Manassero Jordan Smith

Course Details:
Course Silverado Resort and Spa (North Course), Napa, California
Length 7,166 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Silverado returned to the Tour schedule in 2014, hosting the frys.com Open for two years, which became the Safeway Open from 2016-2020, then the Fortinet Championship from 2021-2023, with Procore the latest sponsors this year. Silverado is a resort course, but the fairways are reasonably tight, the rough is fairly thick, and tall trees line the cut and prepared, while the small poa annua greens are full of undulation. All the dancefloors are guarded by heavy bunkering, closely-mown run-off areas or water
Type of player suited to the challenge This is a classic tree-lined assignment, where finding the fairways is essential, then accuracy is required to small targets on approach
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Procure Championship
Course: Silverado Resort (North Course)
Yardage: 7123

Maverick McNealy
JJ Spaun
Sahith Theegala

Notes:
Formly called the Fortinet Championship, now called Procure Championship.
Sahith Theegala won the Fortinet last year winning by 2.
Weather is good throughout the week, small breeze on Thursday with rest of the weekend calm 
Top5 last year stats:
All within top10 T2G
4/5 top15 ARG
4/5 top20 Putting
Maverick McNealy did not play last year due to injury, previous to that he missed the cut in 22 when he wasn’t in particularly great form. However the 22 edition he missed out by a shot to Max Homa who was simply on a mad one that day, putting from everywhere and a hole out eagle. Since then Maverick has still yet to win on the PGA Tour but you would think he is due one. This is a relatively short course measuring 7123, Maverick does tend to perform better when there isn’t such an emphasis on distance, combining this with his exquisite short game & putting could give him his first long overdue win on tour. 
Sahith Theegala will be looking to repeat Max Homa’s feat of winning this tournament back-to back. He won this event relatively comfortably last year winning by 2 shots last year. Unbelievably it is still his only PGA tour win and he will be well up for making it 2x wins at a course he knows very well. His form this year has been flaky but a few recent top5s, one of which at the Tour Championship shooting one shot better than Scottie Scheffler to finish 3rd as well as a T4 finish at the the Scottish Open
J.J. Spaun is on fire at the moment, his last two starts are T3 in the Wyndham & T9 3M Open. From both of these last two starts he has been in 3rd, 13th T2G, 35th, 21st PUTT. His form figures at Silverado for the last 5 attempts show CUT, T9, CUT, T59, T11. Baring in mind he started last year with a 71 and progressively getting better throughout the week T11 is pretty good, so he knows his way round here and is in good form.

Post Tournament Notes:
This was the first FedEx Fall event of the season and played up to expectations. Course was fairly average for PGA Tour, a tougher Sunday setup from pin placements cooled scoring down but Thurs-Sat was very scorrable. Long-time tour player Patton Kizzire (200-1) carded 4 very good rounds to win by 5 in the end, it was good to see former DPWT player David Lipsky finish second he didn’t have the Sunday round to get close to Kizzire in the end. JJ Spaun had a disappointing weekend after a spectacular round on Friday put him in the mix, Sahith didn’t really get close enough to contend, probably keeping an eye on the Presidents Cup in 2 weeks time.
Eye Catchers: Patrick Fishburn, Tom Hoge, Neal Shipley

Course Details:
Course Royal County Down Golf Club, Newcastle, County Down, Northern Ireland
Length 7,186 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Links
Course overview Royal County Down opened in 1889 and is one of the oldest golf clubs in the world. It is rated the best course in the world by Golf Digest. It was originally designed by Old Tom Morris, refined by others including Harry Colt, with Donald Steel conducting the most recent update. The Championship Course is a famous links which hosted the Irish Open in 1928, 1935 and 2015, the Amateur Championship in 1970 and 1999, the Walker Cup in 2007 and the Palmer Cup in 2012. The rugged terrain, numerous blind shots, penal rough, gorse, heather and bunkering make for a challenging layout. Two under par was enough to top the leaderboard after 72 holes of the last Irish Open at Royal County Down. The first and 12th were the only holes which played under par in that event
Type of player suited to the challenge Royal County Down is a demanding links track, so support strong ball-strikers who are comfortable on this type of terrain
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Irish Open
Course: Royal County Down GC
Yardage: 7186

Debating:
Guido Migliozzi 
Rasmus Nergaard-Peterson
David Ravetto
Robert MacIntyre
Mathew Jordan

Notes:
Royal County Down last hosted the Irish Open in 2015 with Soren Kjeldsen winning with a score -2 (looks like scoring around here could be tough with pars being good scores?)
Weather forecast looks mixed:
Thursday dry and breezy (upto 16mph)
Friday wet from 11am for the rest of the day with moderate breeze
Saturday wet with wind uptown 17 mph
Sunday dry with low winds
Good ball striker, plays in the wind & good links record
Behind Scottie & Xander you could definitely make a case for Robert MacIntyre being the 3rd best player in the world this year. Mad to think back to the Ryder Cup last year where he was an uncertain pick and had yet to make an impression on the PGA Tour. Both of his wins this year on the PGA Tour were in very credible competitions against relatively good fields they both also show pointers to why he will do well this week. The most obvious parallel you can draw is from the Scottish Open where Bob really thought hard in some fairly tough conditions to win with a birdie at the last. The Canadian open was also fought over in tough conditions on the Sunday at least. Now he has the Scottish & Canadian Opens in the bag I’m sure he will be looking for a big performance to give him a Irish Open win to make it the treble this year.
Mathew Jordan is a links specialist, his Open form the past two years is T10 & T10. With a brutal test predicted for this Irish Open could play into his hands this week. His form figures lately aren’t much to shout about he showed glimpses last week with a 68 & 69 before a tough weekend set him back. 
David Ravetto has had a great year, picking up a challenge tour win back in Feb followed by a win at the Czech Masters seemed a crazy price for someone who has had good form lately

Course Details:
Course Crans-sur-Sierre Golf Club, Crans Montana, Switzerland
Prize money $3.25m ($541,775 to the winner)
Length 6,823 yards
Par 70 - three par-five holes; ten par-fours; five par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Highest-ranked players in field (world ranking in brackets) Matt Fitzpatrick (27), Nicolai Hojgaard (50), Thriston Lawrence (62), Erik van Rooyen (68), Ryo Hisatsune (78)
Course records - 72 holes 260 Colin Montgomerie (1996) 18 holes 60 Jamie Spence (1992), James Morrison (2021)
Course winners taking part Miguel Angel Jimenez, Richie Ramsay, Matt Fitzpatrick (twice), Sebastian Soderberg, Rasmus Hojgaard, Thriston Lawrence
Time difference Switzerland is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Last week – British Masters 1 N Norgaard (45-1), 2 T Lawrence (30-1), 3 R Hojgaard (25-1), 4 J W Ko (400-1), 5 J Svensson (60-1), T6 J Campillo (66-1), M Manassero (45-1), 8 M Wallace (22-1)
Course type Parkland
Course overview There has been a Tour event staged at Crans every year since 1972, apart from the 2020 Covid cancellation. A Seve Ballesteros redesign in 1999 made it tougher, creating lots of run-off areas around the greens. The sixth and seventh are driveable par-fours. Small, firm, unreceptive greens are the main problem for the competitors, although they are aided by the fact that the ball flies further than normal at the altitude. Most fairways are tree-lined and undulating
The story of last year Ludvig Aberg made his DP World Tour breakthrough, winning the event on his Crans debut
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green and course-management have been the keys to success. The honours board is full of precise operators such as Miguel Angel Jimenez, Thomas Bjorn (twice), Richie Ramsay and Matthew Fitzpatrick (twice)
Key attribute Accuracy

Jacob Scov Olesen
Matteo Manassero 
David Ravetto
Rasmus Hogaard

5 of the last 6 winners of the European Masters have been newbies to this tournament, one of those was Matt Fitzpatrick defending his win.
I am keeping the faith with Thriston Lawrence this week after a good 2nd last week at the British Masters. He was at the top of the leaderboard throughout and was beaten by two shots to Nicolas Norgaard who had a fantastic week, I felt as if he was quite timid on his Sunday round but realistically 4 shots was quite a deficit and Norgaard didn’t really put a foot wrong apart from maybe 15th double bogey but by then it was over. Thriston is playing brilliant golf at the moment will be keen to keep up the form to earn his PGA Tour card for next season. His best results do seem to be when he has a few consecutive weeks on the road, this will be his third week having won in SA two weeks ago, 2nd British Masters and now European Masters which he won in 2022. 
From now on if I see Jacob Scov Olesen name on the start sheet I will likely back him at the prices he’s at. The current British Amateur champion must be full of confidence having given us a great run for our money two weeks ago in the Danish Championship - T5. He will have likely gained experience from that and will be ready for another big performance here this week in Switzerland. He is a top talent to keep an eye on will likely go on to have a brilliant career.
Adrien Saddier has been on my radar the last few weeks not least because he is French and they have been in great form lately but due to his recent form T5 Danish & T3 Czech Masters. He also has good course form round here, T7 2021 & T13 2023. He is from the Alps area of France only a few hours away across the border to this weeks venue, coming off a weeks rest at home he will be well up for this.
Jesper Svenson is another who is riding on crest of a wave of great form. He has already bagged a first DPWT win in his rookie season and will be looking for a second to secure his PGA tour card for next season (currently 2nd). 
I might have missed out on the early juice on Matteo Manassero’s price but I do think this week could shape up well for him. I was hesitant to take such a low price on him but having looked at last weeks strokes gained at The Belfry he was at the top of the leaderboard for all areas that I checked - 1st GiR, 4th T2G & 10th APP. He will need to have a better week on the greens but I’m confident this course will play into his strengths.

Post Tournament Notes:
An iconic venue for this weeks stop on the DPWT, this is a short quirky course where scoring is determined by the conditions. This week proved that in abundance, wind was up most of the week and caused havoc especially on the Saturday with the lowest score being one under! (on a sub 7000y track). Overall it was a disappointing week for my fancies, Jesper & Matteo missed the cut and those who made it to the weekend ended up being blown off the course… literally! Matt Wallace won on the first playoff hole against Garcia-Heredia for a well deserved win, Ive seen a fair bit of Matt Wallace on the PGA Tour and this will boost his confidence going forward.
Eye Catchers: Andrew Johnstone

Course Details:
Course Crans-sur-Sierre Golf Club, Crans Montana, Switzerland
Prize money $3.25m ($541,775 to the winner)
Length 6,823 yards
Par 70 - three par-five holes; ten par-fours; five par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Highest-ranked players in field (world ranking in brackets) Matt Fitzpatrick (27), Nicolai Hojgaard (50), Thriston Lawrence (62), Erik van Rooyen (68), Ryo Hisatsune (78)
Course records - 72 holes 260 Colin Montgomerie (1996) 18 holes 60 Jamie Spence (1992), James Morrison (2021)
Course winners taking part Miguel Angel Jimenez, Richie Ramsay, Matt Fitzpatrick (twice), Sebastian Soderberg, Rasmus Hojgaard, Thriston Lawrence
Time difference Switzerland is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Last week – British Masters 1 N Norgaard (45-1), 2 T Lawrence (30-1), 3 R Hojgaard (25-1), 4 J W Ko (400-1), 5 J Svensson (60-1), T6 J Campillo (66-1), M Manassero (45-1), 8 M Wallace (22-1)
Course type Parkland
Course overview There has been a Tour event staged at Crans every year since 1972, apart from the 2020 Covid cancellation. A Seve Ballesteros redesign in 1999 made it tougher, creating lots of run-off areas around the greens. The sixth and seventh are driveable par-fours. Small, firm, unreceptive greens are the main problem for the competitors, although they are aided by the fact that the ball flies further than normal at the altitude. Most fairways are tree-lined and undulating
The story of last year Ludvig Aberg made his DP World Tour breakthrough, winning the event on his Crans debut
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green and course-management have been the keys to success. The honours board is full of precise operators such as Miguel Angel Jimenez, Thomas Bjorn (twice), Richie Ramsay and Matthew Fitzpatrick (twice)
Key attribute Accuracy

Post Tournament Notes:
An iconic venue for this weeks stop on the DPWT, this is a short quirky course where scoring is determined by the conditions. This week proved that in abundance, wind was up most of the week and caused havoc especially on the Saturday with the lowest score being one under! (on a sub 7000y track). Overall it was a disappointing week for my fancies, Jesper & Matteo missed the cut and those who made it to the weekend ended up being blown off the course… literally! Matt Wallace won on the first playoff hole against Garcia-Heredia for a well deserved win, Ive seen a fair bit of Matt Wallace on the PGA Tour and this will boost his confidence going forward.
Eye Catchers: Andrew Johnstone

Course Details:
Course Crans-sur-Sierre Golf Club, Crans Montana, Switzerland
Prize money $3.25m ($541,775 to the winner)
Length 6,823 yards
Par 70 - three par-five holes; ten par-fours; five par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Highest-ranked players in field (world ranking in brackets) Matt Fitzpatrick (27), Nicolai Hojgaard (50), Thriston Lawrence (62), Erik van Rooyen (68), Ryo Hisatsune (78)
Course records - 72 holes 260 Colin Montgomerie (1996) 18 holes 60 Jamie Spence (1992), James Morrison (2021)
Course winners taking part Miguel Angel Jimenez, Richie Ramsay, Matt Fitzpatrick (twice), Sebastian Soderberg, Rasmus Hojgaard, Thriston Lawrence
Time difference Switzerland is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Last week – British Masters 1 N Norgaard (45-1), 2 T Lawrence (30-1), 3 R Hojgaard (25-1), 4 J W Ko (400-1), 5 J Svensson (60-1), T6 J Campillo (66-1), M Manassero (45-1), 8 M Wallace (22-1)
Course type Parkland
Course overview There has been a Tour event staged at Crans every year since 1972, apart from the 2020 Covid cancellation. A Seve Ballesteros redesign in 1999 made it tougher, creating lots of run-off areas around the greens. The sixth and seventh are driveable par-fours. Small, firm, unreceptive greens are the main problem for the competitors, although they are aided by the fact that the ball flies further than normal at the altitude. Most fairways are tree-lined and undulating
The story of last year Ludvig Aberg made his DP World Tour breakthrough, winning the event on his Crans debut
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green and course-management have been the keys to success. The honours board is full of precise operators such as Miguel Angel Jimenez, Thomas Bjorn (twice), Richie Ramsay and Matthew Fitzpatrick (twice)
Key attribute Accuracy

#DPWorldTour 
#OmegaEuropeanMasters 
#CransSurSierreGC
Pre Tournament Notes:
DPWT
Tournament: Omega European Masters
Course: Crans-sur-Sierre GC
Yardage: 6823y

Debating:
Thriston Lawrence
Jesper Svenson
Adrien Saddier
Jacob Scov Olesen
Matteo Manassero 
David Ravetto
Rasmus Hogaard

5 of the last 6 winners of the European Masters have been newbies to this tournament, one of those was Matt Fitzpatrick defending his win.
I am keeping the faith with Thriston Lawrence this week after a good 2nd last week at the British Masters. He was at the top of the leaderboard throughout and was beaten by two shots to Nicolas Norgaard who had a fantastic week, I felt as if he was quite timid on his Sunday round but realistically 4 shots was quite a deficit and Norgaard didn’t really put a foot wrong apart from maybe 15th double bogey but by then it was over. Thriston is playing brilliant golf at the moment will be keen to keep up the form to earn his PGA Tour card for next season. His best results do seem to be when he has a few consecutive weeks on the road, this will be his third week having won in SA two weeks ago, 2nd British Masters and now European Masters which he won in 2022. 
From now on if I see Jacob Scov Olesen name on the start sheet I will likely back him at the prices he’s at. The current British Amateur champion must be full of confidence having given us a great run for our money two weeks ago in the Danish Championship - T5. He will have likely gained experience from that and will be ready for another big performance here this week in Switzerland. He is a top talent to keep an eye on will likely go on to have a brilliant career.
Adrien Saddier has been on my radar the last few weeks not least because he is French and they have been in great form lately but due to his recent form T5 Danish & T3 Czech Masters. He also has good course form round here, T7 2021 & T13 2023. He is from the Alps area of France only a few hours away across the border to this weeks venue, coming off a weeks rest at home he will be well up for this.
Jesper Svenson is another who is riding on crest of a wave of great form. He has already bagged a first DPWT win in his rookie season and will be looking for a second to secure his PGA tour card for next season (currently 2nd). 
I might have missed out on the early juice on Matteo Manassero’s price but I do think this week could shape up well for him. I was hesitant to take such a low price on him but having looked at last weeks strokes gained at The Belfry he was at the top of the leaderboard for all areas that I checked - 1st GiR, 4th T2G & 10th APP. He will need to have a better week on the greens but I’m confident this course will play into his strengths.

Course Details:
Course Brabazon Course, The Belfry, Sutton Coldfield, England
Length 7,336 yards
Par 72 – three par-fives; 12 par-fours; three par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Brabazon Course hosted the Ryder Cup in 1985, 1989, 1993 and 2002. Several Tour events have been staged at The Belfry, including the International Open (2000-2003), the British Masters (2006, 2007, 2008, 2021, 2022, 2023), and UK Championship (2020). The 2021 British Challenge (Challenge Tour) was also at the Brabazon. There is more water on the layout than just about any inland course in the British Isles, so scoring can be volatile, and the front nine particularly demands accuracy. The back nine is more open, although heavily bunkered
Type of player suited to the challenge Strong ball-strikers have traditionally thrived at this venue, which features a series of long, punishing par-fours
Key attribute Accuracy

#BritishMasters 
#DPWorldTour
Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: British Masters
Course: Brabazan Course, The Belfry
Yardage: 7336

Debating:
Callum Hill
David Micheluzzi
Pedro Figueiredo
Tyrrell Hatton
Ewen Ferguson
Thriston Lawrence

Notes:
For me Tyrrell Hatton is by some way the best player in this field. No matter if it’s LIV, PGA or a major he is more often than not in contention and this is quite a step down to the usual competition he faces. Having watched him a fair bit this year I’ve come to notice how good all aspects of his game are, he is impressively straight off the tee with above average distance and his LIV putting stats rank him in top5. He will desperate to put aside those doubters who do not think he should be competing this week, it is also another opportunity to bag those all important OWGR ranking points.
David Micheluzzi gave me a good run for my money last week with a T10 finish, sadly missing out on the places however considering the conditions and some of the talent he was up against that is a very good performance. Given that the course last week was an unknown, he knows The Belfry slightly more despite missing the cut last year. Im not too concerned about that given he wasn’t in great form and it was his first visit. These week in splendid conditions and at the top of his game I expect another good finish, with big prices combined with lots of places I couldn’t pass up the chance to back the Aussie once more.
Having had a quick glance through the bottom of the markets I noticed 5th place finisher last week Pedro Figueiredo at a huge price against with plenty of places to offer. Very streaky player who pops up a few times a season with a top20 I thought perhaps he could ride the wave into this week and put up another big performance 
Ewen Ferguson was very impressive when winning at the BMW International Open last month. One of the best swings on tour he is unbelievably talented, combined with a cool calm demeanour I do expect him to win more in the future including here at The Belfry. The venue of the BMW International Open - Golfclub Munchen Eichenried did remind me a bit of The Belfry in terms of some intricate holes with a fair amount of water and hazards. Ewen was T4 here last year and after some well earned time off after the Open he could put the field to the sword here.
I was first impressed with Thriston Lawrence at the European Open (Green Eagle) in Germany T2 after an opening 77 showed real character to come back most notably a 68 in tough conditions on Sunday to give himself a shot to win, ultimately missing out by 2. On the subject of tough conditions he produced his best major performance at the The Open finishing 4th. He comes here this week to a course he knows well making the cut on both visits here and off a win in his previous start on the Sunshine Tour in SA. He is playing great golf at the moment and could pick a consecutive win.

#TyrrellHatton 
#EwenFerguson
#ThristonLawrence
Post Tournament Notes:
The Brabazon course at The Belfry is simply iconic and a brilliant venue for this weeks DPWT British Masters. It was the Tyrell Hatton show for Thursday & Friday before a spectacular capitulation on the Saturday 76 followed by similar on the Sunday 75. Apart from Thriston Lawrence none of my picks really featured but Thriston 2nd in the end was in contention from start to finish but could not quite catch Niklas Noorgard who had particularly good rounds on Friday 66 & Saturday 64 to give him a 4 shots lead prior to Sundays round. This was quite the deficit for Thriston to pull back and turned out to be a shot or two too much. Nevertheless it was good to see Noorgard hold his nerve and win this week having played great golf all year round.
EyeCatchers: Jesper Svenson, Rasmus Horjgaard
