Tournament: British Masters
Course: Brabazan Course, The Belfry
Yardage: 7336

Debating:
Callum Hill
David Micheluzzi
Pedro Figueiredo
Tyrrell Hatton
Ewen Ferguson
Thriston Lawrence

Notes:
For me Tyrrell Hatton is by some way the best player in this field. No matter if it’s LIV, PGA or a major he is more often than not in contention and this is quite a step down to the usual competition he faces. Having watched him a fair bit this year I’ve come to notice how good all aspects of his game are, he is impressively straight off the tee with above average distance and his LIV putting stats rank him in top5. He will desperate to put aside those doubters who do not think he should be competing this week, it is also another opportunity to bag those all important OWGR ranking points.
David Micheluzzi gave me a good run for my money last week with a T10 finish, sadly missing out on the places however considering the conditions and some of the talent he was up against that is a very good performance. Given that the course last week was an unknown, he knows The Belfry slightly more despite missing the cut last year. Im not too concerned about that given he wasn’t in great form and it was his first visit. These week in splendid conditions and at the top of his game I expect another good finish, with big prices combined with lots of places I couldn’t pass up the chance to back the Aussie once more.
Having had a quick glance through the bottom of the markets I noticed 5th place finisher last week Pedro Figueiredo at a huge price against with plenty of places to offer. Very streaky player who pops up a few times a season with a top20 I thought perhaps he could ride the wave into this week and put up another big performance 
Ewen Ferguson was very impressive when winning at the BMW International Open last month. One of the best swings on tour he is unbelievably talented, combined with a cool calm demeanour I do expect him to win more in the future including here at The Belfry. The venue of the BMW International Open - Golfclub Munchen Eichenried did remind me a bit of The Belfry in terms of some intricate holes with a fair amount of water and hazards. Ewen was T4 here last year and after some well earned time off after the Open he could put the field to the sword here.
I was first impressed with Thriston Lawrence at the European Open (Green Eagle) in Germany T2 after an opening 77 showed real character to come back most notably a 68 in tough conditions on Sunday to give himself a shot to win, ultimately missing out by 2. On the subject of tough conditions he produced his best major performance at the The Open finishing 4th. He comes here this week to a course he knows well making the cut on both visits here and off a win in his previous start on the Sunshine Tour in SA. He is playing great golf at the moment and could pick a consecutive win.

Course Details: N/A

Course East Lake Golf Club, Atlanta, Georgia
Length 7,490 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Format A handicap system has been in operation for the Tour Championship since 2019, with the FedEx Cup leader starting on ten under par, the second in the standings at eight under par, the third at seven under, fourth at six under, fifth at five under, sixth to tenth at four under, 11th to 15th at three under, 16th to 20th at two under, 21st to 25th at one under, and 26th to 30th at level par
Course type Parkland
Course overview East Lake is the curtain-closer to the PGA Tour season, with the most successful performers of the year ending their Stateside campaign in the Tour Championship. East Lake has hosted this event in 1998, 2000, 2002 and from 2004 onwards. The famous course was designed by Tom Bendelow and Donald Ross, but Rees Jones took charge of a redesign in 1994 and at the end of 2007 (he switched all the greens from bentgrass to Bermuda), and an even more extensive redesign has taken place since the last Tour Championship, with bunkers relocated and reshaped, trees removed, mounds added, and all 18 greens revised, with many much larger and more undulating than before. Andrew Green took charge of the year-long project. The par has changed from 70 to 71. A SubAir drainage system means firm, fast greens are almost certain. Oak Hill, which hosted last year's US PGA, was subject to a redesign by Green prior to that championship
Type of player suited to the challenge Finding fairways has traditionally been extremely difficult at East Lake. The course has been dramatically redesigned, so previous course form counts for less, but strong ball-striking remains the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Danish Golf Championship
Course: Lubker Golf Resort
Yardage: 7026y


Debating:
Bernd Wiesberger
Sam Bairstow
David Micheluzzi
Jacob Scov Olesen
Richard Mansall

Notes:
David Micheluzzi caught my eye in the BMW International Open in Germany a few weeks back. Form can be quite patchy but has shown some good stuff this year, he comes across as very fearless with that huge shot on 18 to make it into second. Looking at the stats if he can get the putter going can be a real threat, he was T17 in made in Himmerland last year and although this is a different course at a big price could go well here.
Sam Bairstow is showing signs of getting back into great form after a slight blip this season, and that can be forgiven in his rookie year. Really impressive Asian Swing stretch of tournaments with a t10 at the soudal open which I wouldn’t have thought would be his cup of tea. Since then has struggled slightly but T6 at co sanctioned ISCO with a T12 in Prague last week shows he’s getting back into the groove. An up & coming star of the DPWT with a first win when not if. The course this week is a slight unknown which in his case is the story of most weeks. He can do well here in an event not all the big players are attending.
Bernd Wiesberger is one of the best ball strikers on tour and consistently in the mix throughout the year no matter the venue or conditions. It is quite surprising that he hasn’t won this year considering his talents from T2G however the one thing that lets him down is putting. He has shown when he is putting well he is in contention. In this slightly depleted field he can win this if the putter behaves.
I caught a glimpse of Jacob Scott Olesen win in the coveted British Amateur this year in horrendous conditions. He is a very talented young player who has had a brilliant junior career and is currently playing at college in Arkansas. At a big price in his home country he could play well in the tough conditions forecasted.

#DanishGolfChampionship
#LubkerGolfResort
#DPWorldTour
#BerndWiesBerger 
#SamBairstow 
#DavidMicheluzzi
#JacobScovOlesen
Post Tournament Notes:
Another new venue on the DPWT for players to work out but it was the weather that really threw those competing. Play was halted on Friday after 45 mph+ winds nearly blew those playing at the time off course. The wind and sometimes rain battered the field all throughout the weekend and the best ball strikers ultimately rose to the top with Frederic Lacroix picking up the win by an impressive 4 shots. He held his nerve in the wind & rain where others just couldn’t get close to him without mistakes. That’s two fantastic weeks for him having finished T3 last week in Prague and following up with a win this week. The French really have some top talent on form at the moment with David Ravetto winning last week and Languasque T2 behind LaCroix.
Sam Bairstow WD which was a surprise, Micheluzzi & Wiesberger both just out of the places but done OK. Jacob Scott Olesen (AM) was the best performer from my picks with a T5 and missing out on a full place unfortunately, but provided a great run for my money at his price. To have a T5 finish in your first tour event in your home country really is impressive and is one to watch out for in the future. 
Eye-catchers: Rasmussen Neergaard-Petersen, Romain Langasque

Course Details:
Course Castle Pines Golf Club, Denver, Colorado
Length 8,130 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview Castle Pines was a PGA Tour venue from 1986 through 2006, hosting The International, which used a modified Stableford format. The track affords breathtaking views of Rocky Mountain. Jack Nicklaus has been overseeing a revision of the layout over the last decade to get it ready for a Tour return. Ten holes have been redesigned, 77 bunkers and ten water hazards rebuilt, and 600 yards added to the length. The course is 6,500 feet above sea level, so balls travel about ten percent further. The fairways are tree-lined, but Nicklaus likes to give players generous landing areas off the tee. Approach-shots are more difficult, with lots of raised greens
Type of player suited to the challenge Distance control is the key to success at Castle Pines, with clubbing so difficult at altitude. Approach-shot accuracy is all-important
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: BMW Championship
Course: Castle Pines Golf Club
Yardage: 8130y

Debating:
Nick Dunlap
Wyndham Clark
Patrick Cantlay
Xander Schaufelle

Notes:
Nick Dunlap showed again last week why he is one of the top players in the world right now with a T5 in his first FedEx event, you have to remember this is his rookie year (aged 20!) with 2 PGA tour wins. One of those wins at altitude in the Barracuda Championship bodes well at this venue in Colorado. 80s for this player desperate to prove he is capable of reaching the Tour Championship is an incredible price.
This is Wyndham Clarks home event, one that he has mentioned is very important to him. Ive been very cool on Wyndham throughout the year as he hasn’t reached the same heights as in 2023 and can be very streaky with the putter but after T7 last week I’m ready to put my money behind him once again this week.
I’m putting my faith back into Patrick Cantlay once again this week. He loves these playoff events having already won a few of these in the past. If it weren’t for a poor R1 last week he would have been right in contention but left himself a bit too much to do in the end. He should relish the high altitude this week and if he can play anything like he did R2-R4 last week he will be in contention this week

#PatrickCantlay
Post Tournament Notes:
Not your typical PGA Tour venue, penalising rough combined with altitude proved to be entertaining viewing. Despite the extreme length it is not actually that long if you consider the extra 10-15% longer players shots were going, however it was the confusion of club selection that proved to be the biggest challange. Keegan Bradley played very solid to pick up his first win of the season whilst Adam Scott just couldn’t get the putter working in the final two rounds combined with some T2G mistakes. Sam Burns made a great charge on Sunday with a -7 round, but mistakes in earlier rounds cost him the win in the end. My fancies gave a decent running but were never in contention and disappointing to not at least get a place especially from Wyndham Clark who at T7 at the start of Sunday, you’d think was primed for a big Sunday but posted +2. 
Eye-Catchers: Sam Burns

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Danish Golf Championship
Course: Lubker Golf Resort
Yardage: 7026y


Debating:
Bernd Wiesberger
Sam Bairstow
David Micheluzzi
Jacob Scov Olesen
Richard Mansall

Notes:
David Micheluzzi caught my eye in the BMW International Open in Germany a few weeks back. Form can be quite patchy but has shown some good stuff this year, he comes across as very fearless with that huge shot on 18 to make it into second. Looking at the stats if he can get the putter going can be a real threat, he was T17 in made in Himmerland last year and although this is a different course at a big price could go well here.
Sam Bairstow is showing signs of getting back into great form after a slight blip this season, and that can be forgiven in his rookie year. Really impressive Asian Swing stretch of tournaments with a t10 at the soudal open which I wouldn’t have thought would be his cup of tea. Since then has struggled slightly but T6 at co sanctioned ISCO with a T12 in Prague last week shows he’s getting back into the groove. An up & coming star of the DPWT with a first win when not if. The course this week is a slight unknown which in his case is the story of most weeks. He can do well here in an event not all the big players are attending.
Bernd Wiesberger is one of the best ball strikers on tour and consistently in the mix throughout the year no matter the venue or conditions. It is quite surprising that he hasn’t won this year considering his talents from T2G however the one thing that lets him down is putting. He has shown when he is putting well he is in contention. In this slightly depleted field he can win this if the putter behaves.
I caught a glimpse of Jacob Scott Olesen win in the coveted British Amateur this year in horrendous conditions. He is a very talented young player who has had a brilliant junior career and is currently playing at college in Arkansas. At a big price in his home country he could play well in the tough conditions forecasted.

#DanishGolfChampionship
#LubkerGolfResort
#DPWorldTour
#BerndWiesBerger 
#SamBairstow 
#DavidMicheluzzi
#JacobScovOlesen
Post Tournament Notes:
Another new venue on the DPWT for players to work out but it was the weather that really threw those competing. Play was halted on Friday after 45 mph+ winds nearly blew those playing at the time off course. The wind and sometimes rain battered the field all throughout the weekend and the best ball strikers ultimately rose to the top with Frederic Lacroix picking up the win by an impressive 4 shots. He held his nerve in the wind & rain where others just couldn’t get close to him without mistakes. That’s two fantastic weeks for him having finished T3 last week in Prague and following up with a win this week. The French really have some top talent on form at the moment with David Ravetto winning last week and Languasque T2 behind LaCroix.
Sam Bairstow WD which was a surprise, Micheluzzi & Wiesberger both just out of the places but done OK. Jacob Scott Olesen (AM) was the best performer from my picks with a T5 and missing out on a full place unfortunately, but provided a great run for my money at his price. To have a T5 finish in your first tour event in your home country really is impressive and is one to watch out for in the future. 
Eye-catchers: Rasmussen Neergaard-Petersen, Romain Langasque

Course Old White, The Greenbrier, White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia
Length 7,299 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 54 holes 187 Bryson DeChambeau (2023) 18 holes 58 Bryson DeChambeau (2023)
Time difference West Virginia is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview Old White staged the Greenbrier Classic on the PGA Tour from 2010 through 2019. There was no event in 2016 due to flooding. The inaugural LIV Greenbrier was 12 months ago. Every event at this flat, fairly straightforward resort course has had a winning score of double-figures under par
Story of last year Bryson DeChambeau carded a final-round 58 in soft conditions to reach 23 under par and win by six shots
Weather forecast Cloudy, warm and calm, with thunder threats over the weekend
Type of player suited to the challenge Old White was overwhelmed last year and another birdie-fest can be expected. Anyone carrying a cold putter will likely be left behind
Key attribute Touch/putting

Tournament: LIV Golf Greenbrier
Course: Old White, Greenbrier 
Yardage: 7299yards

Debating: 
Joaquin Niemann

Notes:
Joaquin has dominated the players standings since he won 2 LIV events earlier this year. He’s played pretty good all throughout the season without winning again but with consistent top10 finishes. Now that Jon Rahm has won, Niemann is starting to feel the pressure slightly. It is now  that we are coming to a close on the season with $18m going to the top player in the standings Joaquin will want to close the door shut on Rahm and win this event which will see him win the 18m.
Post Tournament Notes: Didn’t watch enough of this event to comment too much. Greenbrier seems like a good course with penalising rough, accuracy T2G and approach are the key here. Joaquin poor putting display again leading to a T15 finish. Jon Rahm just missed out on his second consecutive LIV win by Brooks Koepka in a playoff

Playoff format This is the first of three FedEx Cup playoff events. The top 50 in the standings will progress to next week's BMW Championship, before the top 30 qualify for the season-ending Tour Championship. 
Course records - 72 holes 258 John Cook (1996) 18 holes 61 Jay Delsing (1993), Bob Estes (2001), Tom Lewis (2020), Justin Rose (2023)

It has been perennially ranked as one of the most difficult on the PGA Tour since. The final St Jude Classic was in 2018, then the WGC-St Jude Invitational was at Southwind from 2019 through 2021, before the first FedEx St Jude Championship in 2022. Course Details:
Course PGA National OAKS Prague, Prague, Czech Republic
Length 7,592 yards
Par 72 - four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Czech Masters was created in 2014, with the Albatross Resort hosting a Tour event annually every year apart from 2020 (cancelled due to Covid), but there is a new venue for the tenth anniversary of the tournament. PGA National OAKS Prague, located just south of the capital city in Nebrenice, opened in the summer of 2020. Kyle Phillips, who created Kingsbarns in Scotland, designed the course. It starts in an oak-studded valley, before opening up from the fourth hole onwards, then tightening up again from the 12th hole. With streams, ravines, wetlands, knee-high rough in places, doglegs and firm greens, this appears a tough track
Type of player suited to the challenge This looks a tougher layout than the Albatross. Strong ball-striking and course-management should yield results
Key attribute Accuracy

#DPWorldTour 
#PGANationalOaks 
#CzechMasters
Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: D+D REAL Czech Masters
Course: PGA National Oaks
Yardage: 

Debating:
Joe Dean
Jack Davidson
Ross Fisher
Sam Bairstow
Tom McKibben
Bernd Wiesburger

Notes:
Joe Dean is continuing his scintillating run of form this season, a very good T25 at the Open last time out (includes a 77 in horrendous conditions) to boost three top5s on the DPWT this season (2 of which are 2nds). The first win isn’t far away at all and at 66s he is way overpriced in a lacklustre field this week missing a few big names. 
With his affiliation with The Caversham I have kept an eye out on the progress of Jack Davidson. Along with other course affiliate and the more notable Marco Penge who won the Challenge tour last year. Jack has had arguably a better start to the DPWT with 4 top25s this year, last time out T13. He has a decent all round game that suits a variety of courses and this being a first time for the entire field to play at this venue which could benefit Jack. At a big price he could go well.
With an emphasis on this relatively long and tight course, good OTT will benefit here. Having watched a fair amount of the DPWT this year there is not many better in this category than Ross Fisher. In this lacklustre field this could be Ross’s chance to get a first win in 10 years. Needs to be better on the greens to go close this week but at a good price Im willing to take a chance.

#DPWorldTour 
#CzechMasters
#PGANationalOaks
#JoeDean 
#JackDavidson
#RossFisher
Post Tournament Notes:
A new venue for the Czech Masters this week and it proved to be a bit meh. David Ravetto blew away the field winning by 4 on -23 from second Jesper Svensson who is having a great season on DPWT. To win here you have to capitalise on the 4 par5s and keep it simple on the par3s which Ravetto did very well. Ross fisher was the closest out of my picks T12, miserable Sunday with the putter again. Jack Davidson did well at his price T28 which could have been better but also had a poor Sunday.
Eye-Catchers: Jesper Svensson, Richard Mansall

Post Tournament Notes:
Hideki Matsuyama won after a late scare with a double at 16 but he was more or less top of the leaderboard from start to finish. I’m bit gutted to not pick Hideki this week at a pretty good price (28s) after his Bronze Medal at the Olympics and this venue setup well for him with accuracy and approach key to playing well here. My picks were disappointing in the end with none in real contention. Cantlay fought back for T12 after a bad start but nothing to get excited about.
Eye-Catchers: Viktor Hovland, Nick Dunlap

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Wyndham Championship
Course: Sedgefield Country Club
Yardage: 7131yards

Debating:
Kurt Kitayama
Aaron Rai
Cam Davies
Billy Horshel
Shane Lowry

Notes:
Tough conditions Thursday & Friday expecting lots of wind & rain however no electrical activity to threat playability, should make things interesting and those players good in the wind should be looked at closely.
Course sets up for accuracy off the tee and good approach play, previous winners include Lucas Glover (’23), Kevin Eisner (’21), Jim Herman (’20).
Kurt Kitayama displayed an impressive showing at the 3m Open last time out T6, The 66 he shot in the last round there was eye catching in tough conditions. Currently 74th in the FedEx standings he will need to have a good week here to make It in to St Jude next week (have to be in top70 to qualify). His last win at 2023 Arnold Palmer was in blustery conditions so he knows how to play in the wind.
I’ve defied Aaron Rai for a while now with doubt over his ability to close in on that elusive PGA Tour win, the closer he gets the more concerned I get not being on him. With an emphasis on accuracy combined with the tough conditions on day1&2 this is where I pull the trigger on him and really like this chances here. Ive seen a lot of him this year and he seems to thrive in these second tier events with super low scoring and the Wyndham seems to provide that.
Cam Davies is having a good year, a great come from behind win at the rocket mortgage and a pretty good 3M Open which should of been so much better is it weren’t for a very poor back 9 in R4 including a double at the last. A classic case of impatience with the putter seeping into the rest of his game in my opinion. If he can continue that form he has a great chance this week at a venue he likes, T7 last year T15 in 2020. If he can continue his form and keep his cool on the greens this could be a very good week for Cam and I fancy his chances.
For me Billy Horshel is the standout player in this event. His record here is impressive including 4th here last year and 2nd in 2020, he is having a great year that includes a win in the Coralas Puntecana in windy conditions and a T2 last time out in some of the toughest conditions they will play this year. Although he is a good 27th in the Fedex cup rankings, he will be thinking about boosting his chances to ensuring he is in the top30 for upcoming tour championship and a win here could almost guarantee him a spot.  

#PGATour 
#SedgefieldCountryClub
#WyndhamChampionship
#KurtKitayama
#AaronRai 
#CamDavies
Post Tournament Notes:
A very strange week that was ultimately successful. A wash out on Thursday caused mayhem with the tee times for the rest of the tournament which meant by the Sunday everyone had to play at least 36 some even more. This shaped up to be a real test of focus and stamina come the last 18 on Sunday with Max Greyersman seemingly running away with it, 4 clear stood on the 14th tee. However an 8 on 14 and a 4 putt double on 16 blew his chances and Aaron Rai lurking in the shadows came to fore with to win. Having backed Rai at the start of the week I watched him closely and wasn’t that confident of a win, he played well but a few miss putts he should of made and some other opportunities fell by the wayside. What he did do well was stay in the mix for second and by the time Max blew up Rai was there to pick up the pieces in near darkness up 18 making an incredible birdie. In terms of the course this is a relivetly short track with a big emphasis on accuracy from the tee, rough was extremely punishing this week costing a shot or more in most cases.
Eye-Catchers: Luke Clanton(A), Davis Thompson

Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes 258 Henrik Stenson (2017), JT Poston (2019) 18 holes 59 Brandt Snedeker (2018)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Donald Ross created this layout in 1926 and it was redesigned by Kris Spence in 2007. Sedgefield has hosted from 2008 onwards, returning to the schedule after a 32-year absence, with winning scores of 20 under par or better in seven of the last eight years. The greens are small and undulating, with plenty of run-off areas around them, so quality scrambling is required
The story of last year Lucas Glover won the first of two PGA Tour titles in a fabulous fortnight, defeating Byeong Hun An by two shots
Type of player suited by challenge There are five relatively difficult holes at Sedgefield – the par fours at 11, 14 and 18, and the two long par threes (seven and 12) – but the rest of the track is straightforward. The champion is likely to have spent four days regularly rolling in birdie putts
Key attribute Touch/putting

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Wyndham Championship
Course: Sedgefield Country Club
Yardage: 7131yards

Debating:
Kurt Kitayama
Aaron Rai
Cam Davies
Billy Horshel
Shane Lowry

Notes:
Tough conditions Thursday & Friday expecting lots of wind & rain however no electrical activity to threat playability, should make things interesting and those players good in the wind should be looked at closely.
Course sets up for accuracy off the tee and good approach play, previous winners include Lucas Glover (’23), Kevin Eisner (’21), Jim Herman (’20).
Kurt Kitayama displayed an impressive showing at the 3m Open last time out T6, The 66 he shot in the last round there was eye catching in tough conditions. Currently 74th in the FedEx standings he will need to have a good week here to make It in to St Jude next week (have to be in top70 to qualify). His last win at 2023 Arnold Palmer was in blustery conditions so he knows how to play in the wind.
I’ve defied Aaron Rai for a while now with doubt over his ability to close in on that elusive PGA Tour win, the closer he gets the more concerned I get not being on him. With an emphasis on accuracy combined with the tough conditions on day1&2 this is where I pull the trigger on him and really like this chances here. Ive seen a lot of him this year and he seems to thrive in these second tier events with super low scoring and the Wyndham seems to provide that.
Cam Davies is having a good year, a great come from behind win at the rocket mortgage and a pretty good 3M Open which should of been so much better is it weren’t for a very poor back 9 in R4 including a double at the last. A classic case of impatience with the putter seeping into the rest of his game in my opinion. If he can continue that form he has a great chance this week at a venue he likes, T7 last year T15 in 2020. If he can continue his form and keep his cool on the greens this could be a very good week for Cam and I fancy his chances.
For me Billy Horshel is the standout player in this event. His record here is impressive including 4th here last year and 2nd in 2020, he is having a great year that includes a win in the Coralas Puntecana in windy conditions and a T2 last time out in some of the toughest conditions they will play this year. Although he is a good 27th in the Fedex cup rankings, he will be thinking about boosting his chances to ensuring he is in the top30 for upcoming tour championship and a win here could almost guarantee him a spot.

#CamDavies
Post Tournament Notes:
A very strange week that was ultimately successful. A wash out on Thursday caused mayhem with the tee times for the rest of the tournament which meant by the Sunday everyone had to play at least 36 some even more. This shaped up to be a real test of focus and stamina come the last 18 on Sunday with Max Greyersman seemingly running away with it, 4 clear stood on the 14th tee. However an 8 on 14 and a 4 putt double on 16 blew his chances and Aaron Rai lurking in the shadows came to fore with to win. Having backed Rai at the start of the week I watched him closely and wasn’t that confident of a win, he played well but a few miss putts he should of made and some other opportunities fell by the wayside. What he did do well was stay in the mix for second and by the time Max blew up Rai was there to pick up the pieces in near darkness up 18 making an incredible birdie. In terms of the course this is a relivetly short track with a big emphasis on accuracy from the tee, rough was extremely punishing this week costing a shot or more in most cases.
Eye-Catchers: Luke Clanton(A), Davis Thompson

Course Details:

Course overview Golf was in the Olympics in 2016 for the first time since 1904 and a new course was built for the occasion in Rio, Brazil. That venue had a capacity for 2,500 seated fans and 17,500 standing, but the Tokyo Olympics four years later was spectator-free due to Covid. Galleries are back for Paris and Le Golf National is one of Europe's finest courses. It usually provides a stern test, staging the French Open from 2002 onwards, aside from 2020 and 2021. It also hosted the Ryder Cup in 2018. The fairways are severely undulating, so precise tee-shots are required. The rough is typically lush, and several water hazards are lurking to punish errant shots. The greens are large and pacy, with plenty of sharp slopes

Pre Tournament Notes:
Olympics
Tournament: Mens Olympics 
Course: Le Golf National
Yardage: 7174yards

Debating:
Rory Mcilroy
Cory Conners
Guido Migliozzi
Joaquin Niemann

Notes:
Rory knows this course as good than anyone else in the field. Ultimately it has been another disappointing year for him winning the odd tournament but still no majors! Despite his negative comments about golf in the Olympics in the past I think he does care about winning this and could turn his year around. After the horror show at the Open and the heartbreak at the US Open I expect he is after some retribution and I’m expecting him to go close this on a course he is familiar with.
Im looking for good Approach play and T2G attributes this week and currently ranked 5th APP & 12th T2G is Cory Conners. He’s in great form at the moment with T10 Scottish, T9 US Open and a 6th place finish in the Canadian Open. He also has the added dynamic of qualifying or being picked for the presidents Cup which is drawing ever closer. He wants a good showing in a star studded field representing Canada and I can see him having a good week here.
Purely down to the fact he won here back in 22 but has also shown good form around similar DPWT courses to Le Golf national I think Guido will relish the challenge of representing Italy here this week. The interactional course where he won the KLM Open a few weeks ago has a similar feel to this, lots of water and accuracy is key. He also played well despite a bad last round at the European Open at Green Eagle which is also similar to this. At a good price I fancy his EW chances.
Joaquin Niemann has shown his abilities the past few weeks and will be desperate to show his Country, Chile and everyone else that despite being on LIV he can still win in this company. Right in the mix at the Open before a disaster in R4, T2 LIV Golf UK shows he’s right in form for this. Once of the best ball strikers in the world however he needs to putt well to be in contention.
Post Tournament Notes:
A really good tournament that provided great entertainment for the neutral fan. The course provided plenty of opportunities for those willing to be aggressive as shown by Scottie Scheffler with a final round 62 to win from 6 back? Jon Rahm was 4 clear going into the back 9 and somehow didn’t even win a medal, Rory & Xander also fading away to insignificance showing the Olympics is no average tournament. Slightly disappointing display from my picks with Conners and Niemann both T9.
EyeCatchers - Nicolai Hojgaard, Thomas Detry

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: LIV Golf United Kingdom
Course: JCB Golf & Country Club
Yardage: 7.255 yards
Previous Winner: Cam Smith

Martin Kaymer
Dean Burmester
Bryson DeChambeau

Notes:
Dean Burmester is playing great golf this year and that was displayed in the Open last week in Troon. He would have been really in the mix come Sunday but scrambled a t 76 on Saturday in extremely tough conditions. A similar story in LIV Andalusia started great but a few bad breaks on Saturday led to 75 and his chances were realistically over by that point.  I think this course could setup better for him with better conditions than last week, more room off the tee for this long hitter and a good putting week could lead to a win.
Bryson Dechambeau has surprised me slightly in that he has not won a LIV event so far this year. His best result thus far is T3 at LIV Nashville a week after a historic US Open win. Looking at previous years he does seem to trend in the latter parts of the season, last year he picked up two LIV wins after the major championships had finished. After a really disappointing Open last week I think he will want to bounce back and show people that he still has the game to win and this course should suit him.

#LIV 
#LIVGolfUnitedKingdom
#JCBGolfandCountryClub
#DeanBurmester
Post Tournament Notes:
My fancies were never in contention this week but one of the more interesting LIV events in thes 2024 schedule. The top 10 ended up with some of the biggest names with perhaps the biggest on top, Jon Rahm. His long awaited first LIV win, but slightly unconvincing after missing a toddler on the last and relying on Hatton to miss a par putt to get into a playoff. That was my first look at the JCB in tournament play and the course was the star of the show this week, I hope to see more events and not just LIV utilise this course.
Eye Catchers: Joaquin Niemann, Cam Smith

Course Details:
Course TPC Twin Cities, Blaine, Minnesota
Length 7,431 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives, 11 par-fours, four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 260 Lee Hodges (2023) 18 holes 62 Scott Piercy, Bryson DeChambeau, Matthew Wolff, Lucas Glover (all 2019)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Twin Cities was designed by Arnold Palmer and opened in 2000. Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: LIV Golf United Kingdom
Course: JCB Golf & Country Club
Yardage: 7.255 yards
Previous Winner: Cam Smith

Martin Kaymer
Dean Burmester
Bryson DeChambeau

Notes:
Dean Burmester is playing great golf this year and that was displayed in the Open last week in Troon. He would have been really in the mix come Sunday but scrambled a t 76 on Saturday in extremely tough conditions. A similar story in LIV Andalusia started great but a few bad breaks on Saturday led to 75 and his chances were realistically over by that point.  I think this course could setup better for him with better conditions than last week, more room off the tee for this long hitter and a good putting week could lead to a win.
Bryson Dechambeau has surprised me slightly in that he has not won a LIV event so far this year. His best result thus far is T3 at LIV Nashville a week after a historic US Open win. Looking at previous years he does seem to trend in the latter parts of the season, last year he picked up two LIV wins after the major championships had finished. After a really disappointing Open last week I think he will want to bounce back and show people that he still has the game to win and this course should suit him.

Post Tournament Notes:
My fancies were never in contention this week but one of the more interesting LIV events in thes 2024 schedule. The top 10 ended up with some of the biggest names with perhaps the biggest on top, Jon Rahm. His long awaited first LIV win, but slightly unconvincing after missing a toddler on the last and relying on Hatton to miss a par putt to get into a playoff. That was my first look at the JCB in tournament play and the course was the star of the show this week, I hope to see more events and not just LIV utilise this course.

Course Details:
Course Royal Troon, Troon, South Ayrshire, Scotland
Length 7,385 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 272 Justin Leonard (1997 Open) 18 holes 63 Phil Mickelson, Henrik Stenson (2016 Open)
Playoff A three-hole playoff for all players tied for the lead after 72 holes, with playoff continuing as sudden-death if players remain tied after the three holes
Course type Links
Course overview Royal Troon is hosting the Open for a tenth time. The historic venue first staged the event in 1923, followed by 1950, 1962, 1973, 1982, 1989, 1997, 2004 and 2016. There was a streak of six consecutive American winners of the Open at Troon until Henrik Stenson stopped that sequence eight years ago. The first six holes run alongside the Firth of Clyde, before the track turns inland to hilly dunes. The par-three eighth is typically the shortest hole on the Open rota, nicknamed the Postage Stamp. The front nine is much easier than the back. The par-four 11th – The Railway – is notorious as one of the toughest holes on the Open rota. This flat, hard-running layout provides a severe ball-striking examination
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy is more important than distance at Troon, with some blind tee shots, bunkers everywhere, thick rough and gorse bushes. Tee-to-green solidity seems the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

#TheOpen 
#RoyalTroon 
#Major
Pre Tournament Notes:
Major
Tournament: The Open
Course: Royal Troon
Yardage: 7385
Previous Winner: Brain Harman


Debating:
Tyrrell Hatton
Louis Oosthuizen
Tom Kim
Zach Johnson
Xander Schauffele
Tony Finau


Notes:
One of the relatively tough regular Open venues, as with all of them much depends on the weather. The weather this week looks typically Scottish, rain each day with variant wind. 
Terrell Hatton has had a decent run in the majors this year, T9 at the masters, T63 after a poor R4 at a poor renewal of the PGA and perhaps most impressively he was T7 after R3 at the US Open before a poor R4 (On an incredibly fast and firm setup) to finish T26. He followed the US Open performance with his first win on LIV in Nashville, dominating that event. A 3rd place finish a shot back from the winner this past week at LIV Andelucia at a very tough Valderrama was good preparation and he could have gone on to win that without some silly mistakes. Prior to LIV he was always one to look at on the links having won the Alfred Dunhill Links twice with a few more top 5s thrown in at that event. His best finish at an Open was T5 at Troon in 2016 and I think with soft conditions and thick rough this could his best chance to bag his first Major. His game is at its peak right now and this straight hitter has been undervalued at 30s, great chance this week.
Tom Kim is another links specialist since 2021 3rd, T6 & T15 this year after a 7under R4 at the Scottish Open. He was T2 in the Open last year in wet and windy conditions (and a dodgy ankle). He is far from a favourite player of mine and started his year quite poorly, however he has turned him form around in recent weeks. His low ball flight and form on links does give him an edge on the links when the conditions get tough. 50s for a top10 finish is a great price for a proven links specialist.
Louis Oosthuizen had a difficult few years with injury from 22-23 season and was probably a part to play for him moving to LIV. Before that he was one of the most consistent performers in majors, 2021 T2 PGA, 3rd US Open & T3 Open Championship. Unbelievablly his only major was the 2010 Open at St Andrews but he’s T2nd/3rd since. He did miss the cut at Troon in 2016 but that can be blamed on bad break with the draw shooting 83 on Friday. Now that he is fully fit and healthy he has had a pretty good year. He won twice on the DPWT earlier this season which looked relatively comfortably. His form on LIV this year has been very good, he has had three 2nds and a recent 4th place finish at Valderrama last week. Barring in mind this will end up being his only Major in 2024 and the form he is on he will desperate for a good performance this week. At 100s this is a great price for arguably one of the best players in the world ranked 26th in Data Golf Rankings.
Zach Johnson is looking to get his game back to decent standard after a very disappointing Ryder Cup captaincy which obviously sets your form back. Having watched a lot of PGA Tour this year it hasn’t escaped me that he occasionally pops up in contention now and again. He’s just struggling to get those 4 good rounds strung together to have a really good finish. Out of the veterans at high prices playing this week his Open Championship record is outstanding, from 2011 -> 2018 he didn’t miss a cut: T16, T9, T6, T47, 1, T12 (Troon), T14, T17. He’s an outsider but with a bit of luck and 4 solid rounds he could sneak in bit of place money this week. Considering his price 750 for 12 places this is pretty good.
Xander Schauffele is the second best golfer in the world behind none other than Scottie. He’s played far too good to win just the one tournament this year albeit a major. His major record in the past two years is outstanding, never finishing outside of the top20. It was a concern that he was racking up these close finishes but not crossing the line until he won the PGA Championship earlier this year. I think that lifts weight from his shoulders and he can now go onto win more in quick fashion and he probably deserves more given the quality of golf he has been playing, but it doesn’t always turn out that way.

#Major 
#TheOpen
#RoyalTroon
#TyrrellHatton
#TomKim
#ZachJohnson
#LouisOosthuizen
#XanderSchauffele
Post Tournament Notes:
Troon produced one of the best Opens venues in the last few years, combined with the tough wind and sometimes rain it could really prove to be a beast with a few under being a very good score. Xander really proved that he is one of the best players in the world currently, dominating the back 9 (the tougher) to win fairly comfortably by 2 shots. 
Eyecatchers - Sam Burns, Russell Henley

Pre Tournament Notes:
Major
Tournament: The Open
Course: Royal Troon
Yardage: 7385
Previous Winner: Brain Harman


Debating:
Tyrrell Hatton
Louis Oosthuizen
Tom Kim
Zach Johnson
Xander Schauffele
Tony Finau


Notes:
One of the relatively tough regular Open venues, as with all of them much depends on the weather. The weather this week looks typically Scottish, rain each day with variant wind. 
Terrell Hatton has had a decent run in the majors this year, T9 at the masters, T63 after a poor R4 at a poor renewal of the PGA and perhaps most impressively he was T7 after R3 at the US Open before a poor R4 (On an incredibly fast and firm setup) to finish T26. He followed the US Open performance with his first win on LIV in Nashville, dominating that event. A 3rd place finish a shot back from the winner this past week at LIV Andelucia at a very tough Valderrama was good preparation and he could have gone on to win that without some silly mistakes. Prior to LIV he was always one to look at on the links having won the Alfred Dunhill Links twice with a few more top 5s thrown in at that event. His best finish at an Open was T5 at Troon in 2016 and I think with soft conditions and thick rough this could his best chance to bag his first Major. His game is at its peak right now and this straight hitter has been undervalued at 30s, great chance this week.
Tom Kim is another links specialist since 2021 3rd, T6 & T15 this year after a 7under R4 at the Scottish Open. He was T2 in the Open last year in wet and windy conditions (and a dodgy ankle). He is far from a favourite player of mine and started his year quite poorly, however he has turned him form around in recent weeks. His low ball flight and form on links does give him an edge on the links when the conditions get tough. 50s for a top10 finish is a great price for a proven links specialist.
Louis Oosthuizen had a difficult few years with injury from 22-23 season and was probably a part to play for him moving to LIV. Before that he was one of the most consistent performers in majors, 2021 T2 PGA, 3rd US Open & T3 Open Championship. Unbelievablly his only major was the 2010 Open at St Andrews but he’s T2nd/3rd since. He did miss the cut at Troon in 2016 but that can be blamed on bad break with the draw shooting 83 on Friday. Now that he is fully fit and healthy he has had a pretty good year. He won twice on the DPWT earlier this season which looked relatively comfortably. His form on LIV this year has been very good, he has had three 2nds and a recent 4th place finish at Valderrama last week. Barring in mind this will end up being his only Major in 2024 and the form he is on he will desperate for a good performance this week. At 100s this is a great price for arguably one of the best players in the world ranked 26th in Data Golf Rankings.
Zach Johnson is looking to get his game back to decent standard after a very disappointing Ryder Cup captaincy which obviously sets your form back. Having watched a lot of PGA Tour this year it hasn’t escaped me that he occasionally pops up in contention now and again. He’s just struggling to get those 4 good rounds strung together to have a really good finish. Out of the veterans at high prices playing this week his Open Championship record is outstanding, from 2011 -> 2018 he didn’t miss a cut: T16, T9, T6, T47, 1, T12 (Troon), T14, T17. He’s an outsider but with a bit of luck and 4 solid rounds he could sneak in bit of place money this week. Considering his price 750 for 12 places this is pretty good.
Xander Schauffele is the second best golfer in the world behind none other than Scottie. He’s played far too good to win just the one tournament this year albeit a major. His major record in the past two years is outstanding, never finishing outside of the top20. It was a concern that he was racking up these close finishes but not crossing the line until he won the PGA Championship earlier this year. I think that lifts weight from his shoulders and he can now go onto win more in quick fashion and he probably deserves more given the quality of golf he has been playing, but it doesn’t always turn out that way.

Course Details:
Course Old Greenwood, Tahoe Mountain Golf Club, Truckee, California
Length 7,480 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 156 The cut Top 65 and ties qualify for round three
Course records - 72 holes +50 Erik van Rooyen 18 holes +22 Kyle Reifers, Chad Campbell
Course winner taking part Chez Reavie
Format The Modified Stableford scoring system was introduced for the first time in 2012, allocating points based on the number of shots taken at each hole. Albatross = eight points; eagle = five; birdie = two; par = zero; bogey = minus one; double-bogey or worse = minus three
Course type Parkland
Course overview The Jack Nicklaus-designed Old Greenwood has hosted since 2020. It usually plays as a par 72, but the tenth hole is a par-four for the Tour professionals. Balls fly far at the altitude (almost 6,000 feet). The second shot is usually more difficult than the first – with water hazards and deep bunkers guarding the fast, severely undulating greens
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate approach shots are always essential on Jack Nicklaus-designed layouts but the greens at Old Greenwood are notoriously difficult and a sharp short-game may be even more important
Key attribute Touch

#PGATour 
#BarracudaChampionship 
#TahoeMountainGolfClub
Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Barracuda Championship
Course: Tahoe Mountain Club
Yardage: 7480
Previous Winner: Akshay Bhatia

Debating:
Neal Shipley
Nick Dunlap

Notes:
Neal Shipley has come from a new class of amateurs making a storm on the tour this year. A stellar amateur career that included loosing in the US amateur Open to Nick Dunlap last year, before brilliant performance as lowest amateur at this years Masters & US Open. Since making his tour debut at the Rocket Mortgage classic he has gone T20, CUT & T6 showing he is more than capable at pro level. Another low tier event this week where Neal could pick up his first win as a pro in quick fashion. 
Nick Dunlap has a similar profile, a brilliant amateur career US amateur champion last year and most famously winning a PGA tour event - The American Express as an amateur. After that amazing win he turned pro and since then he has had a mix bag of results, most notable results T11 Houston Open, T12 Memorial & T10 Rocket Mortgage Classic. He impressed me in the latter showing solid all round golf, so much so I backed him in the John Deere Classic where he was very disappointing. I’ll put this down to an off week especially on the greens which is quite uncharacteristic for him, however the greens there were slow bentgrass and this was his 4th tournament in a row so I will let him off. He is back in the same state as his first win and he could pick up a second PGA Tour win this week to propel him in the FedEx cup rankings.

#PGATour 
#NickDunlap 
#NealShipley
#BarracudaChampionship
#TahoeMountainClub
Post Tournament Notes:
Second tier PGA Tour event which it plays upto the billing. An interesting modified stableford scoring which made for interesting viewing. From the little I watched it did seem to me that Nick Dunlap was by far the best ball striker in the field with only his putter letting him down slightly until the last round where he came from T20ish post R3 to post a really good +49 score which no one else in the field could quite catch. Nick is heading to greatness one of the most talented rookies I have seen for a long time and really looking forward to backing him more in future.
EyeCatchers - Mac Meissner, Patrick Rodgers

Course overview Royal Troon is hosting the Open for a tenth time. The historic venue first staged the event in 1923, followed by 1950, 1962, 1973, 1982, 1989, 1997, 2004 and 2016. There was a streak of six consecutive American winners of the Open at Troon until Henrik Stenson stopped that sequence eight years ago. The first six holes run alongside the Firth of Clyde, before the track turns inland to hilly dunes. The par-three eighth is typically the shortest hole on the Open rota, nicknamed the Postage Stamp. The front nine is much easier than the back. The par-four 11th – The Railway – is notorious as one of the toughest holes on the Open rota. This flat, hard-running layout provides a severe ball-striking examination
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy is more important than distance at Troon, with some blind tee shots, bunkers everywhere, thick rough and gorse bushes. Tee-to-green solidity seems the key to success
Key attribute Accuracy

Yardage: 7385

Debating:
Tyrrell Hatton
Louis Oosthuizen
Tom Kim
Zach Johnson
Xander Schauffele
Tony Finau


Notes:
One of the relatively tough regular Open venues, as with all of them much depends on the weather. The weather this week looks typically Scottish, rain each day with variant wind. 
Terrell Hatton has had a decent run in the majors this year, T9 at the masters, T63 after a poor R4 at a poor renewal of the PGA and perhaps most impressively he was T7 after R3 at the US Open before a poor R4 (On an incredibly fast and firm setup) to finish T26. He followed the US Open performance with his first win on LIV in Nashville, dominating that event. A 3rd place finish a shot back from the winner this past week at LIV Andelucia at a very tough Valderrama was good preparation and he could have gone on to win that without some silly mistakes. Prior to LIV he was always one to look at on the links having won the Alfred Dunhill Links twice with a few more top 5s thrown in at that event. His best finish at an Open was T5 at Troon in 2016 and I think with soft conditions and thick rough this could his best chance to bag his first Major. His game is at its peak right now and this straight hitter has been undervalued at 30s, great chance this week.
Tom Kim is another links specialist since 2021 3rd, T6 & T15 this year after a 7under R4 at the Scottish Open. He was T2 in the Open last year in wet and windy conditions (and a dodgy ankle). He is far from a favourite player of mine and started his year quite poorly, however he has turned him form around in recent weeks. His low ball flight and form on links does give him an edge on the links when the conditions get tough. 50s for a top10 finish is a great price for a proven links specialist.
Louis Oosthuizen had a difficult few years with injury from 22-23 season and was probably a part to play for him moving to LIV. Before that he was one of the most consistent performers in majors, 2021 T2 PGA, 3rd US Open & T3 Open Championship. Unbelievablly his only major was the 2010 Open at St Andrews but he’s T2nd/3rd since. He did miss the cut at Troon in 2016 but that can be blamed on bad break with the draw shooting 83 on Friday. Now that he is fully fit and healthy he has had a pretty good year. He won twice on the DPWT earlier this season which looked relatively comfortably. His form on LIV this year has been very good, he has had three 2nds and a recent 4th place finish at Valderrama last week. Barring in mind this will end up being his only Major in 2024 and the form he is on he will desperate for a good performance this week. At 100s this is a great price for arguably one of the best players in the world ranked 26th in Data Golf Rankings.
Zach Johnson is looking to get his game back to decent standard after a very disappointing Ryder Cup captaincy which obviously sets your form back. Having watched a lot of PGA Tour this year it hasn’t escaped me that he occasionally pops up in contention now and again. He’s just struggling to get those 4 good rounds strung together to have a really good finish. Out of the veterans at high prices playing this week his Open Championship record is outstanding, from 2011 -> 2018 he didn’t miss a cut: T16, T9, T6, T47, 1, T12 (Troon), T14, T17. He’s an outsider but with a bit of luck and 4 solid rounds he could sneak in bit of place money this week. Considering his price 750 for 12 places this is pretty good.
Xander Schauffele is the second best golfer in the world behind none other than Scottie. He’s played far too good to win just the one tournament this year albeit a major. His major record in the past two years is outstanding, never finishing outside of the top20. It was a concern that he was racking up these close finishes but not crossing the line until he won the PGA Championship earlier this year. I think that lifts weight from his shoulders and he can now go onto win more in quick fashion and he probably deserves more given the quality of golf he has been playing, but it doesn’t always turn out that way.

Course Details:
Course Real Club Valderrama, Sotogrande, Cadiz Province, Spain
Length 7,010 yards
Par 71 – three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Field 54 (no cut)
Course records - 72 holes 265 Adrian Otaegui (2022 Andalucia Masters) 18 holes 62 Bernhard Langer (1994 Volvo Masters)
Course winners taking part Ian Poulter, Graeme McDowell, Sergio Garcia (three times), Talor Gooch
Course type Parkland
Course overview Valderrama has hosted numerous DP World Tour events – the Volvo Masters from 1988 to 1996 and from 2002 to 2008; the Ryder Cup in 1997; the WGC-AmEx Championship in 1999 and 2000; the Andalucia Masters in 2010, 2011, and from 2017 to 2022; the 2016 Spanish Open; and the inaugural LIV Golf Andalucia last year. Valderrama is one of the tightest tracks in Europe, with thin, tree-lined fairways flanked by heavy rough. Small, undulating greens
Story of last year Talor Gooch won his third LIV title of 2023, edging Bryson DeChambeau by a shot
Type of player suited to the challenge Accuracy from tee to green is essential at Valderrama, especially with so much wind on Friday
Key attribute Accuracy

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: LIV Golf Andelucia
Course: Real Club Valderrama
Yardgae: 7010

Debating:
Jon Rahm
David Puig

Notes: 
David Puig is arguably the best Spanish player in the world right now. He qualified for the Open back in February so he will be raring to go this week to prepare for Troon next week. In terms of LIV performances this year it has been a mixed bag, however I was impressed with his T3 in Houston. Last year at the LIV event in Valderrama he finished T5 which included a round of 74 so he knows how to get it around here.
Jon Rahm is too good not to win a LIV event soon, apart from a WD in Houston he has finished in the top 10 of all LIV events this year! Jon loves playing back in Spain winning multiple times in the past on the DPWT. He finished T2 here in 2019 and missed the cut in 2020. With three good rounds this week he can dominate round here and would be fitting for him to get his first LIV win in Spain.

Course records - 72 holes 262 Jim Herman (2019) 18 holes 61 Kelly Kraft (2019)

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: Isco Championship
Course: Keene Trace Golf Club (Champions Course)
Yardage: 7328

Debating:
Johannes Vermahn
Michael Thorbjornson

Notes:
Michael Thorbjornson is one of the star rookies to come on tour this year. Earning his card through the NCAA College league standings, similar to Ludvig Aberg. Beofre turning pro he had a great record at events he has received sponsors invites including 11th place at the Dubai Desert Classic earlier this year. I have kept a close eye on him the past few weeks since his tour debut as a pro and it has been a mixed bag which you’d expect, but a T2 at the John Deere Classic last week shows he is ready to compete at the top of the PGA tour. This lower grade event this week could be his chance to bag that first win and in my opinion 18s was a great price and he should probably be favourite for this event.

Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: BMW International Open
Course: Golfclub Munchen Eichenried
Yardage: 7347
Previous Winner: Thriston Lawrence 

Debating:
Calum hill
Joe Dean
Keita Nakajima
Rikuya Hoshino
Jannik De Bruyn

Notes:
Calum Hill has been on a decent bit of form lately. He missed the cut at the Italian Open last week but that can be forgiven with it being firm and fast and unlike the test player will face this week. He was T10 here last year with most notably strokes gained off the tee and putting. All signs that he could be closing in on a second DPWT win.
Keita Nakajima for me is the best player in terms of form this season in field. He tore apart the field in the Indian Open and should have done much better in the European Open but couldn’t hold a putt last time out. It will have been three weeks since that last event a well rested Nakajima could get this European stretch (already qualified for the open) off to a great start with a win here.
Another Japanese player who has good course form at this venue is Rikuya Hoshino. He like Nakajima has already qualified for the Open and has shown some good form. T29 in the Italian Open which was very fast and firm, with a T10 at the KLM Open. He was T3 here last year helped by a great putting perforamance making up nearly 2.5 strokes on the field. Also like nakajima he won earlier this year in Qatar and could pick up a second DPWT win this week.
Jannik De Bruyn has caught many peoples eyes since an impressive display at the European Open three weeks ago. He ended up T18 in that event but has a slight meltdown on the final day which you can understand with the pressures of being in contention in your home country in a big event. Since then he’s finished 3rd on the Challenge tour, T60 in KLM Open and a very solid T3 in the Italian Open. His game is versatile for any type of track and now we are back in Germany I fancy him to do well. 

#CalumHill
#keitaNakajima
#RikuyaHoshino
#JannikDeBruyn
#DPWorldTour 
#BMWInternationalOpen
#GolfClubMunchenEichenried
Post Tournament Notes: A really interesting week on the DPWT at a great venue. All four of my players had great chances going into the weekend but unfortunately only Hoshino prevailed with a good place (T6). Ewen Ferguson won by 2 shots 18 under par and was really impressed with his composure on Sunday, his swing held up fantastically well and he never looked in doubt at any stage. I was impressed with Mathew Southgate’s performance and he’s usually In his element on links, so the next fortnight to come he is worth keeping an eye on.

Course Details:
Course TPC Deere Run, Silvis, Illinois
Length 7,289 yards
Par 71 - three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 257 Michael Kim (2018) 18 holes 59 Paul Goydos (2010)
Course type Parkland
Course overview The John Deere Classic has been held at TPC Deere Run since 2000. The track, crafted by DA Weibring in 1999, is one of the easiest the Tour stars face all season. The first and second holes are extremely simple, so there is scope for a fast start, and this is a birdiefest where only the hottest of putters will contend
Story of last year Sepp Straka needed a birdie at his final hole for a 59, but found water with his approach. The resulting double-bogey meant a two-shot victory
Weather forecast A mixture of sunshine and cloud. Warm throughout, calm for round one, with moderate breezes from Friday onwards
Type of player suited to the challenge Steve Stricker, awesome with wedge in hand and one of the best putters in the world, won this event three times in a row before Zach Johnson, a player of a similar ilk, ended that streak. The John Deere lends itself to low scoring, but three breezy days should check scoring a little this time
Key attribute Touch

#PGATour 
#TPCDeereRun
#JohnDeereClassic
Pre Tournament Notes:
Tournament: John Deere Classic
Course: TPC Deere Run
Yardage:7289
Previous Winner: Sepp Straka

Debating:
Sungjae Im
Nick Dunlap

Notes:
An un-inspiring field this week at what is typically easy course so expect low scoring.
Sungjae Im has impressed me the past few months and is one a few stand-out players in this field. Apart from the majors he has not finished out of the top15 in all tournaments he’s played in, this includes a win in South Korea and four signature advents! I fancy him to bag a PGA Tour victory this week. 
Nick Dunlap went through a difficult patch after his incredible win as an amateur earlier this season and turning pro. He’s now finding his feet on the PGA tour. He caught my eye in the Memorial a few weeks ago with a very impressive T12 finish, and he was also in contention last week at the Rocket Mortgage Classic. I fancy him to do well in this field at a decent price.
Post Tournament Notes: Another un-inspiring week on a course that is far too easy for this field. TPC Deere Run was soft and putts just seemed to roll in from everywhere. Davis Thompson won by 4 shots shooting 28 under, he is definitely a player to watch in the future and I was slightly gutted to miss out on him considering his strong 2nd place finish the week before in the rocket mortgage. My main pick Sungjae Im looked really strong until a very average 70 on the Saturday blew away his chances and he lost so much ground on this shootout course it was tough for him to even get in the places. Michael Thorbjornson was T2 and definitely caught my eye which he had done even before this week. One to watch for the future.

Sungjae Im has impressed me the past few months and is one a few stand-out players in this field. Apart from the majors he has not finished out of the top15 in all tournaments he’s played in, this includes a win in South Korea and four signature advents! I fancy him to bag a PGA Tour victory this week. 
Nick Dunlap went through a difficult patch after his incredible win as an amateur earlier this season and turning pro. He’s now finding his feet on the PGA tour. He caught my eye in the Memorial a few weeks ago with a very impressive T12 finish, and he was also in contention last week at the Rocket Mortgage Classic. I fancy him to do well in this field at a decent price.
Post Tournament Notes: Another un-inspiring week on a course that is far too easy for this field. TPC Deere Run was soft and putts just seemed to roll in from everywhere. Davis Thompson won by 4 shots shooting 28 under, he is definitely a player to watch in the future and I was slightly gutted to miss out on him considering his strong 2nd place finish the week before in the rocket mortgage. My main pick Sungjae Im looked really strong until a very average 70 on the Saturday blew away his chances and he lost so much ground on this shootout course it was tough for him to even get in the places. Michael Thorbjornson was T2 and definitely caught my eye which he had done even before this week. One to watch for the future.

Course Details:
Course TPC Deere Run, Silvis, Illinois
Length 7,289 yards
Par 71 - three par-fives; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 257 Michael Kim (2018) 18 holes 59 Paul Goydos (2010)
Course type Parkland
Course overview The John Deere Classic has been held at TPC Deere Run since 2000. The track, crafted by DA Weibring in 1999, is one of the easiest the Tour stars face all season. The first and second holes are extremely simple, so there is scope for a fast start, and this is a birdiefest where only the hottest of putters will contend
Story of last year Sepp Straka needed a birdie at his final hole for a 59, but found water with his approach. The resulting double-bogey meant a two-shot victory
Weather forecast A mixture of sunshine and cloud. Warm throughout, calm for round one, with moderate breezes from Friday onwards
Type of player suited to the challenge Steve Stricker, awesome with wedge in hand and one of the best putters in the world, won this event three times in a row before Zach Johnson, a player of a similar ilk, ended that streak. The John Deere lends itself to low scoring, but three breezy days should check scoring a little this time
Key attribute Touch

Course Details:
Course Detroit Golf Club, Detroit, Michigan
Length 7,370 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives; ten par-fours; four par-threes
Highest-ranked players in field (world ranking in brackets) Tom Kim (16), Akshay Bhatia (29), Chris Kirk (33), Min Woo Lee (36), Stephan Jaeger (41)

Course overview The inaugural Rocket Mortgage Classic at the Donald Ross-designed Detroit GC took place in 2019 and it has been played every year since. Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Rocket Mortgage Classic
Previous Winner: Ricky Fowler
Course: Detroit Golf Club
Yardage: 7370


Debating:
Michael Thorbjornson
Ryan Fox
Taylor Pendrith
Akshay Bhatia

Notes:
Michael Thorbjornson looked good on his professional debut at the Travellers last week. A first round 73 scuppered his chances of getting close but a 64 R2 and 66 R3 showed this guy can compete in the big league. This will be another low scoring event and forgiving him last week as it’s his debut if he can string 4 good rounds together could get him right in the mix. This guy looks a real talent and should keep an eye on him in future.
Ryan Fox first performance since the Canadian Open where he finished T7. This course should suit his game and his form has been progressing nicely. Like TPC Craig Ranch (where Fox did not play) This course also is generous off the tee with not a big penalty for missing fairways. Like Pendrith Fox is trying to play his way into the presidents cup as a pick and a good performance here will help his cause.
Taylor Pendrith game should suit this course, a big hitter off the tee (29th in distance) who in the past 6 months is ranked 4th for putting. This course seems to be similar to TPC Craig Ranch where he won his first PGA Tour event, since then he has been consistently progressing. Like the Byron Nelson CJ Cup where scoring was very low (-23) expect the same this week especially if conditions are favourable. There is also the added incentive of looming President Cups picks which he is certainly not guaranteed, he is currently 12th in the rankings (top 6 are automatic) so a good performance will go along way for a presidents cup pick
Akshay Bhatia is on the cusp of another win. In the last 3 events he has been in contention come Sunday but just come up short. These are not just any events they were 2 signature events and a US Open. He should be at least second favourite in this but somehow fourth. He won the Texas Open earlier this year with a similar field and with the form he is currently on he can pick up his second win

#MichaelThorbjornson
#RyanFox 
#TaylorPendrith
#AkshayBhatia
Post Tournament Notes: Another fairly typical shootout tournament on the PGA Tour with a winning score of -18, which in the end was interesting as leading score was -17 after round 3. The higher scoring on the Sunday was due to the poor course conditions after rain on the poa greens making putting difficult aswell as some obvious nerves from the contenders. Cam Davies came through with the win after playing the best golf on the Sunday in the end, my man Akshay Bhatia 3 putted the last to miss out on a playoff which was a very disappointing finish. Cameron Young was the main threat I though on Sunday but a blow up including a driver shaft snap head off displayed why he hasn’t crossed the winning line yet, he is definitely someone I can oppose until I see an improvement in his demeana. Aaron Rai also looked very good throughout the entire week but just came up short with some uncharacteristic poor ball striking on Sunday.

Course Details:
Course Adriatic Golf Club Cervia, Milano Marittima, Ravenna, Italy
Length 6,965 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 267 Greg Turner (1993) 18 holes 64 Frank Nobilo, Stephen Bennett, Jose Coceres, Eduardo Romero, Seve Ballesteros, Greg Turner
Time difference Italy is one hour ahead of the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview The tournament moves to Adriatic GC this week, having spent three years at Marco Simone GC in Rome. Adriatic has not been used since Greg Turner won the 1993 Italian Open there. Much of the course runs through a pine forest, some is more exposed, while there are numerous water hazards waiting to punish the errant
Story of last year Adrian Meronk edged Romain Langasque by a shot at Marco Simone – the third of his four DP World Tour titles
Type of player suited to the challenge Rounds of 65, 70, 68 and 64 saw accurate Kiwi Greg Turner victorious in 1993, with equally tidy operators like Jose Coceres, David Gilford and Barry Lane just behind. Precision appears the key to Adriatic success
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
DPWT
Tournament: Italian Open
Course: Adriatic Golf Club Cervia
Previous Winner: Adrian Myronk
Yardage: 6965

Debating:
Alex Fitzpatrick
Joe Dean

Notes:
Alex Fitzpatrick has been playing lately and  progressing nicely this season. I think is due a win which will take his career to the next level. This would also be a good opportunity to earn a spot into the Open which he will be looking to get into after his good performance at Royal Liverpool last year
Joe Dean was unlucky last week loosing in a playoff last week. He’s been in the top 5 3 times this season and getting closer to that win.

#AlexFitzpatrick
#JoeDean
#DPWorldTour 
#ItalianOpen
Post Tournament Notes:
A really entertaining watch, far more so than the PGA tour this week. This was an interesting Italian Open where it hasn’t been staged at this course for something like 30 years? The course played fast and firm making it tough for scoring low. There wasn’t too much penalty areas off the tee as such but very easy to put yourself out of position and  if you end up on the wrong side of a hole and you could be facing real difficulties. Marcel Siem won this spectacularly having seemingly bottling it on the back 9 and then sinking a 25 footer on the last to secure a playoff with Tom McKibben (who finished about 3 hours prior!) and then sinking another great putt on the first playoff hole. This was a tough tournament to predict a winner and a contrasting course to what they have been playing on lately on the DPWT (soft and sometimes long). 

Eye catchers: Jannik De Bruyne, Sean Crocker

Course Details:
Course TPC River Highlands, Cromwell, Connecticut Length 6,835 yards Par 70 – Two par-five holes; 12 par-fours; four par-threes Course records - 72 holes 257 Keegan Bradley (2023) 18 holes 58 Jim Furyk (2016)

Akshay Bhatia looked very good until a disappointing last few holes took him out of contention.

Debating:
Joaquin Niemann

Notes:
Strange week for a LIV event right off the back of a US Open, not to mention the fact LIV Houston was the week before that. This is a big factor when questioning some of these players motivation for this event. I think many of those who played in the US Open last week could do without this. The best LIV player this season Joaquin Neimann did not play last week and I think he will want to make a point this week whilst the US Open players could be tired and weary. He was also right in contention after R2 in Houston and had a very uncharacteristic 77 in R3 which he will want to amend this week with a win.

Post Tournament Notes: Didn’t watch much of this event and not much interest with only on bet pre tournament. I thought it was hard to tell how the players who played the week previous in the US Open would get on and it was a mix bag. Tyrell Hatton more or less dominating the event winning by 6 shots. Sam Horsfield T2 and Westwood T3 seem to have found some form after some horrendous performances on LIV lately. So worth keeping an eye on in future.

Course Details:
Course Pinehurst Resort & Country Club (Course No. 2), Pinehurst, North Carolina
Length 7,548 yards
Par 70 – two par-fives; 12 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 271 Martin Kaymer (2014) 18 holes 65 Martin Kaymer (2014)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Pinehurst No. 2, designed by Donald Ross, is an iconic venue which has hosted three previous US Opens. Payne Stewart won the 1999 edition with a one-under-par total just four months before dying in a plane crash. Walton Heath qualifier Michael Campbell shocked the world by winning the 2005 US Open at level par in 2005, leaving Tiger Woods as runner-up. The last US Open at Pinehurst became a procession – Martin Kaymer won by eight shots in 2014 after going nine under par for 72 holes. The difficulty of Pinehurst No. 2 comes from the typically firm, fast-running terrain and the heavily domed greens which repel approaches. Hardpan sand and native scrub borders the fairways. The US Amateur Championship in 2008 and 2019 provides some extra course form for punters to study
Weather forecast Sunny, hot and calm throughout, with a small thunderstorm threat for Saturday afternoon. Temperatures ranging from 20C to 33C
Type of player suited to the challenge Accurate iron-play and distance management on approach is the key to success at Pinehurst No. 2, while razor-sharp scrambling is required for the inevitably missed greens
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
Major
Tournament: US Open
Course: Pinehurst no.2
Previous Winner: Wyndham Clark
Yardage: 7548

Debating:
Scottie Scheffler
Sahith Theegala
Viktor Hovland
Hideki Matsuyama
Sepp Straker

Notes:
Scottie is coming off a great win at the Memorial, strokes gained he is superior to anyone ranking 1st in T2G & APP whilst 2nd in OTT. His putting lately has been improving, 22nd in the memorial & 16th at the Charles Schwab. With his accuracy and short game this fast and furious course is set up perfectly for Scottie who I think is in a different league to anyone else and the market suggests that.
Sahith Theegala was an eye-catcher last week at the memorial finishing 12th, I think that was great prep for this week being it so firm and emphasis on accuracy and approach. Intrestingly Theegala only ranked 44th PUTT which is usually a strong part of his game ranking 19th PUTT overall so if he can improve on the greens this week he could have a big chance this week.
Viktor Hovland is showing signs he is back to his best. He was very disappointing on Saturday carding a 77 with an awful back 9 to blame for that. If you can forget that his PGA Championship performance.
Hideki’s US Open record is impressive with 6 top 20s in this event. The last year or so have been impacted by injury hence the smaller schedule and perhaps the higher price. He looked impressive last week at the Memorial with T8 finish. Stats wise 7th T2G and 10th ARG.. I think these as well as putting are what will be key at Pinehurst this week. Without injury and improvement in putting he could go close this week.
Ive backed Sepp quite a bit lately and with good reason, he has been on great form on the PGA tour and also to note is that he thrives on tough golf courses. Since ive watched him closely I do notice a tendency o have a 9/18 hole stretch where he struggles when the pressure is on. US Open record isn’t the best but has been improving a lot in Majors over the past year. I will leave him for this week at my own peril.

#Major 
#USOpen
#PinehurstNo2
#ScottieScheffler 
#SahithTheegala
#ViktorHovland
#HidekiMatsuyama
Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament Notes
Pinehurst played as a fairly typical US Open venue, very hard “turtle back” greens with only a few places to land if you are to keep it on the green. From the tee the fairways were relatively generous but if you strayed off these there were some penalising sand based native areas which you can be lucky from but sometimes not. This is a course where you can easily play yourself out of contention very quickly, the 13th hole on Saturday night saw Ludvig and Tony Fine rack up triples out of nowhere playing ping pong across the green. In the final round Rory (-5) missed two short putts on 16 & 18 both within a foot or two to bottle the championship, Bryson (-6) won with a brilliant sand save down the last to win by a shot.

Eye-catchers: Tony Finau, Russel Henley, Matthieu Pavon

PGA Tour
Tournament: The Memorial Tournament
Course: Muirfield Village
Previous winner: Victor Hovland (-7)
Length: 7569, par 72 

Debating:
Keegan Bradley
Lee Hodges
Viktor Hovland
Sepp Straka

Notes:
Par 72, 4 par5s, 4 par4s.
Keegan Bradley caught my eye at colonial, with a good T2 performance. He ranked 1st OTT, 8th T2G & 9th PUTT. His form has been outstanding of late and building up to a win. Before Colonial T18 at the PGA Championship and T21 at the Wells Fargo. He has a reasonable record at Muirfield and worth a punt at 50s 8 places.
Lee Hodges has caught my eye lately, an all round solid golfer. Some good form lately including T12 at colonial, T12 at the PGA.
Viktor Holland won this event last year and usually does well where he is defending a championship. Since his FedEx win last year he has switched coaches and as a result been playing very poor. He has gone back to previous coaches before quail hollow and is now on the up T24 at QH and went very close at the PGA Championship T3. He will be looking for a big week here and at 18s that’s not a bad price for such a good player on his day.
I have backed Sepp Straka a lot lately, he has a great all round game and seems to specialise in tough golf courses, T16 Augusta, T5 Hilton Head, T8 Quail Hollow and T5 Colonial. He’s on great form and at a 50s 8 places I think it’s a good price and could go close.
