Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament notes
Memorial was a great watch this weekend, the golf course is fantastic and ultimately the cream rose to the top. 
It seems silly now that I didn’t really consider Scottie at the start of the week considering his near misses here in previous years especially last year 3rd place finish whilst literally holing nothing. This was perfect prep for the US Open next week and has blown away any off course concerns. I don’t think he played his best here with some big mistakes especially on the Saturday round, but his temperament and bounce back ability is arguably the best since Tiger. The fact he won by a shot having to make a decent 10 footer on the last to hold off Morikawa was very encouraging to see his putting hold up under pressure.
Muirfield definitely suits accuracy off the tee with good approach play the rough and bunkers were punishing here and incredibly easy to rack up big scores here, for example Viktor Hovland.
Eye-Catchers: Sahith Theegala, Hideki Matsuyama, Colin Morikawa

Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament notes
Memorial was a great watch this weekend, the golf course is fantastic and ultimately the cream rose to the top. 
It seems silly now that I didn’t really consider Scottie at the start of the week considering his near misses here in previous years especially last year 3rd place finish whilst literally holing nothing. This was perfect prep for the US Open next week and has blown away any off course concerns. I don’t think he played his best here with some big mistakes especially on the Saturday round, but his temperament and bounce back ability is arguably the best since Tiger. Muirfield definitely suits accuracy off the tee with good approach play the rough and bunkers were punishing here and incredibly easy to rack up big scores here, for example Viktor Hovland.

Course Details:
Course Hamilton Golf & Country Club, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada
Length 7,084 yards
Par 70 – Two par-fives, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 258 Rory McIlroy (2019) 18 holes 61 Rory McIlroy (2019)
Time difference Hamilton is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview This will be the sixth occasion that the Hamilton Golf & Country Club has hosted the Canadian Open and the first time since Rory McIlroy destroyed the track five years ago. It is a short and tight layout that contains three long par-threes, with undulating fairways. New greens and bunker changes have been introduced since McIlroy's victory, which the designers hope will enable trickier pin placements this time. Some tees have been pushed back too.
Type of player suited to the challenge Length off the tee is not necessarily an advantage but solid tee-to-green play and good putting were required for a strong finish five years ago.
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: RBC Canadian Open
Course: Hamilton Golf & Country Club
Previous Winner: Nick Taylor
Length: 7084 par 70

Debating:
Rory Mcilroy
Gary Woodland
Ryan Fox
Tommy Fleetwood
Alex Noren

Notes:
Alex Noren has been playing some brilliant golf lately, T12 at the PGA Championship on a course which typically wouldn’t suit him. He caught my eye in the CJ Cup where he was T3. I think a shorter course with emphasis on strategy off the tee and short game. He is due a maiden win on the PGA tour. However 25s for a player who has not won on tour before is not good enough for me.
Tommy Fleetwood is due a maiden PGA Tour victory, he came close to winning this event last year but lost in a playoff. A relatively short course with small greens should suit him with his fantastic ball striking, but for someone who hasn’t won on the PGA tour yet I feel his price of 18s (2nd fav) is not something I’d be willing to take.
Rory is by far the best player in the field. Even not on his best game he should be winning this event, he won here at Hamilton back in 2019 by 7 shots. With quite a few other top players missing he has a fantastic chance here. 
Gary Woodland caught my eye at colonial last week, his health issues set him back earlier this year but results are steadily getting better. Colonial wouldn’t be the obvious choice of venue for the big hitter but he was right in the mix come Sunday until a +6 round. Sunday was by far the hardest conditions and I can forgive him for that. At a big price he could be an interesting pick.
Ryan Fox has been finding his feet since making the move to the PGA Tour, since a T4 at the Myrtle Beach Classic his form has been on the up. He was right in the mix at Colonial until a disappointing +4 in his final round which was by far the toughest conditions all week, like Woodland I can forgive them both and expect the good form to continue into this week. This course gives me some Wentworth vibes where he won on the DPWT last year, with his form and a course he should like he could go very close this week.

#GaryWoodland
Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament Notes Fantastic to see Bob Macintyre win this week, a very solid performance. Course was really well setup, not particularly long but a huge benefit to be in the fairway and in the right areas of the greens. Bunkers with surrounding fescue and the rough proved to be very penalising. A mix of areas in stokes gained towards the top, Bob ranked 1st in PUTT and 7th in ARG. Whereas Ben Griffin 2nd, was 1st in APP and 3rd T2G.

Eye-Catchers Tom Kim, Victor Perez

Course Details:
Course North Course, Green Eagle Golf Courses, Hamburg, Germany
Length 7,882 yards
Par 73 – Six par-fives, seven par-fours, five par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 274 Paul Casey (2019) 18 holes 64 Bernd Wiesberger (2019), Romain Wattel (2019), Kalle Samooja (2022)
Course type Parkland
Course overview This course is a monster, containing six par-fives, three of which appear in the final four holes. Water comes into play on virtually every hole, but the tees are moveable and it is unlikely to be played at its optimal length. A good 300 yards could be taken off the total distance. The greens are big put contain plenty of ups and downs so finding the correct section will be vital.
Story of last year Tom McKibbin kept his nerve with an inspired five-iron approach to 18 to claim a two-shot victory and his first European Tour success.
Type of player suited to the challenge The length of the course will encourage the big hitters, as will the wide fairways, but the omnipresence of water will also have to be accounted for.
Key attribute Power
Pre Tournament Notes:
DP World Tour
Tournament: European Open
Previous Winner: Tom McKibbin
Course: Green Eagle Golf Courses
Length: 7882 par 73

Debating:
Sam Bairstow
Tom McKibbin
Richard Mansall
Alejandro del rey
Maximillian Kiefer

Notes:
Long course that will suit big hitters, a par 73 with 5 par 5s 
Sam Bairstow has really caught my eye lately, he’s a relatively big hitter that should like this course. He is a rookie so not played here before. He has been playing extremely good golf lately and at the time of writing is 9th in the Soudal Open, this is after earlier in the week qualifying for the US open 10
Tom McKibbin didn’t play in Belgium Soudal Open this week, but like Sam Bairstow he also qualified for the US open this week. He is here this week as defending champion winning by 2 shots last year. 10
I fancied Richard Mansall last in the Soudal Open last week but a few just pipped him for me. He ended up having a great week T13. He was another who has qualified for the US Open at Walton Heath earlier last week. He has good results at the Green Eagle course with respectable T29 last year and a T3 here in 2022.  5
Maximillian Kiefer has had a mix bag of form this season but a T35 at the Soudal Open last week is not bad at all. He was T2 at this event last year and I’m sure he will be relishing his chances again at a home country course he has played at lots in his career. At a big price he is worth a e/w shot in my opinion.

Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament Notes The course surprised me slightly in the fact it wasn’t as much a power OTT type of course, although you can’t be a short hitter. This is mainly a ball strikers course, not many if all par5s are not reachable in 2. There is a lot of water on this course and there was a  real emphasis to keep it in play especially off the tee where not only could you be in the water but also some heavy rough.

Eye-Catchers Garrick Porteous, Julian Guerrier

Course Details:
Course Hamilton Golf & Country Club, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada
Length 7,084 yards
Par 70 – Two par-fives, 12 par-fours, four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 258 Rory McIlroy (2019) 18 holes 61 Rory McIlroy (2019)
Time difference Hamilton is five hours behind the UK and Ireland
Course type Parkland
Course overview This will be the sixth occasion that the Hamilton Golf & Country Club has hosted the Canadian Open and the first time since Rory McIlroy destroyed the track five years ago. It is a short and tight layout that contains three long par-threes, with undulating fairways. New greens and bunker changes have been introduced since McIlroy's victory, which the designers hope will enable trickier pin placements this time. Some tees have been pushed back too.
Type of player suited to the challenge Length off the tee is not necessarily an advantage but solid tee-to-green play and good putting were required for a strong finish five years ago.
Key attribute Accuracy
Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: RBC Canadian Open
Course: Hamilton Golf & Country Club
Previous Winner: Nick Taylor
Length: 7084 par 70

Debating:
Rory Mcilroy
Gary Woodland
Ryan Fox
Tommy Fleetwood
Alex Noren

Notes:
Alex Noren has been playing some brilliant golf lately, T12 at the PGA Championship on a course which typically wouldn’t suit him. He caught my eye in the CJ Cup where he was T3. I think a shorter course with emphasis on strategy off the tee and short game. He is due a maiden win on the PGA tour. However 25s for a player who has not won on tour before is not good enough for me.
Tommy Fleetwood is due a maiden PGA Tour victory, he came close to winning this event last year but lost in a playoff. A relatively short course with small greens should suit him with his fantastic ball striking, but for someone who hasn’t won on the PGA tour yet I feel his price of 18s (2nd fav) is not something I’d be willing to take.
Rory is by far the best player in the field. Even not on his best game he should be winning this event, he won here at Hamilton back in 2019 by 7 shots. With quite a few other top players missing he has a fantastic chance here. 
Gary Woodland caught my eye at colonial last week, his health issues set him back earlier this year but results are steadily getting better. Colonial wouldn’t be the obvious choice of venue for the big hitter but he was right in the mix come Sunday until a +6 round. Sunday was by far the hardest conditions and I can forgive him for that. At a big price he could be an interesting pick.
Ryan Fox has been finding his feet since making the move to the PGA Tour, since a T4 at the Myrtle Beach Classic his form has been on the up. He was right in the mix at Colonial until a disappointing +4 in his final round which was by far the toughest conditions all week, like Woodland I can forgive them both and expect the good form to continue into this week. This course gives me some Wentworth vibes where he won on the DPWT last year, with his form and a course he should like he could go very close this week.

#RBCCanadianOpen
#HamiltonGolfCountryClub
#RoryMcilroy 
#RyanFox
#GaryWoodland
Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament Notes Fantastic to see Bob Macintyre win this week, a very solid performance. Course was really well setup, not particularly long but a huge benefit to be in the fairway and in the right areas of the greens. Bunkers with surrounding fescue and the rough proved to be very penalising. A mix of areas in stokes gained towards the top, Bob ranked 1st in PUTT and 7th in ARG. Whereas Ben Griffin 2nd, was 1st in APP and 3rd T2G.

Eye-Catchers Tom Kim, Victor Perez

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Charles Schwab Classic
Course: Colonial Country Club
Yardage
Previous Winner: Emilliano Grillo

Debating:
Scottie Scheffler
Aaron Rai
Sepp Straker
Austin Eckroat
Harris English

Notes:
7289 yards, par 70.
Weather looks mixed, risk of thunderstorms Thursday PM and some potential downpours Friday, weekend looks good.
Course has had a redesign as well as relaid greens. There is a premium for driving accuracy but with firm greens expected a high ball flight also benefits.
Scottie Scheffler back at home in Dallas coming off a crazy last week at the PGA Championship after being arrested. He was 2 in 2022, T3 last year, he knows Colonial very well. By far the best player in the field, I’m taking 10/3 outright winner with a relatively small stake.
Aaron Rai caught my eye with his performance at the CJ Cup Byron Nelson two weeks ago (also played in Texas). T12 in this last year ranking 2nd in APP and 5th T2G, he is coming into this in better form this year fresh off a very respectable T39 (two 68s in R1&2) I like his chances this week.
Sepp Starker has been playing fantastic of late. He had a difficult PGA Championship last week missing the cut, although I was not convinced Valhalla was his sort of course. Looking at this best performances this year he plays best at tight intricate courses with tough greens such as Hilton Head and Augusta. I think he will want a fast start here to forget last weeks disappointment so I’ll have him e/w as well as first round leader as he has an AM tee time Thursday benefiting from better weather.
Austin Eckroat
Harris English

Yannik Paul also played US Open qualifying but didn’t play as well. Im willing to put a line through that and look back at his recent DP World Tour form which has been very good, he caught my eye in the ISPS Hands a few weeks ago.

Scottie Scheffler
Aaron Rai

Austin Eckroat

Notes:
7289 yards, par 70.
Weather looks mixed, risk of thunderstorms Thursday PM and some potential downpours Friday, weekend looks good.
Course has had a redesign as well as relaid greens. There is a premium for driving accuracy but with firm greens expected a high ball flight also benefits.
Scottie Scheffler back at home in Dallas coming off a crazy last week at the PGA Championship after being arrested. He was 2 in 2022, T3 last year, he knows Colonial very well. By far the best player in the field, I’m taking 10/3 outright winner with a relatively small stake.
Aaron Rai caught my eye with his performance at the CJ Cup Byron Nelson two weeks ago (also played in Texas). T12 in this last year ranking 2nd in APP and 5th T2G, he is coming into this in better form this year fresh off a very respectable T39 (two 68s in R1&2) I like his chances this week.
Sepp Starker has been playing fantastic of late. He had a difficult PGA Championship last week missing the cut, although I was not convinced Valhalla was his sort of course. Looking at this best performances this year he plays best at tight intricate courses with tough greens such as Hilton Head and Augusta. I think he will want a fast start here to forget last weeks disappointment so I’ll have him e/w as well as first round leader as he has an AM tee time Thursday benefiting from better weather.

Course Details:
Length 7,609 yards
Par 71 – three par-five holes; 11 par-fours; four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 268 Rory McIlroy (2014) 18 holes 63 Jose Maria Olazabal (2000)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Valhalla is a long track with bentgrass greens, designed by Jack Nicklaus and opened in 1986. It staged its first Major in 1996 as Mark Brooks won the US PGA there, then its second in 2000. That was the year Tiger Woods famously beat Bob May in a playoff to win a third consecutive Major title. The 2008 Ryder Cup – a 16.5-11.5 USA victory – was at Valhalla. The course then staged the 2014 US PGA – Rory McIlroy's fourth and last Major success. The layout is wrapped around Floyd's Fork River, which comes into play on several holes, and is known as a difficult but fair test. Most greens are elevated, with run-off areas around them. The front nine is more open and scoreable than the tighter back. The fairways changed from bentgrass to Zeon Zoysiagrass in 2021, creating firmer, faster conditions, and the rough between fairways and fairway bunkers was removed, encouraging balls into sand. The rough in wider spots is juicy and penal after an early spring aided growth
Type of player suited to the challenge The course changes since 2021 mean Valhalla puts more of a premium on accurate driving and controlled shotmaking. Precise iron-play is essential to elevated greens which easily repel approaches
Key attribute Accuracy

Debating:
Rory Mcilroy - Already backed with Wells Fargo x USPGA double
Scottie Scheffler
Dean Burmester
Tommy Fleetwood
Byeong Hun An
Bryson DeChambeau


Notes:
Third longest course this year - 7,609 yards, with the third smallest greens.
Should favour long, drawers who putt well
Weather has been wet in the area lately with rain expected in the lead up to Thursday and possibly Friday. No obvious draw bias to note atm.
Scottie Scheffler first time playing since his first the baby arrived. Should have enough time to get there and back into the swing of things. He is the best player In the world at the moment by quite a distance. https://datagolf.com/true-sg-query. The long setup with small greens should be perfect for the 2nd OTT & 1st T2G. Shapes the ball both ways so not worried about his natural fade
Rory coming into this very hot, having just destroyed the field in the Wells Fargo. His last major win was the USPGA when last at this venue in 2014. The long wet setup should suit and I was impressed with his putting at Quail Hollow which can be iffy at times. Could argue this is one of the best chances he has had to win a major in a long time.
Dean Burmester has caught my eye whilst watching LIV, won LIV Miami in March April, followed up with 3rd In Liv Adelaide. Driving distance ranks 11th in strokes gains, he’s 21st in total for the last 6 months. If he can have a good performance on and around the greens he should have a good chance of placing at a big price.
Tommy Fleetwood Strokes gained 13 overall, impressively he is 10th in ARG. He’s been consistently good in majors, T18 in the PGA last year, T5 US open, T10 Open & a very impressions T3 Masters this year. Looked good a quail hollow and if he can get the putter good this week (big if) he could win this. Not bothered about slightly less distance as he hits a draw and 21st TTG.
Very tempted to go with either Tommy Fleetwood, Max Homa and Bryson DeChambeau but I think I’ll leave it now ive missed out on the better prices at the start of the week. Instead Ive gone with Bryson DeChambeau first round leader at 35s ew 6 places which is a very good price in my opinion considering he was FRL at this years masters as well as PGA Championship last year.
Post Tournament Notes:
Post Tournament Notes for a major championship venue Valhalla was disappointing, it turned out to be very soft and beneficial to big hitter with no punishment for wayward drives. No emphasis on around the greens or putting. Xander ranked 40th ARG and 14th PUTT, his was impressive OTT 4th and T2G 2nd.
Eye-Catchers Bryson DeChambeau, Sahith Theegala

Pre Tournament Notes:
PGA Tour
Tournament: Wells Fargo Championship	
Course: Quail Hollow
Previous Winner: Wyndham Clark

Debating:
Ludvig Aberg 14/1 ew (1/5, 6 places) - PP (Withdrawn)
Wyndham Clark 18/1 ew (1/5, 6 places) - PP
Max Homa 20/1 ew (1/5, 10 places) - Coral
Jake Knapp 125/1

Notes:
Quail Hollow favours long hitters and good putters.
I backed Ludvig but has since WD.
Wyndham Clark is arguably the third best player in the world right now. Second to Scottie Scheffler twice this season and third to him last time out at Harbour Town. With Scottie & Ludvig not playing this week I think he is the best in the field. He won this event last year and has the perfect game for this track. A bit gimmicky but I thought his full swing episode was my favourite so far and shows how far his putting has come. Excited to see what he can do without Scottie in the field for the next few weeks potentially.

Course: Hidden Grace Golf Club (Formily: Genzon Golf Club)
Course Details:
Length 7,147 yards
Par 72 – four par-fives, ten par-fours, four par-threes
Course records - 72 holes 268 Mikko Korhonen (2019), Benjamin Hebert (2019) 18 holes 62 Alexander Levy (2014)
Course type Parkland
Course overview Hidden Grace, formerly known as Genzon GC, has hosted five DP World Tour events – the China Open in 2014 and 2019, and the Shenzhen International in 2015, 2016 and 2017. Hidden Grace also hosted the China Open in 2020 (as merely a China Tour event), 2021 (China Tour) and 2023 (Asian Tour). The course circles a large lake, which comes into play on many holes. The largely tree-lined track gets more open over the closing seven holes. Fairways are generous and the course is entirely comprised of paspalum grass
Story of last year Sarit Suwannarut won the China Open by six shots at Hidden Grace in an Asian Tour sanctioned event
Type of player suited to the challenge Welcoming soft fairways, four par-fives and windless skies make this layout vulnerable to attacking big-hitters
Key attribute Power
